proposals.title: Narrative Graph Alignment for Loose Media Adaptations via Cross-Modal Scene Embedding and Probabilistic Relaxation
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for aligning movies and books rely on strict, scene-by-scene correspondence, failing for the majority of real-world adaptations which are loose, thematic reinterpretations. This limits applications in AI-assisted creative analysis, content retrieval, and socially intelligent agents that understand narrative across media. A method to align narratives based on semantic and structural coherence, rather than direct visual-textual matches, is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling narratives as structured graphs of semantic events and their relations, a model can learn to align loose adaptations by optimizing for global narrative coherence, outperforming methods based on local feature similarity alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a cross-modal narrative graph for each source (movie, book) using scene/sentence embeddings from a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP-ViT, BLIP-2). Graph nodes represent narrative units, with edges encoding temporal, causal, or character-based relations inferred via language model prompting. Alignment will be formulated as a graph matching problem under relaxed constraints, solved using a neurally-guided probabilistic model (inspired by NOPA) that iteratively proposes and evaluates alignments based on multi-level similarity (object, action, sentiment) and global narrative flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new benchmark dataset of loosely aligned movie-book pairs with human-annotated narrative correspondences. It will yield a novel model for cross-modal narrative understanding that moves beyond direct retrieval, enabling applications in creative AI and media analysis. The method will demonstrate how pretrained multimodal transformers can be leveraged for high-level structural reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on video-text alignment, this explicitly relaxes the assumption of direct correspondence, focusing on narrative structure over local similarity. It uniquely combines graph-based narrative representation with neurally-guided probabilistic reasoning for cross-modal alignment.

proposals.title: Narrative-Asynchronous Multimodal Alignment: Learning Embeddings for Stories with Divergent Temporal Start Points
proposals.motivation: Current video-text alignment models (e.g., for book-movie retrieval or scene grounding) assume a shared narrative timeline, requiring stories to start at the same point. This fails for real-world adaptations where narratives diverge (e.g., films starting in medias res, prologues omitted). This limits the robustness of AI systems for understanding cultural content and building assistants that navigate multimedia libraries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling narrative asynchrony through a contrastive learning objective that is invariant to temporal offsets, we can learn a joint embedding space where semantically aligned book and movie segments are matched regardless of their absolute position in the narrative timeline.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream neural architecture processing text (book chapters) and visual-audio features (movie clips). Inspired by CLIP's contrastive framework, we will introduce a novel 'temporal-agnostic' alignment loss. Instead of aligning fixed temporal segments, we will use a dynamic programming module (akin to Dynamic Time Warping or a learned attention mechanism over a sliding window of text/video chunks) to find optimal soft alignments during training. This will be trained on existing datasets (e.g., MovieBook) augmented with synthetic narrative shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new benchmark for evaluating narrative-asynchronous multimodal alignment, 2) a model that robustly aligns book-movie pairs with non-linear correspondences, and 3) insights into 'narrative invariance' in multimodal embeddings, advancing towards more flexible media understanding systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on video-text alignment which assumes temporal synchrony or uses manually annotated clip-sentence pairs, our method explicitly learns to be invariant to narrative starting points, a form of temporal robustness not previously addressed in multimodal embedding learning.

proposals.title: Text-to-Scene Generation via Inverted Multimodal Alignment: Synthesizing Coherent Visual Worlds from Narrative Descriptions
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models excel at describing existing images or video clips, but the inverse task—generating coherent, detailed visual scenes directly from rich, open-ended textual narratives like book passages—remains a significant challenge. This capability is crucial for applications in creative AI, assistive technologies, and human-computer interaction, where generating a visual world from a textual description is a fundamental cognitive skill. Grounding this in the physical and object-centric world, as studied in Torralba's prior work, is essential for generating plausible and consistent scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the alignment direction of a pre-trained, multimodal embedding space (e.g., from CLIP or a panoptic segmentation model), we can train a generative model to synthesize detailed, object-aware visual scenes that are semantically faithful to complex, paragraph-length textual descriptions from books.
proposals.method: We will leverage a frozen, pre-trained text encoder from a strong vision-language alignment model (e.g., CLIP-ViT-L) to condition a diffusion-based image generator. The key innovation is an 'embedding modulation' network, inspired by recent panoptic segmentation work, which will explicitly condition the diffusion process on object-level embeddings extracted from the text via open-vocabulary detectors. We will train this system on a large-scale dataset pairing book excerpts (e.g., from Project Gutenberg) with corresponding visual scenes, potentially using synthetically generated data from existing 3D scene databases (like Hypersim or SUN RGB-D) annotated with descriptive captions to bootstrap training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for text-to-scene generation that explicitly reasons about objects and their spatial relationships, moving beyond single-object generation. It will demonstrate the utility of inverting alignment models for creative synthesis and provide a new benchmark for evaluating the coherence and physical plausibility of generated scenes from long-form text.
proposals.novelty: While text-to-image generation is well-studied, our approach uniquely inverts alignment models trained for perception/explanation and integrates explicit, neurally-guided object-level control from the text, ensuring generated scenes are not just visually plausible but are semantically decomposable and consistent with the narrative's object inventory.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Alignment from Paired Movie-Book Data: Learning Visual-Semantic Embeddings without Large-Scale Text Corpora
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models rely on massive, noisy web-scale image-text datasets, which are expensive to curate and can embed societal biases. Alignment methods often depend on these vast, unsupervised text corpora for semantic grounding. This research investigates a more constrained, data-efficient paradigm using the rich, structured, and naturally aligned multimodal signals in paired movie-book datasets, which offer dense temporal and semantic correspondence between visual scenes, dialogue, sound, and narrative text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal model trained primarily on paired movie-book data can learn a robust, aligned visual-semantic embedding space by leveraging the dense, multi-layered correspondences within this data, achieving competitive alignment performance on downstream tasks without dependence on large external text corpora.
proposals.method: We will leverage a dataset like MovieBook, aligning movie clips with book paragraphs. The core architecture will be a dual-encoder model with a video encoder (e.g., a 3D CNN or Transformer) and a text encoder (initialized from a pre-2026 language model like BERT). Crucially, we will employ embedding modulation techniques, inspired by Torralba's prior work, to adapt the text encoder's representations using features from the video and audio tracks. A contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) will align video-text pairs, while auxiliary losses will leverage object-centric representations from the visual stream and potentially align dialogue (sound) with character actions on screen, creating a multi-faceted alignment signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel pathway for data-efficient multimodal alignment, reducing reliance on web-scale data. It will provide new insights into how narrative structure and dense, temporally aligned multimodal signals can serve as a foundational grounding resource. The resulting model and analysis will be valuable for applications in assistive AI and video understanding where narrative coherence is key.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately avoiding large unsupervised text for semantic grounding, instead using the intrinsic structure of narrative multimedia as the primary alignment signal. This contrasts with the dominant paradigm of scaling up noisy web data and connects Torralba's expertise in visual objects, datasets, and embedding modulation to the challenge of data-efficient multimodal learning.

proposals.title: Narrative Alignment: Learning Object-Centric World Models from Descriptive Text and Internal Monologue
proposals.motivation: Current vision-and-language models are trained on web-scale image-text pairs, which are often shallowly aligned and lack the rich, causal, and compositional descriptions of the physical world found in narrative prose. Books provide a vast, untapped source of dense, multi-sensory descriptions of objects, their interactions, and the internal states of agents, offering a stronger signal for learning grounded, compositional representations. This is crucial for building AI that understands the physical and social reality behind visual scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: By training a multimodal model to align visual representations with dense, narrative text descriptions—particularly internal monologues and non-visual object attributes—we can learn more robust, object-centric world models that generalize better to reasoning about physical interactions and social contexts.
proposals.method: We will curate a large dataset of book passages paired with relevant visual scenes (e.g., from movies or synthetic engines). We will extend an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation model (like the one from Torralba's prior work) with a transformer-based text encoder fine-tuned on narrative text. A key component is an 'embedding modulation' layer, inspired by prior work, that conditions the visual feature extraction on contextual narrative embeddings, forcing the model to resolve object properties (e.g., 'heavy,' 'slippery,' 'sentimental') from text. We will use contrastive and masked token modeling losses between modulated visual object embeddings and the narrative text embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dataset and benchmark for narrative-visual alignment, 2) a model architecture that leverages dense text for improved object-centric representation learning, and 3) evidence that narrative context, especially internal monologue, provides a superior learning signal for physical and social reasoning compared to standard caption data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work aligns images with short captions, this proposal uniquely focuses on long-form narrative prose and internal monologue as a primary alignment target for visual features. It moves beyond describing 'what is where' to modeling 'why it matters and how it feels,' a step toward socially intelligent perception.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Narrative Alignment: A Scalable Framework for Book-to-TV Series Scene Correspondence
proposals.motivation: Existing cross-modal alignment research focuses on short video clips or movies, neglecting the complex, episodic structure of modern TV series. Aligning literary source material with long-form visual narratives is crucial for building intelligent systems that understand plot, character arcs, and semantic correspondence across media, with applications in assistive technologies, content retrieval, and enriched media experiences. This addresses a significant gap in modeling dense, long-horizon narrative structures.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, multi-granularity alignment framework, leveraging pre-trained vision-language models and temporal attention mechanisms, can robustly establish semantic correspondences between book chapters and TV series episodes by modeling both local scene-object dynamics and overarching narrative progression.
proposals.method: We will extend the book-movie alignment framework by first using a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to encode book sentences and densely sampled TV series frames into a joint embedding space. To handle the long-form nature of TV series, we will implement a hierarchical temporal transformer that aligns sequences at the scene, act, and episode levels, informed by narrative segmentation cues. We will introduce a novel 'probabilistic assistance' module, inspired by NOPA, to iteratively refine alignments using character and object co-occurrence graphs derived from the text and visual streams. The model will be trained and evaluated on a newly curated dataset of book-TV series pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first scalable method for aligning books with long-form TV narratives, a new benchmark dataset for the community, and insights into how neural models capture high-level narrative structure. It will demonstrate how object-centric and language-guided representations can be unified for complex temporal reasoning, advancing multimodal understanding beyond short clips.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond aligning short, contiguous sequences to modeling the hierarchical and often non-linear narrative correspondence in serialized content, integrating object-level grounding from Torralba's prior work with the neural multimodal reasoning suggested by recent language model analyses.

proposals.title: Visual Narrative Transformer: Long-Range Temporal Alignment for Embodied Task Completion
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for embodied agents struggle with long-horizon tasks, as they often fail to align visual observations with long-range narrative structure and temporal dependencies. This limits their ability to follow complex, multi-step instructions in real-world environments, a core challenge for building socially intelligent assistants. Grounding this in Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets, we address a critical gap in making AI systems understand and act within extended physical narratives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling long-range temporal dependencies and object state transitions within a multimodal transformer framework will significantly improve an agent's ability to align its actions with a narrative instruction over extended sequences.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained text-only transformer (e.g., GPT-2/3) with multimodal neurons for visual input, building on recent findings. The core innovation is a Hierarchical Temporal Memory (HTM) module that operates on top of standard self-attention. The HTM will use learned slot-based representations to track objects and their state changes over time, employing a recurrent gating mechanism to compress and propagate key narrative elements. This model will be trained on a new, large-scale dataset of long-horizon, instruction-following episodes in simulated home environments, combining visual streams with dense object annotations and language instructions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for long-range temporal alignment in embodied AI, a methodology for injecting persistent object-state memory into transformers, and a challenging new benchmark dataset for evaluating narrative understanding in physical tasks. It directly advances the goal of creating assistants that can understand and execute complex, multi-stage commands.
proposals.novelty: While existing work uses sequence models for short-term action prediction, our approach uniquely integrates a persistent, object-centric memory mechanism within the transformer to explicitly model narrative causality and state evolution over long horizons, moving beyond local token-to-token attention.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Alignment as Translation: Leveraging Statistical MT for Fine-Grained Vision-Language Grounding
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) learn coarse, sentence-level alignments between images and text, but lack fine-grained, compositional understanding of which image regions correspond to specific words or phrases. This limits their utility for detailed scene understanding and reasoning, which is critical for real-world applications like assistive robotics and open-vocabulary segmentation. Pre-2026, statistical machine translation (SMT) developed robust, unsupervised alignment models (like IBM Models 1-5 and FastAlign) for mapping words and phrases between languages, a structurally similar problem to mapping visual concepts to textual descriptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alignment models from statistical machine translation, when adapted to the cross-modal setting, can learn more interpretable and semantically precise mappings between image regions (or object features) and linguistic constituents than end-to-end neural similarity learning alone, improving fine-grained grounding without requiring dense region-text supervision.
proposals.method: We will treat an image as a 'source sentence' of visual tokens (e.g., patch features from a ViT backbone or object proposals from a detector) and its caption as a 'target sentence.' We adapt the Expectation-Maximization-based IBM Model 2 and the fast, discriminative HMM alignment model to learn a soft alignment matrix between visual and textual tokens. This alignment model will be integrated as a plug-in module on top of a frozen, pre-trained vision-language model (like CLIP or ALIGN). The alignment probabilities will be used to modulate the cross-modal attention in a transformer-based fusion network, guiding it to focus on relevant region-word pairs for tasks like open-vocabulary phrase grounding and referring expression comprehension.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel method for fine-grained vision-language alignment that is more interpretable and data-efficient than purely neural approaches. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating compositional grounding in existing datasets (e.g., RefCOCO, Flickr30k Entities). The integration of classical SMT techniques with modern vision-language models will provide a fresh perspective on cross-modal learning, potentially improving downstream performance in segmentation and robotic instruction following.
proposals.novelty: While neural cross-modal attention is standard, directly repurposing and rigorously adapting well-understood *statistical* translation alignment models for vision-language grounding is a novel, underexplored direction. This approach explicitly models the many-to-many, unordered alignment problem inherent in vision-language data, differing from the implicit learning in transformer-based multimodal models.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Learning-to-Rank: Aligning Book Narratives to Video with RankNet and Modulated Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Automatically finding textual descriptions for visual content is a core challenge in multimodal AI, with applications in assistive technologies and content indexing. Current methods for retrieving relevant book sentences for a video clip often rely on simple cosine similarity in a joint embedding space, which fails to model the complex, relative relevance of multiple candidate sentences. Adapting established Learning-to-Rank (LTR) techniques from information retrieval can provide a principled framework to learn these nuanced, contextual rankings from human annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting neural ranking models, specifically pairwise or listwise LTR objectives, to the cross-modal retrieval task will significantly outperform standard similarity-based retrieval by learning to order sentences by their descriptive relevance to a video segment.
proposals.method: We will build on pre-2026 cross-modal encoders (e.g., CLIP or custom video-text models) to generate initial embeddings for video clips and book sentences. Instead of using direct similarity, we will fine-tune the model using a ranking loss, such as RankNet or ListNet, on a dataset of video clips paired with ordered lists of relevant sentences. Inspired by Torralba's work on embedding modulation, we will explore a lightweight modulation network that conditions the text embedding on the video context before computing ranking scores, allowing for dynamic, context-aware re-ranking.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, ranking-optimized model for cross-modal sentence retrieval, demonstrated on a newly curated or adapted dataset of video-book alignments. It will provide a rigorous evaluation of LTR methods for multimodal tasks, moving beyond retrieval accuracy to ranking quality metrics like NDCG.
proposals.novelty: While LTR is mature in IR, its application to cross-modal video-text ranking is novel. The integration of embedding modulation for dynamic re-ranking within an LTR framework creates a hybrid approach distinct from standard two-tower retrieval models.

proposals.title: Structured Visual Narratives: Enforcing Scene Coherence with Conditional Random Fields for Embodied AI
proposals.motivation: Embodied AI agents and social home assistants must generate or interpret visual scenes that are not just collections of objects but coherent narratives grounded in physical reality. Current vision-language models, while powerful, often produce descriptions or scene graphs with physically implausible object relationships or inconsistent temporal states, limiting their reliability for real-world interaction. This gap highlights the need for structured constraints that enforce narrative and physical coherence in multimodal scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating structured prediction models, specifically Conditional Random Fields (CRFs), as a post-processing or integrated layer on top of pretrained vision-language embeddings will significantly improve the physical and narrative coherence of generated scene descriptions and panoptic segmentations for dynamic environments.
proposals.method: We will build upon Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation models and multimodal transformer embeddings (e.g., from CLIP). A CRF layer will be defined over the output space of object labels and their relationships, where the pairwise potentials are learned functions of the visual embeddings and geometric features (e.g., relative position, scale) from the image. For temporal coherence in video or assistance scenarios, we will extend to a chain-structured CRF, leveraging object tracks from datasets like Ego4D or Habitat. Inference will use efficient mean-field approximation integrated into a neural training loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for injecting structured, physically-grounded constraints into open-vocabulary vision systems, improving their robustness for embodied tasks. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating narrative and physical coherence in scene generation and a model that demonstrates improved performance in online assistance scenarios, as tested in simulated home environments.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are classic in low-level vision, their application to enforce high-level narrative and physical coherence on the output of modern, large-scale vision-language models is unexplored. The novelty lies in learning CRF potentials directly from multimodal embeddings to bridge the gap between statistical language priors and structured physical reality.

proposals.title: Visual Narrative Warping: Cross-Modal Alignment of Video and Text Using Dynamic Time Warping Priors
proposals.motivation: Aligning long-form video (e.g., film, instructional content) with descriptive text (e.g., screenplay, book) is a core challenge for multimodal understanding and retrieval. Pre-2026 methods often rely on expensive, fully-supervised training on paired data, which is scarce for diverse narrative content. This limits the ability of AI systems to understand the temporal correspondence between visual scenes and textual descriptions in an unsupervised or weakly-supervised manner.
proposals.hypothesis: That dynamic time warping (DTW) techniques, proven effective for unsupervised speech-text alignment, can provide a robust temporal prior for learning the correspondence between video and long-form narrative text, enabling accurate alignment without dense, frame-level supervision.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend neural DTW frameworks from audio (e.g., differentiable DTW, Soft-DTW) to the video-text domain. Video will be encoded using a pre-trained visual backbone (e.g., from Torralba's prior work on object-centric datasets), and text will be encoded with a language model. A transformer-based modulation network, inspired by embedding modulation techniques, will learn to project these embeddings into a joint space where a differentiable DTW loss can minimize the alignment path cost. We will leverage large-scale but weakly-aligned datasets (e.g., movie scripts with timestamps, instructional videos with subtitles) for training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, efficient method for unsupervised video-text temporal alignment, reducing reliance on expensive annotations. It will advance the field of multimodal learning by demonstrating the transfer of alignment techniques across sensory modalities. The resulting model can serve as a foundational tool for tasks like automated video summarization, rich video captioning, and content-based retrieval.
proposals.novelty: While DTW is established in audio, its application to aligning video with long-form, paragraph-level narrative text (rather than short captions) is unexplored. The key novelty is using a neurally-guided DTW framework, informed by recent advances in embedding modulation, to handle the significant modality gap and non-linear temporal structure between visual scenes and descriptive prose.

proposals.title: Unified Scene Narratives: Cross-Modal Summarization of Books and Movies via Object-Centric Embedding Fusion
proposals.motivation: Humans understand stories by integrating information across modalities, but AI systems struggle to create coherent, unified narrative explanations from disparate sources like text and video. This limits applications in education, assistive technologies, and content analysis. Pre-2026, multimodal fusion often treats modalities separately, lacking a mechanism for deep, entity-level narrative synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying multi-document summarization principles to cross-modal data, an object-centric embedding alignment framework can generate unified, coherent story explanations that fuse complementary narrative information from books and their film adaptations.
proposals.method: We will leverage Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets to build a pipeline. First, we extract object-centric spatio-temporal graphs from movie clips (using pre-2026 models like SlowFast) and entity-relation graphs from book text (using spaCy or similar). Inspired by 'Multimodal Neurons in Pretrained Text-Only Transformers,' we will align these graphs in a shared embedding space using a cross-modal attention mechanism akin to embedding modulation from open-vocabulary segmentation. A transformer-based fusion module, informed by neurally-guided probabilistic models (NOPA), will then perform abstractive summarization, generating a unified narrative.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel dataset and benchmark for cross-modal narrative summarization, pairing book excerpts with movie scenes. It will also introduce a new method for object-centric cross-modal alignment, advancing the field of socially intelligent assistants by enabling them to explain stories using multiple sources.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying multi-document summarization techniques to cross-modal (text-video) narrative fusion, specifically through an object-centric embedding alignment strategy, rather than treating video as a single 'document' or focusing solely on global features.

proposals.title: Panel-Bubble Alignment: A Multimodal Framework for Grounding Text in Comic Book Imagery
proposals.motivation: Comics are a rich, structured multimodal medium where visual narrative and text (dialogue, narration) are tightly coupled, yet existing vision-language models struggle with their unique spatial and semantic relationships. Grounding text in comic panels is a critical step for building assistive technologies for the visually impaired, enabling content-based retrieval, and advancing the understanding of visual storytelling. This problem offers a constrained, real-world testbed for multimodal alignment, distinct from natural images or video.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural alignment framework, leveraging pre-trained vision and language encoders with a cross-modal attention mechanism, can learn to spatially and semantically associate speech balloons and narrative text with their corresponding visual entities and actions within comic panels.
proposals.method: We will adapt the embedding modulation framework from open-vocabulary segmentation to this domain. Using a dataset like COMICS, we will employ a Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone for panel images and a text encoder (e.g., from CLIP or BERT) for dialogue/narration. A lightweight cross-modal transformer will process fused embeddings to predict spatial attention maps, localizing the visual region most relevant to a given text snippet. Training will use a contrastive loss between correct and incorrect text-panel pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel model and benchmark for comic panel-text alignment, advancing multimodal reasoning in structured graphical domains. It will contribute a new application of embedding modulation techniques and provide insights into how pretrained models handle stylized, non-photorealistic imagery and complex text-visual layouts.
proposals.novelty: While alignment has been studied for photos and video, applying it to the highly stylized, compositionally deliberate domain of comics, where text is graphically integrated via balloons, is novel. It uniquely combines Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets with recent methods in language-vision alignment for a new, impactful medium.

proposals.title: Open-World Video Narrator: Scaling Visual-Language Alignment to Unstructured Web Video with Embedding Modulation
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models are trained on curated, static image-text pairs, limiting their understanding of the dynamic, multi-event narratives found in real-world video. Scaling alignment to massive, unstructured web video, with its inherent noise and rich temporal structure, is crucial for building AI that can understand and interact with the physical world as depicted in online media. This addresses a core challenge in moving from object-centric static images to holistic, event-based video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and scaling embedding modulation techniques—successful for open-vocabulary segmentation in images—to the video domain, we can learn robust, fine-grained alignments between long, unstructured video clips and their corresponding narrative text descriptions, even with significant noise in the data.
proposals.method: We will extend the embedding modulation framework from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation to video by using a pretrained, frozen visual encoder (e.g., VideoMAE) and text encoder (e.g., from CLIP). A lightweight modulation network will be trained to adapt the visual embeddings based on textual context, operating on spatiotemporal features. We will leverage large-scale, noisy web video-text pairs (e.g., from YouTube-8M or a newly scraped corpus) and employ a contrastive learning objective with a noise-robust loss, potentially inspired by techniques from ALIGN or BASIC, to handle mismatched narrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable method for aligning web-scale video and text, a new benchmark for evaluating open-vocabulary video-text retrieval and narration grounding, and a model capable of fine-grained, queryable understanding of events and objects in diverse, in-the-wild video. It bridges Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets with recent multimodal and language-guided methods.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is applying the parameter-efficient, fine-grained control of embedding modulation—previously used for pixel-level image tasks—to the challenging domain of long-form, unstructured video-text alignment at scale, moving beyond curated datasets to real web data.

proposals.title: Video2Narrative: Cross-Modal Retrieval of Book Passages from Unconstrained Video Queries
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models excel at describing scenes but struggle with the inverse problem: finding a specific, rich textual narrative from a vast, unaligned corpus given a visual query. This capability is crucial for building intelligent assistants that can, for example, find a story passage matching a user's recorded experience or help visually-impaired users navigate literature. Pre-2026, methods are largely limited to short, aligned caption-image pairs, not the complex, long-form narrative retrieval required.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly embedding video clips and book passages into a shared, high-dimensional semantic space using a contrastive objective, a model can learn to retrieve the most semantically relevant narrative text from a large collection of books without any prior alignment between the specific videos and texts.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-encoder architecture. For video, we use a pre-trained SlowFast network to extract spatiotemporal features, followed by a transformer encoder to aggregate into a single video embedding. For text, we process book passages with a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT) and a similar transformer to produce a passage embedding. We train the model using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on a newly curated dataset of video clips paired with descriptive passages from public domain books and scripts, leveraging weak supervision from existing image-caption datasets and automatic scene description generation. Embedding modulation techniques, inspired by Torralba's prior work, will be explored to refine cross-modal alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel task and benchmark for long-form, cross-modal retrieval. It will advance methods for learning joint embeddings of dynamic visual scenes and complex narrative text. The resulting model and dataset will provide a foundation for next-generation retrieval systems in augmented reality, creative writing, and assistive technology.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond retrieving short captions or aligned video clips to addressing the open-ended challenge of finding relevant long-form narrative in an entirely unaligned corpus, a significant scaling of the cross-modal retrieval problem. The integration of dynamic video understanding with dense passage embedding for book-length corpora is largely unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Narrative Alignment: A Dual-Task Framework for Book-to-Movie Scene Grounding
proposals.motivation: Aligning narrative content across books and their film adaptations is a core challenge in multimodal AI, with applications in accessibility, content retrieval, and media studies. Current methods often rely on single-step alignment, which struggles with the significant modality gap between dense textual descriptions and dynamic visual sequences. A robust solution would enable fine-grained analysis of narrative translation and support next-generation assistive technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decomposing the book-to-movie alignment problem into two intermediate tasks—first aligning the movie script to the book text, then aligning the script to the video—will yield more accurate and interpretable scene correspondences than direct cross-modal matching.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-task transformer-based architecture. First, a fine-tuned text encoder (e.g., BERT) will perform semantic alignment between book paragraphs and movie script lines, using a contrastive loss. Second, this aligned script will serve as a strong prior for a vision-language model (inspired by CLIP or Flamingo) to temporally ground script sentences in video frames, building on Torralba's work in open-vocabulary segmentation and object grounding. The pipeline will be trained and evaluated on a new dataset pairing classic novels with their film adaptations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel two-stage framework for cross-modal narrative alignment that reduces the semantic gap, 2) a new benchmark dataset for book-script-video alignment, and 3) insights into how high-level narrative structure is preserved across modalities, extending research on multimodal neurons and semantic grounding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly using the movie script as a disentangled, structured intermediary, which is a non-obvious but natural pivot point that leverages stronger text-text and text-video relationships to solve the harder book-video problem.

proposals.title: Fine-Grained Object-Action-Attribute Alignment for Open-World Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) align entire image regions with sentence-level captions, which is insufficient for detailed scene understanding. This coarse alignment fails to ground specific objects, their actions, and attributes precisely, limiting applications in robotics, assistive AI, and detailed image retrieval. A shift to fine-grained, compositional alignment is needed to bridge the gap between high-level semantics and low-level visual perception.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly disentangling and aligning visual representations of objects, actions, and attributes with their corresponding linguistic constituents, we can significantly improve the model's ability to parse, reason about, and generate descriptions of complex real-world scenes.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture that builds upon a pretrained VLM (e.g., CLIP) and a panoptic segmentation network (e.g., from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation work). We introduce a 'constituent grounding' module that uses cross-attention between segmented object/part features and a parsed dependency tree of the caption, isolating tokens for objects, verbs, and modifiers. Inspired by multimodal neuron analysis, we will train a lightweight modulation network to refine embeddings, enforcing alignment between visual regions and specific linguistic constituents via a granular contrastive loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a model capable of detailed, compositional scene description and querying, moving beyond 'what is where' to 'what is doing what, and how'. It will produce a new benchmark for fine-grained vision-language grounding and release a large-scale dataset with dense, constituent-level annotations derived from existing datasets (e.g., Visual Genome, Ego4D) using automated parsing and refinement.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard sentence-image alignment or object tagging, our method explicitly models the tripartite structure of object-action-attribute relationships. It leverages neural interpretability insights to guide the disentanglement of multimodal representations, a novel synthesis of Torralba's visual grounding expertise with recent language-model probing techniques.

proposals.title: Beyond Saliency: Localizing Critical but Non-Discriminative Parts for Robust Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current visual recognition models rely heavily on highly discriminative local features, which can lead to brittleness and poor generalization when these features are absent or corrupted. In real-world applications, robust classification often depends on a broader set of contextual and supportive visual parts that are not the most salient individually but are collectively essential. This project aims to model these 'non-discriminative critical parts' to build more reliable and interpretable vision systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that image regions which are individually non-discriminative but collectively critical for classification can be systematically identified, and that leveraging these regions improves model robustness and interpretability compared to models focused solely on peak saliency.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-branch neural architecture built upon a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP). One branch performs standard classification. The second branch is trained via a novel contrastive loss to produce embeddings for image patches that are maximally informative for the class label while being minimally aligned with the primary discriminative saliency map (derived from Grad-CAM or attention). We will use a probabilistic graphical model to infer the set of critical non-discriminative parts, drawing inspiration from online assistance frameworks like NOPA. Evaluation will use panoptic segmentation datasets (e.g., ADE20K) and robustness benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method to localize and characterize non-discriminative critical parts in images, 2) new metrics and a benchmark for evaluating model reliance on holistic scene context versus sparse discriminative features, and 3) evidence that explicitly modeling these parts enhances robustness to occlusions and adversarial perturbations.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on finding the *most* discriminative regions, we explicitly seek and model the *least* discriminative yet *still critical* parts. This inverts the standard saliency paradigm and connects visual recognition to probabilistic, part-based reasoning for robustness.

proposals.title: Robust Visual Explanations: Quantifying the Stability of Class Activation Maps under Adversarial and Natural Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Class Activation Maps (CAMs) are widely used to explain CNN decisions, but their reliability under real-world noise or adversarial attacks is poorly understood. For vision systems in robotics or assistive devices to be trustworthy, their explanatory mechanisms must be robust to the imperfect inputs they encounter. This work directly addresses the fragility of visual explanations, a critical gap for deploying interpretable AI in physical environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard CAM variants are highly unstable under both adversarial perturbations and common natural corruptions, and that this instability can be mitigated by integrating perturbation-aware training or by developing a new explanation method grounded in physical scene consistency.
proposals.method: We will systematically evaluate CAM, Grad-CAM, and Score-CAM on ImageNet and ADE20K under two conditions: 1) adversarial attacks (PGD, FGSM) targeting both classification and segmentation outputs, and 2) natural perturbations (e.g., noise, blur, weather effects from benchmarks like ImageNet-C). We will quantify instability using region overlap metrics (IoU) and saliency shift measures. Building on Torralba's work on open-vocabulary segmentation and multimodal grounding, we will explore a defense by fine-tuning vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) with an auxiliary loss that enforces consistency between clean and perturbed image embeddings, regularizing the resulting attribution maps.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first benchmark for CAM robustness, a quantitative analysis of failure modes, and a novel training method to stabilize visual explanations. The outcomes will provide crucial insights for building more reliable and interpretable vision systems for robotics and human-in-the-loop applications.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial robustness of predictions is well-studied, the robustness of the explanatory maps themselves is a novel and critical line of inquiry. Our approach uniquely connects explanation stability to multimodal physical scene understanding, moving beyond purely diagnostic studies to propose a corrective solution.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Visual Grounding via Activation Map Synthesis in Embedding-Based Architectures
proposals.motivation: Modern vision models, especially for open-vocabulary tasks, increasingly replace the final class-specific fully-connected layer with a dynamic embedding space (e.g., CLIP-style models). This shift renders traditional class activation mapping (CAM) methods inapplicable, creating a critical gap in model interpretability and localization capability. Understanding 'where' a model looks for a novel, text-described concept is essential for building trustworthy, socially intelligent assistants that can interact with real-world objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantically meaningful activation maps for novel, open-vocabulary queries can be synthesized directly from the spatial feature representations of embedding-based architectures, without relying on class-specific parameters, by leveraging the alignment between visual features and language embeddings.
proposals.method: We will build upon embedding-modulated architectures (e.g., from open-vocabulary segmentation) and recent findings on multimodal neurons in transformers. The core method involves probing a frozen visual encoder's spatial feature maps. For a given text query, we will compute its embedding and measure its similarity to all spatial locations, synthesizing a dense similarity map. We will then investigate neural guidance mechanisms, inspired by work on socially intelligent agents, to refine these maps by attending to the most discriminative feature channels activated by the text concept, effectively performing a gradient-free 'activation decomposition'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational technique for generating visual explanations and localization signals in the increasingly prevalent class-agnostic, embedding-based vision models. It will directly enhance the interpretability and spatial reasoning of models used in home assistants and robotics, enabling them to better justify their decisions and focus on relevant objects.
proposals.novelty: While CAM variants exist for classification networks, our approach is novel in targeting architectures that fundamentally lack a final classification layer, synthesizing maps purely from the interaction between spatial visual features and a dynamic, open-ended language embedding space.

proposals.title: Explainable Multimodal Models: Visual Grounding for Captioning and VQA via Cross-Modal Activation Mapping
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models for tasks like image captioning and Visual Question Answering (VQA) have achieved high performance, but their decision-making processes remain opaque. This 'black-box' nature limits their trustworthiness and hinders debugging, especially for safety-critical applications. A technique to visually localize the image regions that influence specific words in a caption or answers in VQA is urgently needed for model interpretability and improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gradient-based class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) techniques, extended to cross-modal attention mechanisms, can reliably identify and visualize the spatial support in an image for generated linguistic tokens, thereby explaining model predictions.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend Grad-CAM to transformer-based vision-language architectures (e.g., BLIP, OFA). For a trained model, we will compute gradients of a target output token (e.g., a noun in a caption or the answer logit in VQA) with respect to the final convolutional feature map or, more innovatively, the visual tokens in a multimodal transformer's cross-attention layers. This will produce a 'Cross-Modal Activation Map' (CMAM) highlighting influential regions. We will validate the maps using existing segmentation datasets (e.g., Flickr30k Entities, VQA-HAT) and human studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, model-agnostic tool for explaining vision-language model outputs, increasing transparency. It will enable the creation of a new diagnostic dataset highlighting model failures (e.g., linguistic bias, spurious correlations) by analyzing incorrect predictions. The technique will also serve as a foundation for developing more grounded and faithful models.
proposals.novelty: While CAM is standard in classification, its application to generative and discriminative vision-language tasks is non-trivial and less explored. The novelty lies in formally adapting the method to cross-modal architectures and systematically evaluating its efficacy for explanation and model debugging across multiple benchmarks.

proposals.title: Instance-Aware Class Activation Mapping via Slot Attention and Embedding Modulation
proposals.motivation: Class Activation Mapping (CAM) and its successors are powerful for weakly-supervised object localization but fundamentally fail to separate multiple instances of the same class, producing a single, merged activation blob. This limits their utility for real-world visual understanding tasks like instance counting, detailed scene parsing, and robotic interaction, where distinguishing between individual objects is crucial. Progress in open-vocabulary segmentation and multimodal foundation models has created a need for better weakly-supervised instance-level reasoning without costly mask annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a slot attention mechanism with language-driven embedding modulation, a CAM-based network can learn to disentangle and localize individual object instances within a single class using only image-level labels.
proposals.method: We will build upon a vision transformer backbone with CLIP-style text-image alignment. The core innovation is an instance-slot attention module that processes spatially-pooled visual features, competing to explain different image regions. This module will be guided by an embedding modulation network, inspired by recent work, which conditions visual features on per-instance textual descriptions (e.g., 'the left chair', 'the large window'). A multiple-instance learning loss will encourage each slot to attend to a distinct, semantically coherent region corresponding to a unique instance, generating instance-specific CAMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel weakly-supervised method for generating instance-level localization maps from image tags alone, a significant step beyond standard CAM. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating multi-instance localization and provide insights into how slot-based reasoning can be integrated with multimodal foundation models for finer-grained visual understanding.
proposals.novelty: While slot attention has been used for object discovery, its combination with embedding modulation for language-conditioned, instance-aware CAM from weak supervision is novel. This approach moves beyond generating a single class heatmap to producing a set of instance-specific heatmaps within a unified, interpretable framework.

proposals.title: Boundary-Aware Refinement of Open-Vocabulary Localization Maps via Physics-Informed Edge Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary models generate coarse localization maps but struggle with precise, pixel-accurate boundaries, limiting their utility in robotics and scene understanding where physical interaction depends on exact object extents. This boundary ambiguity is a key gap between high-level semantic recognition and actionable, metric scene geometry. Sharpening these maps is essential for building assistants that can manipulate real-world objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating explicit, learned physical edge priors with diffusion-based refinement processes can effectively sharpen the soft boundaries of open-vocabulary localization maps, translating coarse semantic awareness into precise segmentation without task-specific retraining.
proposals.method: We will extend embedding modulation frameworks (e.g., from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation) by adding a boundary refinement module. This module uses a lightweight transformer to predict a physics-informed edge map from RGB and coarse logits, modeling occlusion and material boundaries. A conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM), conditioned on this edge map and the original CLIP embeddings, will then iteratively refine the segmentation mask, enforcing boundary adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a plug-in refinement model that upgrades any open-vocabulary localization system to produce precise segmentations, advancing towards metric scene understanding. We will contribute a new benchmark dataset with high-quality boundary annotations for real-world indoor scenes, evaluating both semantic and geometric accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of a neurally-predicted physical edge prior to guide a diffusion-based refinement process specifically for open-vocabulary segmentation, moving beyond post-processing CRFs or training on closed-vocabulary data.

proposals.title: The Emergence of Spatial Fidelity: A Cross-Layer Analysis of Localization in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Understanding how deep networks localize objects is crucial for building robust, interpretable, and safe vision systems, especially for embodied agents and robotics. While CNNs are known to transition from texture to shape across layers, a systematic, quantitative study of how precise pixel-level localization ability emerges and evolves across the full network depth is lacking. This gap limits our ability to design architectures or select features for tasks like open-vocabulary segmentation or robotic manipulation that require spatial precision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that localization capability, measured by pixel-level precision, does not monotonically increase with network depth but follows a specific trajectory, peaking in intermediate layers before semantic abstraction in later layers degrades spatial fidelity. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this trajectory is modulated by the visual and linguistic context provided during training.
proposals.method: We will analyze vision-language models like CLIP and ALIGN, building on Torralba's work with embedding modulation and multimodal neurons. Using a curated dataset of images with precise pixel-level annotations (e.g., from COCO, ADE20K), we will systematically probe each convolutional and transformer block. We will measure localization accuracy by upsampling feature maps and computing IoU against ground-truth masks for objects specified via text prompts, extending methods from open-vocabulary segmentation. Statistical analysis will correlate layer depth, receptive field size, and semantic category with localization performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a detailed map of localization ability across network layers, providing foundational knowledge for model interpretability. The findings will directly inform the design of more efficient architectures for segmentation and detection, suggesting optimal layers for feature extraction. It will also offer insights into the interplay between visual representation and language grounding in multimodal models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focusing on classification or activation visualization, this proposal provides a rigorous, quantitative, and layer-wise dissection of pixel-level localization as a core capability. It uniquely bridges the analysis of traditional CNNs with modern vision-language models, investigating how linguistic supervision shapes spatial representations.

proposals.title: Explicit Cross-Modal Attention for Physics-Aware Object Localization in Unconstrained Environments
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 vision models, especially for open-vocabulary and panoptic tasks, often treat object localization as a purely visual matching problem, ignoring rich physical and contextual priors. This limits robustness in cluttered, real-world scenes where objects can be occluded, deformed, or appear in unlikely configurations. Integrating non-visual signals, like the physical plausibility of object relations or co-occurrence statistics, could significantly improve grounding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicit attention mechanisms, inspired by those in text-only transformers, can effectively weight spatial visual features using conditioning signals derived from physical and commonsense priors, leading to more accurate and context-aware object localization.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language backbone (e.g., CLIP) with a novel 'Physics-Aware Attention' module. This module takes as input a sequence of visual region embeddings and a conditioning vector encoding physical/contextual priors (e.g., extracted from a language model or a knowledge graph of object affordances). Using a transformer decoder block, it performs cross-attention where the visual features are the keys/values and the conditioning signal is the query, producing spatially re-weighted feature maps. This will be integrated into an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation framework like MaskFormer for end-to-end training on datasets such as ADE20K and a newly curated 'PhysicalScene' dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel architecture for injecting non-visual, physics-grounded priors into dense prediction tasks via explicit attention, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating physics-aware localization in complex scenes, and 3) improved performance on open-vocabulary segmentation, particularly for rare or ambiguous objects where visual cues alone are insufficient.
proposals.novelty: While cross-attention is common in vision-language models, its use to explicitly modulate spatial features with *physics and real-world context* signals, rather than just textual labels, is novel. This bridges Torralba's work on visual context and objects with recent findings on multimodal neurons in language models.

proposals.title: Spatially-Consistent Open-World Perception: Integrating CRFs with Multimodal Embedding Modulation
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary models, such as those for segmentation, leverage powerful vision-language embeddings but often produce activation maps that are spatially noisy and lack coherence, especially for novel objects. This limits their utility in real-world robotics and scene understanding applications where spatial consistency is as critical as semantic recognition. Imposing structured, physics-inspired priors on these neural predictions remains an underexplored avenue for improving robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) as a post-processing or fine-tuning module on top of open-vocabulary embedding-based models will enforce spatial smoothness and coherence, significantly improving the accuracy and visual quality of panoptic segmentation outputs without sacrificing open-vocabulary flexibility.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Embedding Modulation framework from Torralba's prior work on open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation. A dense CRF, with pairwise potentials based on both pixel color and position, will be applied to the class affinity logits derived from the modulated visual-text embeddings. We will explore both a standalone post-processing CRF and an end-to-end trainable CRF-RNN module for joint optimization. The model will be evaluated on datasets like ADE20K and COCO, with specific metrics for boundary accuracy and region consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified model that combines the semantic richness of large-scale vision-language pretraining with the spatial rigor of probabilistic graphical models. It will provide a principled method to reduce spurious activations and sharpen boundaries in open-vocabulary segmentation, advancing towards more reliable perception for assistive agents and embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are classic in closed-set segmentation, their integration with modern open-vocabulary, embedding-modulated networks to enforce spatial consistency on novel categories is novel. This bridges a gap between structured probabilistic models and flexible neural feature spaces.

proposals.title: GradCAM-Guided Perturbation for Open-Vocabulary Object Localization Refinement
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary models enable detection of novel objects, but their localization, especially for panoptic segmentation, can be imprecise due to reliance on coarse text-image alignment. Pre-2026, interpretability methods like GradCAM and perturbation analysis are used post-hoc to explain model decisions, but not actively to correct them. This gap limits the real-world utility of these systems in robotics and assistive agents where precise spatial understanding is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gradient-based attribution maps from a vision-language model can be used to generate targeted spatial perturbations, which, when applied iteratively, will refine the initial coarse localization masks from an open-vocabulary segmenter towards more physically plausible and accurate object boundaries.
proposals.method: We will build upon an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation backbone (e.g., from the proposer's prior work). For each predicted object region, we will compute GradCAM-style attributions from a frozen VL model (like CLIP) using the object's text label. These heatmaps will guide a novel perturbation module that selectively masks or alters image features in areas of high attribution uncertainty. This perturbed input will be re-processed by the segmenter, with the process repeated in a lightweight, online optimization loop. We will integrate a sound or physical consistency loss (inspired by NOPA) to penalize segmentations violating basic object continuity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new training-free, inference-time refinement module that boosts the precision of open-vocabulary localization. It will create a novel bridge between model interpretability and model correction. The resulting method will be validated on complex, real-world datasets (e.g., ADE20K, Open Images), demonstrating improved mIoU for novel categories.
proposals.novelty: While interpretability methods are typically diagnostic, we repurpose them as a corrective mechanism for localization. The novelty lies in the closed-loop, attribution-guided perturbation strategy specifically designed for open-vocabulary settings, moving beyond post-hoc explanation to active performance enhancement.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Class Activation Mapping for Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Class Activation Mapping (CAM) has proven crucial for interpreting discriminative spatial regions in static images, but video understanding requires localizing key spatiotemporal patterns. Pre-2026, methods for explaining video model decisions are limited, hindering progress in action recognition, video captioning, and embodied AI. A robust spatiotemporal localization technique is needed to improve model transparency, enable dataset debugging, and facilitate learning from video with weak supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending CAM principles to video by aggregating gradients or activations across a temporal dimension within a standard 3D CNN or video transformer backbone can reliably identify discriminative spatial regions *and* their critical temporal intervals, without requiring temporal annotations for training.
proposals.method: We will adapt Grad-CAM and its variants to operate on 3D convolutional features from models like I3D or SlowFast networks. The core technical challenge is designing a temporal pooling strategy (e.g., adaptive temporal pooling or attention-weighted aggregation) to combine frame-level saliency into coherent spatiotemporal tubes. We will also experiment with transformer-based video models (e.g., TimeSformer), leveraging their inherent attention maps across space and time to generate explanations, inspired by Torralba's work on multimodal neurons in text transformers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first generalized framework for spatiotemporal CAM, generating visual explanations as 'attentional tubes' in video. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for video datasets (e.g., Kinetics, Something-Something) and enable weakly-supervised learning for temporal localization tasks. The code and benchmark will be released to advance interpretable video AI.
proposals.novelty: While 2D CAM is well-established, a principled extension to the spatiotemporal domain for modern video architectures is lacking. The novelty lies in formally defining and evaluating temporal localization via explanation methods, bridging Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets with the temporal dynamics of physical events.

proposals.title: EfficientCAM: Lightweight Class Activation Mapping for Real-Time Visual Object Localization on Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: Real-time visual object localization is crucial for mobile robotics and augmented reality, but high-resolution Class Activation Maps (CAMs) from models like CNNs are computationally expensive. Deploying such models on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge, limiting their practical impact. A lightweight, efficient CAM variant is needed to enable responsive, on-device scene understanding without relying on cloud computation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying neural architectural search (NAS) and knowledge distillation to a pre-trained, open-vocabulary visual backbone, we can generate a significantly smaller network that produces spatially-precise CAMs with minimal latency and accuracy loss for real-time localization.
proposals.method: We will develop EfficientCAM by first using a hardware-aware NAS framework (e.g., ProxylessNAS) to search for optimal micro-architectures within a mobile-friendly design space (e.g., inverted residual blocks). The search will be guided by a multi-objective loss balancing localization accuracy (trained on panoptic datasets like COCO) and on-device latency. The final model will be trained via distillation from a large, frozen teacher model (e.g., a CLIP-based vision transformer) using both response-based and feature-based distillation to preserve its open-vocabulary object grounding capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly available lightweight network architecture specifically optimized for efficient CAM generation, 2) a systematic methodology for distilling open-vocabulary localization knowledge into compact models, and 3) benchmark results demonstrating real-time performance on embedded hardware, advancing the field of on-device computer vision.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been used for classification, its application to optimize the specific task of efficient, high-resolution activation map generation for open-vocabulary localization is novel. The integration of distillation from a multimodal, text-aligned teacher to preserve semantic richness in a tiny model is a key non-obvious advancement.

proposals.title: InverseCAM: Visual Synthesis via Targeted Activation Map Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Class Activation Maps (CAMs) are widely used for weakly-supervised localization and model interpretation, but they are primarily diagnostic tools. The inverse problem—synthesizing or editing images from a desired CAM—remains largely unexplored. Solving this would enable direct, interpretable control over image generation, bridging the gap between discriminative model explanations and generative model control, with applications in dataset augmentation, interactive editing, and testing model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the mapping from image to activation map, we can generate or modify realistic images through targeted, user-specified manipulations of spatial class evidence, and these synthesized images will be correctly classified by the original network.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pretrained, fixed visual backbone (e.g., a CLIP image encoder or a ResNet) and its associated CAM generation mechanism. We will train a conditional generative model (specifically, a diffusion model or a GAN inversion framework) to take a target CAM and a noise vector as input and output a corresponding image. The training objective will combine adversarial loss, a perceptual loss from the fixed backbone, and a novel 'CAM reconstruction loss' that ensures the generated image, when passed through the fixed network, reproduces the target activation map.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for controllable image synthesis grounded in the interpretable intermediate representations of discriminative models. It will produce a novel benchmark for evaluating the invertibility of visual explanations and release a tool for generating counterfactual images to probe and augment vision models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses text or segmentation maps for control, direct synthesis from class-specific spatial evidence maps (CAMs) is novel. The approach uniquely leverages a frozen, interpretable pathway from a discriminative model as the conditioning signal for a generative model, creating a closed-loop between explanation and synthesis.

proposals.title: Dual-Stream Visual Recognition: Improving Classification via Self-Supervised Spatial Guidance
proposals.motivation: Current deep classifiers often rely on spurious correlations and background features, limiting their robustness and interpretability. While weakly-supervised localization methods like CAM can highlight discriminative regions, they are typically a passive byproduct of classification, not actively used to improve the primary task. A tighter, synergistic integration of localization and classification is needed to build more reliable and physically-grounded vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: Actively using discovered discriminative regions to guide or regularize the classification training loop will force the model to learn more robust and semantically meaningful features, leading to improved generalization and interpretability without additional supervision.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-stream architecture where a primary classification network is trained concurrently with an auxiliary region proposal module (e.g., a lightweight transformer or a spatial attention network). The proposal module generates spatial attention masks from intermediate features. These masks are used in two ways: 1) to modulate feature maps via gated multiplication, focusing the classifier on salient regions, and 2) to compute a consistency regularization loss, penalizing the classifier if its predictions are confident on features from non-salient, masked-out areas. We will train end-to-end on large-scale datasets like OpenImages or ADE20K.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework that explicitly enforces spatial grounding in standard classifiers, improving their robustness to background shifts and adversarial patches. It will also provide a method to generate more accurate and stable visual explanations as an inherent part of the model, advancing interpretable AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses localization as a passive diagnostic or a separate task, we actively and dynamically use discovered regions as a *training signal* to regularize the classifier itself. This creates a closed-loop, self-improving system where localization guides classification and vice-versa.

proposals.title: Disrupting Narrative Flow: Training Vision-Language Models on Scenegraph-Shuffled Data for Robust Object and Relation Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) are trained on web-scale image-text pairs, where text captions often follow a coherent narrative or descriptive flow. This may bias models to rely on spurious sequential linguistic cues rather than grounding each visual concept independently. Understanding the impact of this sequential bias is crucial for building robust, interpretable models for real-world visual reasoning and human-assistive robotics, where descriptions can be non-sequential or fragmented.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly training a VLM on non-narrative, semantically discontinuous text paired with images will force the model to develop stronger, more disentangled visual grounding for individual objects and their relations, improving performance on compositional and open-vocabulary reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel dataset by taking existing panoptic segmentation datasets (e.g., ADE20K, COCO) and their detailed scene-graph annotations. For each image, we will generate 'scrambled' captions by randomly ordering sentences describing individual objects and their relations, breaking natural narrative continuity. We will train an embedding-modulated architecture, similar to Torralba's prior work on open-vocabulary segmentation, where a text encoder processes these discontinuous captions and a visual encoder learns to associate textual embeddings with specific image regions, irrespective of sentence order. We will compare this against a baseline model trained on coherent narrative captions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for diagnosing and mitigating sequential linguistic bias in VLMs, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating compositional visual reasoning under disrupted language, and 3) insights into building more robust models for home assistants that must parse non-sequential, imperative, or fragmented human instructions.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and hard example mining are common, systematically disrupting high-level semantic continuity in training text—rather than just word order or noise—to probe and improve visual grounding is a novel direction. It directly connects the study of 'multimodal neurons' in language models to the challenge of learning robust vision-language representations.

proposals.title: Visual and Acoustic Context Embedding for Open-Vocabulary Object Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary models primarily use text as the conditioning signal for segmentation and reconstruction. However, real-world context is multimodal; an object's identity and properties are often defined by its visual surroundings and associated sounds, not just a textual label. Leveraging these non-linguistic signals could enable more robust and intuitive perception for agents operating in unstructured environments, such as home assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conditioning a generative reconstruction model on dense embeddings from visual scenes or ambient audio, rather than text prompts, will yield more accurate and physically plausible object reconstructions, especially for categories with weak or ambiguous textual descriptions.
proposals.method: We will extend embedding modulation frameworks from open-vocabulary segmentation (e.g., OV-Panoptic) to a 3D reconstruction task. A frozen, pretrained multimodal encoder (like CLIP or ImageBind) will extract context embeddings from a reference image or a short audio clip. These embeddings will modulate the features of a 3D neural field (e.g., a NeRF variant) via cross-attention layers. The model will be trained on large-scale, multimodal datasets (e.g., SoundSpaces, Hypersim) to reconstruct object geometry from sparse views, using the non-textual context signal as the primary conditioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for context-aware 3D understanding that moves beyond language prompts. It will contribute a model and benchmark for evaluating reconstruction quality under non-linguistic conditioning, with direct applications in robotics and augmented reality where environmental cues are paramount.
proposals.novelty: While embedding modulation exists for segmentation, its application to 3D reconstruction conditioned purely on visual or acoustic context—without textual intermediation—is unexplored. This shifts the focus from language-aligned concepts to physics-aligned, sensorimotor representations.

proposals.title: Context-to-Gap Generation: Inverting Encoder-Decoder Roles for Visual Narrative Completion
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models excel at generating captions for images or answering questions, but they struggle with the inverse task: generating a coherent visual or textual description of a missing event given its surrounding context. This capability is crucial for building AI assistants that can reason about gaps in narratives, predict occluded objects in scenes, or provide plausible explanations for missing steps in a process, directly impacting areas like robotics and video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the standard encoder-decoder paradigm—encoding the surrounding context sentences and decoding to predict a missing target sentence—we can train models to perform robust 'gap-filling' in multimodal narratives, leading to richer, more context-aware representations of visual scenes and stories.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., a ViT-L/14 CLIP image encoder paired with a transformer decoder). For training, we will curate datasets (building on Torralba's work with datasets like Places) containing sequential visual scenes or descriptive paragraphs. We will mask a middle frame or sentence, encode the surrounding context (both images and text) into a fused representation, and task the decoder with generating the missing target description. We will experiment with embedding modulation techniques (inspired by Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation) to condition the generation on specific object-level features extracted from the context.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training objective for structured narrative completion, advancing beyond standard next-sentence prediction. It will yield a model capable of generating coherent, context-grounded descriptions of missing events, contributing new benchmarks for evaluating narrative reasoning in AI.
