The learned 'inverse' representations could enhance downstream tasks like visual question answering and socially intelligent assistance.
proposals.novelty: While models predict next tokens or captions, systematically treating the surrounding multimodal context as the input encoding and the central gap as the decoding target is a non-obvious inversion of the standard generative framework. This explicitly trains the model for abductive reasoning rather than extrapolation.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Visual Recognition via Dataset-Derived Prototypical Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary models rely on pre-trained word embeddings (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe) to map novel category names to a semantic space. This creates a bottleneck, as visual recognition performance becomes dependent on the quality and biases of an external, text-only model trained on a generic corpus, which may not align with visual semantics or specialized domains. A method that learns category semantics directly from visual data and associated language context within the training dataset would be more flexible and potentially more visually grounded.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can learn a robust, dataset-derived semantic embedding space for visual categories by jointly training a vision encoder and a lightweight text projector on image-caption pairs, eliminating the need for fixed, pre-trained word vectors. This learned space will support effective zero-shot generalization to novel visual concepts.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP) by replacing its fixed text encoder's final token embeddings with a trainable 'prototype network'. This network will take raw category names as input and map them to embeddings, but its parameters will be optimized end-to-end using the visual contrastive loss on large-scale image-text datasets (e.g., LAION). The key is to initialize this network randomly, not with pre-trained word vectors, and constrain its capacity to force the discovery of a visually relevant semantic structure. We will benchmark on open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation (extending Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation work) and image classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for vocabulary expansion in vision systems, decoupling them from off-the-shelf linguistic models. It will yield a more visually-aligned semantic space and demonstrate competitive or superior zero-shot performance on niche and fine-grained categories. The method will be released as a modular component for existing open-vocabulary architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed word embeddings or large language models for semantics, our approach learns the mapping from words to a semantic space purely from visual supervision within a multimodal dataset, making the representation inherently visual-task-oriented. This is distinct from simply fine-tuning a text encoder.

proposals.title: Visual Grounding of Abstract Relations: A Dataset and Model for Predicting Discontinuous Semantic Links from Images and Text
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models excel at recognizing objects and literal scene descriptions but struggle with inferring abstract, non-spatial relations (e.g., 'causes', 'used for', 'symbolizes') that are discontinuous in the visual field. This limits their reasoning for tasks like visual question answering, instruction following, and social intelligence in assistive agents, where understanding intent and implicit connections is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly training a model on a dataset of images paired with sentences describing abstract relations between depicted objects, we can learn multimodal representations that generalize to predict discontinuous semantic links beyond lexical co-occurrence or spatial proximity.
proposals.method: We will first construct the 'Abstract-Visual-Relations (AVR)' dataset by extending existing panoptic segmentation datasets (e.g., ADE20K, COCO) with crowd-sourced annotations for abstract relations between segmented objects. We will then train a transformer-based architecture that fuses visual features from a CNN/vision transformer with text embeddings, using a contrastive loss (inspired by CLIP) but augmented with a relation prediction head. This head will be trained to classify the abstract relation type between any two object embeddings modulated by the global context, moving beyond standard visual relationship detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark dataset for evaluating abstract relational reasoning in vision-language systems, and 2) a model demonstrating improved performance on downstream tasks requiring inference of non-obvious connections, such as neurally-guided assistance or open-vocabulary scene understanding. It will provide a framework for moving from 'what is where' to 'why it matters'.
proposals.novelty: While prior work detects spatial or verb-based relationships (e.g., 'person riding horse'), we focus on semantically rich, often discontinuous relations that require commonsense or causal knowledge, explicitly bridging Torralba's expertise in visual scenes with recent advances in multimodal language models and embedding modulation.

proposals.title: Generative Contextual Embedding for Open-Vocabulary Visual Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary models for segmentation and detection rely on aligning visual features with static, pre-computed text embeddings from an encoder (e.g., CLIP). This limits their ability to reason about nuanced, contextual relationships between objects and their real-world settings, which is crucial for building socially intelligent assistants that understand scenes as dynamic narratives, not just collections of objects. A model that can generate the linguistic context surrounding an object would create richer, more grounded multimodal representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a vision-language model with a decoder trained to generate the natural language sentences that describe an object's context (e.g., 'The cup is on the table next to the laptop') will produce more semantically powerful and generative visual embeddings, leading to improved performance on open-vocabulary reasoning and segmentation tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend an embedding modulation framework, like that used in Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation, by integrating a conditional language model decoder (e.g., a GPT-2/3 style transformer). This decoder will be trained to generate plausible surrounding sentences conditioned on the visual encoder's hidden state for an image region. The training objective will combine a reconstruction loss for known context (from datasets like Visual Genome) with a contrastive loss to ensure the generated text embedding aligns with the visual feature, modulating it for downstream tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel model architecture that moves from static text-visual alignment to dynamic, generative context modeling. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating the contextual understanding of vision-language systems and deliver improved performance on open-vocabulary segmentation and visual reasoning by leveraging generated linguistic context as an intermediate representation.
proposals.novelty: While existing work uses text encoders to *match* visual content, our approach uses a text decoder to *generate* contextual descriptions from visual features, creating a generative loop for multimodal representation learning. This integrates Torralba's expertise in visual datasets and object semantics with recent advances in neurally-guided assistance and multimodal neurons in language models.

proposals.title: Syntactically Grounded Visual Embeddings: Unsupervised Learning with Linguistic Constraints
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models like CLIP learn from loose image-text pairs, often ignoring the rich compositional and relational structure within language. This limits their ability to ground complex visual scenes with multiple objects and relationships, a core challenge in scene understanding and embodied AI. Integrating explicit linguistic structure can provide a stronger inductive bias for learning more semantically coherent and factorized visual representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating explicit syntactic constraints (e.g., dependency parses) or semantic role labels into the contrastive learning objective will force the model to learn visual embeddings that better disentangle objects, attributes, and relations, leading to improved performance on compositional reasoning and open-vocabulary segmentation tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the CLIP-style contrastive framework by modulating the text encoder's output embeddings based on a pre-computed dependency parse tree of the caption. Specifically, we will use a Graph Neural Network (GNN) over the parse tree to produce role-specific embeddings (e.g., for subject, object, modifier) which will be aligned with corresponding image regions identified by an off-the-shelf unsupervised object discovery module or a pretrained panoptic segmentation model. The training objective will combine the standard image-text contrastive loss with auxiliary losses that enforce consistency between visual regions and their predicted linguistic roles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that bridges syntactic linguistics and unsupervised visual representation learning. It will yield a model with more interpretable and compositionally-aware visual embeddings, directly advancing Torralba's themes of object-centric scene understanding. We will evaluate on challenging benchmarks for compositional visual reasoning and open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used language as a weak signal, explicitly enforcing syntactic structure as a constraint within the core contrastive objective is novel. This moves beyond simply using larger datasets or more parameters, introducing a structured prior derived from linguistic theory.

proposals.title: DocSkip: Learning Coherent Document Embeddings via Hierarchical Skip-Thought Objectives
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models and text-only transformers excel at short-range dependencies but struggle to capture the long-range narrative or logical coherence of multi-paragraph documents. This limits their ability to perform tasks requiring holistic document understanding, such as long-form QA, multi-document summarization, or generating consistent, context-rich descriptions for complex visual scenes. A robust method for learning paragraph- and document-level representations is a critical missing piece for scaling multimodal reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical encoder, trained with a scaled skip-thought objective that predicts surrounding paragraphs from a central one, can learn semantically rich and coherent embeddings for long text units, and that these embeddings will significantly improve the grounding of language in long-form visual narratives.
proposals.method: We will adapt the skip-thought vector framework to a hierarchical Transformer encoder. The model will first encode individual sentences, then aggregate them into a paragraph representation via a learned pooling layer or a lightweight paragraph-level Transformer. The core objective will be to reconstruct the embeddings of a document's preceding and succeeding paragraphs from the current paragraph's representation. We will leverage large-scale, pre-2026 multimodal datasets (e.g., Conceptual Captions, LAION) and long-form text corpora (e.g., BookCorpus) for training, evaluating the embeddings on tasks like paragraph ordering, document retrieval, and generating detailed descriptions for complex, real-world images from datasets like ADE20K or OpenImages.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, scalable method for learning coherent document-level embeddings, 2) a new model capable of linking long-form textual narratives to complex visual scenes, and 3) a public benchmark for evaluating paragraph- and document-level representation learning in a multimodal context, advancing the field beyond sentence- and object-level grounding.
proposals.novelty: While skip-thought exists for sentences and transformers model long sequences, the explicit hierarchical formulation for learning paragraph/document coherence via a predictive, context-reconstruction objective is novel. Its direct integration with Torralba's expertise in visual object datasets and multimodal grounding (from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation) creates a unique path to long-form vision-language understanding.

proposals.title: Sparse, Non-Local Context Windows for Visual-Language Embedding Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) align image regions with contiguous text spans, assuming dense, sequential context. This fails to capture the real-world, compositional nature of scenes where objects relate through complex, non-adjacent descriptions (e.g., 'the red mug' and 'on the wooden table' separated by other attributes). Exploring alternative context windows is crucial for building more robust, compositional, and physically-grounded scene understanding, directly impacting applications like robotics and assistive agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training VLMs with deliberately sparse and non-adjacent text context windows will improve their ability to learn disentangled, compositional object representations, leading to superior performance on open-vocabulary segmentation and retrieval tasks requiring complex relational reasoning.
proposals.method: We will extend embedding modulation frameworks (e.g., from open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation) by designing a context sampling module. During training, for a given image region, we will sample text tokens from non-adjacent sentences in the caption or from a structured scene graph, creating 'skip-context' windows. We will experiment with attention masking strategies in transformer-based text encoders and a gating mechanism to fuse these sparse signals with standard local context, evaluating on datasets like Visual Genome and simulated home environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for VLMs that decouples visual feature learning from strict textual locality. It will provide new insights into the emergence of multimodal 'neurons' for object properties and relations, and deliver models with improved compositional generalization for real-world, interactive systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work modulates embeddings with language, it relies on contiguous context. Our approach systematically breaks this contiguity, exploring a previously uncharted design space for context definition to explicitly foster non-local, relational grounding.

proposals.title: Unified Visual Encoders via Joint Unsupervised and Supervised Multitask Learning
proposals.motivation: Current vision models often rely on either large-scale unsupervised pre-training or supervised learning on specific, curated datasets. This can lead to a trade-off: unsupervised models learn rich, general features but may lack semantic precision, while supervised models are accurate on known tasks but fail to generalize beyond their training distribution. A method that effectively combines these paradigms is needed to create robust, semantically-grounded visual representations for real-world applications like home assistants and open-vocabulary recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a visual encoder on an unsupervised 'skip-task' (e.g., masked image modeling) alongside auxiliary supervised tasks (e.g., panoptic segmentation, object detection, sound source localization) will yield a unified representation that surpasses either paradigm alone in terms of generalization, semantic richness, and task performance.
proposals.method: We will build upon a transformer-based visual encoder (e.g., a ViT). The core unsupervised task will be a masked autoencoding objective, reconstructing masked patches. In parallel, we will attach lightweight, task-specific heads for several supervised auxiliary tasks. These will leverage Torralba's recent work, including open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation (using CLIP-style text alignment) and multimodal tasks linking visual objects to sound or impact events. A dynamic gradient balancing strategy will be used to manage the joint loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework that produces a single, multifunctional visual encoder excelling at both discriminative and generative tasks. It will provide empirical evidence on the synergistic effects of combining self-supervised and supervised signals. The resulting model and analysis will be a valuable resource for multimodal and embodied AI research.
proposals.novelty: While multitask learning is well-studied, the specific integration of a generative, patch-based 'skip-task' with diverse, semantically-rich supervised tasks (including non-visual modalities like sound) within a single, end-to-end trained encoder is novel. This moves beyond simple sequential pre-training/fine-tuning or multi-head setups on purely visual tasks.

proposals.title: Contrastive Learning of Multimodal Object Embeddings for Open-World Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 vision-language models often learn from web-scale image-text pairs, which can be noisy and lack precise, physically-grounded object correspondences. This limits their ability to perform fine-grained, open-vocabulary reasoning about objects in real-world scenes, a core theme in Torralba's work. A method is needed to structure the joint embedding space to better reflect the compositional and physical properties of objects.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a multimodal contrastive loss that explicitly aligns visual object regions with their textual descriptions, while separating them from incorrect pairings, will yield more semantically structured and physically grounded embeddings than standard image-caption contrastive learning.
proposals.method: We will build on frameworks like CLIP and OpenSeg, using a pretrained visual encoder (e.g., ViT) and text encoder (e.g., Transformer). We will leverage datasets with localized object annotations (e.g., Open Images, LVIS) to extract region features via ROI pooling. The core technical approach is a modified contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) applied at the object-description level, where positive pairs are an object crop and its class name or a referring expression, and negatives are mismatched crops and text. We will experiment with hard negative mining based on visual or semantic similarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a training methodology for more disentangled and physically meaningful object-centric embeddings. It will directly improve performance on downstream tasks central to the researcher's themes, such as open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and referring expression grounding, by providing a better-aligned multimodal feature space.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established, its targeted application to align *region-level* visual features with *object-level* language, using curated object-centric datasets rather than global image captions, is a focused and non-trivial extension for improving physical scene understanding.

proposals.title: Visual Denoising Autoencoders for Robust Multimodal Embedding Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models rely on clean, aligned image-text pairs, which are expensive to curate and often brittle to real-world noise and misalignment. Pre-2026, denoising autoencoders have proven powerful for learning robust representations in unimodal domains, but their principled application to the multimodal alignment problem, especially for grounding language in visual scenes, is underexplored. This limits model robustness and the efficient use of noisy, web-scale data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a multimodal model with a denoising autoencoder objective—corrupting either the linguistic input, the visual input, or their association—will force the model to learn more robust and disentangled joint embeddings, improving performance on tasks requiring fine-grained visual grounding and open-vocabulary recognition.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based vision-language architecture (e.g., a CLIP-like model) with a dedicated corruption and reconstruction module. For a given image-text pair, we will stochastically apply one of several corruption strategies: dropping or shuffling words in the caption, applying visual patches or noise to the image, or replacing the paired caption with a mismatched one from the dataset. The model will be trained to reconstruct the original, clean multimodal context, using a combination of contrastive loss (for alignment) and a masked token/patched pixel reconstruction loss (for denoising). We will benchmark on datasets like Visual Genome and COCO, and evaluate on open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and phrase grounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework for learning noise-invariant multimodal representations. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of denoising objectives for visual grounding and could lead to models that better utilize uncurated data. The resulting robust embeddings could enhance downstream applications in home assistants and scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While denoising is established in NLP and computer vision separately, its structured application to *corrupt the link* between modalities, and to specifically improve visual grounding and open-vocabulary segmentation, is a novel direction. This bridges Torralba's expertise in visual objects and datasets with recent methods in language and multimodal learning.

proposals.title: Memory-Augmented Embedding Modulation for Context-Aware Open-Vocabulary Vision
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary models for segmentation and recognition struggle with long-tail objects and complex scenes where visual appearance alone is ambiguous. Pre-2026, methods like embedding modulation use language to guide vision, but they process text prompts in a single forward pass without retaining scene-specific context, limiting their ability to reason about persistent entities or relationships over time or across a panoramic scene.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a vision-language model with a differentiable memory network to store and retrieve contextual sentence embeddings will enable more consistent and accurate open-vocabulary recognition by allowing the model to accumulate and reference descriptive knowledge beyond a single input frame.
proposals.method: We will extend an embedding modulation framework (e.g., from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation) with a key-value memory network. The keys will be learned embeddings of object-centric regions or spatial features, and the values will be the corresponding modulated text embeddings from detected entities. A cross-attention retrieval mechanism will allow the model to query this memory with current visual features to retrieve relevant prior context, which will be fused with the current language embeddings. We will train and evaluate on panoptic datasets (e.g., COCO, ADE20K) and dynamic video sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for integrating persistent contextual memory into open-vocabulary vision systems. It will provide quantitative improvements in consistency for video panoptic segmentation and for recognizing rare objects in complex, cluttered scenes by leveraging accumulated descriptions.
proposals.novelty: While memory networks exist, their integration into embedding modulation for open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation is novel, specifically using the memory to store and retrieve *modulated language embeddings* rather than raw features, creating a persistent, interpretable scene description bank.

proposals.title: Topic-Aware Multimodal Embeddings: Integrating Bayesian Topic Models with Vision-Language Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) learn joint embeddings from image-text pairs but often lack structured, interpretable semantic representations of the linguistic context. This limits their ability to reason about scene composition and object relationships in a human-understandable way, which is crucial for complex tasks like open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and socially intelligent assistance. Integrating probabilistic topic models, a well-established technique for discovering latent thematic structure in text, could provide this missing layer of semantic organization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting the text encoder of a pretrained VLM (e.g., CLIP) with a Bayesian topic model (LDA) will yield topic-aware sentence representations that improve model interpretability and performance on fine-grained, compositional visual reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will freeze the visual encoder and primary text transformer layers of a pretrained VLM. A neural variational inference network will be attached to the final [CLS] token embedding to approximate the posterior distribution over latent topics for an input sentence, following a Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) prior. The inferred topic distribution will be used to modulate the final sentence embedding via a gating mechanism before computing the image-text similarity. We will train this hybrid model on large-scale datasets (e.g., LAION) using a combined loss: the standard contrastive loss and the evidence lower bound (ELBO) from the topic model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable architecture that bridges classical probabilistic topic modeling with modern deep vision-language learning. It will provide a method to decompose scene descriptions into constituent topics (e.g., 'sports,' 'indoor,' 'cooking'), offering clearer insights into model decisions. We expect demonstrated improvements on tasks requiring nuanced semantic understanding, such as retrieval of images with complex captions or fine-grained attribute recognition.
proposals.novelty: While topic models have been used with word embeddings, their integration into the representation space of a frozen, large-scale VLM to create topic-modulated joint embeddings for vision-language tasks is novel. This approach uniquely combines the interpretability of Bayesian generative models with the powerful discriminative capabilities of pretrained transformers.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Context Attention for Open-Vocabulary Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary models for segmentation and detection rely on aligning image regions with text embeddings, but they often treat language prompts as flat, ignoring the compositional and hierarchical nature of natural language descriptions. This limits their ability to parse complex scenes where objects are defined by multi-sentence context or where certain words (e.g., 'large', 'metallic') are more critical for disambiguation than others. Grounding this in Torralba's work on visual datasets and object semantics, a method is needed to dynamically weight linguistic context at multiple granularities.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical attention mechanism that separately models attention over words and sentences, then fuses them, will enable more precise grounding of visual concepts by allowing the model to focus on the most salient linguistic context for each image region.
proposals.method: We will extend an embedding modulation framework (inspired by Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation) by adding a two-tier transformer-based attention module. The first tier computes word-level attention scores within each sentence. The second tier computes sentence-level attention across the full prompt. These scores will modulate the visual feature maps via a gating mechanism before computing region-text similarity. We will train and evaluate on panoptic and object detection tasks using datasets like ADE20K and LVIS, measuring performance on rare and composite categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architecture for fine-grained language-vision alignment that improves open-vocabulary recognition, especially for long-tail and context-dependent objects. It will also offer insights into how pretrained language models (e.g., from 'Multimodal Neurons' work) can be explicitly structured for hierarchical visual grounding.
proposals.novelty: While cross-attention is common, explicitly disentangling and hierarchically combining word and sentence-level attention for visual embedding modulation is unexplored. This bridges Torralba's recent focus on language-driven visual tasks with a structured, interpretable mechanism for context weighting.

proposals.title: Scaling Visual-Physical World Models with Internet-Scale Multimodal Narratives
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models are trained on curated image-text pairs, which are limited in scale and lack rich, contextual descriptions of object physics, function, and real-world interaction. To build socially intelligent assistants that understand the physical world, we need models grounded in the vast, unstructured knowledge of the internet, which contains implicit physical and causal relationships not found in books or standard datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a multimodal transformer on a massively scaled corpus of internet-derived text, aligned with visual data, will yield emergent representations of object affordances, physical properties, and common-sense reasoning that significantly outperform models trained on curated datasets alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a new dataset, WebNarrative-10B, by scraping and filtering HTML from diverse public web pages, preserving textual narratives around objects and scenes. We will align this text with images and videos using CLIP-style contrastive pre-training, but extend the architecture with a 'physical grounding' module inspired by multimodal neurons in text-only transformers. This module will attend to textual descriptions of mass, material, force, and sound, modulating visual embeddings to form unified visuo-physical representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, internet-scale multimodal dataset focused on physical and functional narratives, 2) a model demonstrating superior performance on open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and physical reasoning benchmarks, and 3) analysis showing the emergence of neurons responsive to abstract physical concepts, advancing interpretability.
proposals.novelty: While scaling text data is known for language models, its targeted application to learn grounded, physical world models from noisy web narratives—bridging Torralba's expertise in visual objects with recent methods in language and sound—is unexplored. The key innovation is the curation and alignment strategy for physical knowledge, not just scale.

proposals.title: Lightweight Visual-Language Embeddings: Distilling Skip-Thought for Efficient Open-Vocabulary Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Large-scale vision-language models like CLIP and large skip-thought encoders have revolutionized open-vocabulary recognition, but their computational cost hinders real-time deployment in robotics and AR/VR. A lightweight, efficient model that retains robust, generalizable visual-semantic embeddings is crucial for scalable, real-world applications requiring fast inference on edge devices. This work addresses the gap between powerful but bulky pre-trained encoders and the practical needs of resource-constrained systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully distilled, compact student network can learn to approximate the rich, multi-modal embedding space of a large skip-thought encoder with minimal performance loss, and that this distilled model will enable faster, open-vocabulary scene parsing and object recognition in dynamic environments.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-stage knowledge distillation framework. A large, frozen skip-thought encoder (teacher) will generate target embeddings for a large-scale, multi-modal dataset (e.g., a combination of image-caption pairs and egocentric video with narration). The student model will be a compact, hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture (e.g., a MobileNetV3 backbone with a small transformer head). Distillation will use a combination of L2 loss on the embedding space, a contrastive loss to preserve semantic structure, and task-specific losses (e.g., masked language modeling on visual features) to enforce robust feature learning, guided by techniques from 'Multimodal Neurons in Pretrained Text-Only Transformers'.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a publicly available, efficient student model for visual-language embeddings, enabling real-time, open-vocabulary perception. It will provide a systematic analysis of distillation strategies for cross-modal encoders and demonstrate the model's impact on downstream tasks like on-the-fly object recognition for socially intelligent assistants and panoptic segmentation in streaming video.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation is well-studied, its targeted application to large-scale, cross-modal skip-thought encoders for real-time, open-vocabulary scene understanding is novel. The approach uniquely integrates insights from multimodal neuron analysis and embedding modulation to guide the distillation of a semantically structured, rather than merely compressed, representation.

proposals.title: Inverse Context Retrieval: Reconstructing Visual and Textual Scenes from Sentence Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal systems excel at generating descriptive embeddings for images or text, but the inverse problem—reconstructing a plausible multimodal scene from a single sentence embedding—remains largely unexplored. Solving this is crucial for interpretability, data augmentation, and for AI assistants to infer unobserved context from partial descriptions, enhancing their reasoning and social intelligence. This aligns with the need for models that understand the implicit, real-world physics and object relationships embedded within language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sentence embedding from a powerful pretrained language model contains sufficient latent information about visual objects and their spatial relationships to enable the retrieval of a semantically and physically plausible multimodal context (e.g., an image or a longer descriptive paragraph) from a large, unlabeled corpus.
proposals.method: We will leverage a frozen, pretrained text-only transformer (e.g., GPT-2 or BERT) to generate query embeddings for sentences. A dual-encoder architecture will be trained to map both images (via a CLIP image encoder) and longer text passages to the same embedding space. The core technical innovation is an 'embedding modulation' network, inspired by recent work, that conditions the retrieval process on the query embedding to perform a neurally-guided, probabilistic search over a large-scale corpus like LAION or Conceptual Captions, ranking candidates by their likelihood of generating the query sentence.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a formal task and benchmark for inverse retrieval, providing a novel tool for analyzing the semantic content of sentence embeddings. It will advance methods for context-aware inference in assistive agents and contribute to understanding how object-centric and physical knowledge is encoded in language models.
proposals.novelty: While retrieval from text to context is standard, the inverse direction—from a condensed sentence vector to a multimodal scene—is a novel formulation. The approach uniquely combines insights from multimodal neurons in text models and neurally-guided probabilistic search to solve this under-constrained problem.

proposals.title: Learning Visual-Textual Embeddings from Unordered Sentence Streams via Cross-Modal Memory Banks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models rely on large, curated datasets of aligned image-text pairs, which are expensive to create and do not reflect the fragmented, non-sequential nature of real-world data streams (e.g., social media captions, IoT sensor logs, or assistive robot observations). Learning from isolated, single-sentence descriptions without explicit visual pairing or temporal order is a critical step toward more autonomous and scalable multimodal learning. This addresses a core limitation in building agents that operate in open-world environments where data arrives asynchronously.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cross-modal memory bank, continuously updated with sentence embeddings, can serve as a latent alignment space, enabling a model to learn robust visual-textual representations from a stream of unpaired, single-sentence descriptions and a separate stream of unlabeled images.
proposals.method: We will adapt a contrastive learning framework (e.g., CLIP-style) but replace batch-based negative sampling with a dynamically maintained memory bank of text embeddings from the sentence stream. An image encoder will be trained to align with this memory using a noise-contrastive estimation loss, where the positive text is retrieved via similarity from the bank. Inspired by Torralba's work on embedding modulation and multimodal neurons, we will incorporate an attention-based modulation layer to condition the visual features on the aggregated memory context, facilitating the discovery of latent object-concept associations without direct pairing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for learning from non-sequential, weakly paired data, expanding the applicability of vision-language models to domains with sparse supervision. It will yield a new benchmark and dataset simulation for evaluating learning from single-sentence streams, and provide insights into how semantic structure emerges from unordered linguistic input.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in replacing explicit image-text pairs with a self-organized, cross-modal memory for alignment, moving beyond batch-based contrastive learning. It uniquely combines insights from embedding modulation for open-vocabulary recognition with the challenge of learning from temporally disconnected, sentence-level supervision.

proposals.title: Active Annotation for Holistic Long-Tail Recognition via Performance-Aware Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: Long-tail recognition remains a critical challenge in real-world computer vision, where models must perform well on both common and rare object categories. Current active learning and annotation strategies often focus narrowly on tail classes, risking significant degradation in head class accuracy and overall model robustness. An adaptive, holistic strategy is needed to allocate finite annotation resources efficiently across the entire class distribution, maximizing a comprehensive performance metric.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic annotation allocation system, guided by real-time validation performance across all classes, can identify the most cost-effective annotation targets to optimize holistic long-tail recognition, thereby improving tail performance without sacrificing core competency on common classes.
proposals.method: We propose a controller agent, implemented as a lightweight transformer or recurrent network, that ingests per-class validation performance metrics (e.g., precision, recall) from a base vision model (e.g., a CLIP-based panoptic segmentation network). This agent will be trained via reinforcement learning, with a reward function balancing gains in tail class metrics against maintenance of head class accuracy. The system will iteratively select image batches for human annotation, prioritizing classes where annotation yields the greatest marginal improvement in a holistic metric like Pareto-balanced accuracy, and will integrate with scalable annotation pipelines like those in NOPA.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive framework for annotation resource allocation that directly optimizes for balanced long-tail performance. It will provide a practical methodology for building more robust vision datasets and models. We will also release a benchmark and analysis of dynamic annotation strategies on large-scale, long-tail datasets like LVIS or Open Images.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static or tail-focused active learning, our method dynamically reallocates annotation effort based on a holistic, real-time performance model. It frames annotation budgeting as an online control problem optimized for a composite metric, preventing the common pitfall of over-specializing on the tail at the expense of overall model utility.

proposals.title: EcoSample: Adaptive Annotation for Long-Tail Visual Recognition via Ecological Survey Strategies
proposals.motivation: Large-scale visual datasets, while foundational, exhibit severe long-tail distributions, leading to poor model performance on rare classes. Current annotation strategies, often uniform or heuristic, inefficiently allocate labeling budget, exacerbating this imbalance. Inspired by ecology, where adaptive sampling is used to monitor rare species, we propose a principled, model-guided framework to make annotation cost-effective for building balanced, high-performance vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: An active annotation system that dynamically stratifies the data space and prioritizes sequences predicted to contain rare visual categories, analogous to ecological survey designs, will yield more balanced datasets and significantly improve open-vocabulary recognition performance on the long tail compared to standard uniform sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop an online system integrating a vision-language base model (e.g., CLIP) as a prior for uncertainty and rarity estimation over unlabeled video streams or image collections. Drawing from adaptive cluster sampling and sequential decision-making, the system will use the model's multimodal embeddings to identify spatial-temporal regions with high likelihood of containing under-represented class embeddings. These prioritized regions will be presented for annotation, iteratively refining the model's own sampling policy. The core algorithm will balance exploration (new scenes) and exploitation (confirmed rare categories).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, ecology-inspired framework for efficient dataset annotation tailored for long-tail recognition. 2) A new active sampling benchmark for video/image streams, moving beyond static image pools. 3) Empirical evidence that strategic annotation informed by model predictions and ecological principles can create more performant and equitable open-vocabulary segmentation and detection models.
proposals.novelty: While active learning exists, the direct analogy to ecological survey methodology—treating rare object categories as endangered species to be actively sought—and its application to streaming, multimodal data for open-vocabulary segmentation is novel. It inverts the focus from improving the model on fixed data to shaping the data collection process itself using model guidance.

proposals.title: Long-Tail Object-Centric Annotation for Robust Open-World Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale visual datasets (e.g., COCO, ADE20K) exhibit a severe long-tail distribution, where rare object categories are critically under-represented. This bias limits the real-world robustness of computer vision models, particularly for open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation tasks, as models fail to recognize or segment less common but semantically important objects. Addressing this annotation imbalance is crucial for building socially intelligent assistants that can operate in diverse, unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically re-allocating annotation effort from common to rare object categories, we can train models that achieve superior open-vocabulary segmentation performance on long-tail distributions without a proportional increase in total annotation cost. We hypothesize that a neurally-guided, online assistance framework can optimize this annotation process.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel interactive annotation system, building on NOPA's online probabilistic assistance, that actively prioritizes image regions likely to contain rare objects from a pre-defined long-tail list. The system will use a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to compute region-level embeddings and estimate category rarity. An embedding modulation network, inspired by open-vocabulary segmentation work, will be fine-tuned online to refine predictions based on annotator feedback, creating a human-in-the-loop cycle that efficiently expands long-tail annotations in datasets like OpenImages or a new curated set.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) a principled framework for cost-effective, long-tail-focused dataset annotation; 2) a new benchmark for evaluating open-vocabulary segmentation on rare objects; and 3) models with significantly improved performance on long-tail categories, advancing towards more equitable and comprehensive scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While dataset imbalance is a known problem, our approach uniquely inverts the annotation priority at the data collection stage and integrates a neurally-guided, interactive system to make long-tail annotation tractable. It combines insights from multimodal neurons, active learning, and open-vocabulary segmentation in a novel, applied pipeline.

proposals.title: Adversarial Consensus: Modeling Annotator Behavior for Robust Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Large-scale visual datasets often rely on crowdsourced annotations, which are vulnerable to systematic bias and coordinated adversarial corruption. This compromises the reliability of models trained on such data, especially for safety-critical applications like autonomous systems. Existing robust aggregation methods primarily address random noise but fail against sophisticated, collusive attacks that can force a model to learn a false consensus.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly modeling a latent visual consensus, individual annotator decision functions, and the statistical signatures of collusion, a system can be made robust to adversarial label corruption while scaling efficiently with a minimal set of trusted expert anchors.
proposals.method: We propose a probabilistic graphical model with a neural parameterization. The core is a vision transformer (ViT) backbone that generates latent object representations. These feed into per-annotator bias networks, modeled as lightweight MLPs, that capture individual labeling tendencies. A collusion detection module, inspired by anomaly detection in graph networks, analyzes correlations in bias patterns across annotators and tasks. The model is trained via a variational objective that maximizes the likelihood of a small set of trusted anchor labels while minimizing agreement with detected adversarial clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable framework for learning from untrusted crowdsourced visual data that is provably robust to coordinated attacks. It will advance the field by providing both a new benchmark for adversarial label corruption in vision datasets and a method that integrates behavioral modeling directly into the representation learning loop.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on label noise, this approach explicitly models and detects collusive annotator behavior as an integral part of the learning process, preventing the convergence to a false visual consensus. It bridges tools from robust statistics, behavioral modeling, and adversarial machine learning for visual data curation.

proposals.title: ConsensusNet: Learning Visual Representations from Noisy, Multi-Annotator Data via Scalable Bayesian Aggregation
proposals.motivation: Scaling high-quality visual annotation for tasks like open-vocabulary segmentation is a major bottleneck, as expert labeling is prohibitively expensive. While crowdsourcing platforms offer a scalable alternative, the resulting data is inherently noisy and unreliable. This project addresses the core challenge of how to efficiently learn robust visual models directly from such imperfect, multi-annotator data without costly expert verification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the reliability of individual non-expert annotators within a scalable Bayesian consensus framework, we can infer high-confidence pseudo-labels that enable training visual models (e.g., for segmentation or detection) whose performance approaches that of models trained on expert data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we adapt and scale the Dawid-Skene model using variational inference for efficient online learning of per-annotator confusion matrices and consensus labels. Second, we integrate this consensus module directly into a vision transformer (ViT) or a Mask2Former-like architecture for panoptic segmentation, allowing the model to be trained end-to-end on raw, redundant annotations. The training will leverage a novel loss that weights the supervision signal from each annotator by their dynamically estimated reliability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a scalable, open-source framework for aggregating noisy visual annotations, 2) a method for training state-of-the-art vision models directly on cheap, non-expert data, and 3) new insights into the robustness of multimodal (vision-language) representations when learned from imperfect supervisory signals.
proposals.novelty: While consensus models exist in crowdsourcing literature, their tight integration into end-to-end trainable, high-capacity vision architectures for complex tasks like panoptic segmentation is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc label aggregation to jointly learn representation and annotator reliability.

proposals.title: FlowCrowd: Closing the Loop for Dense Correspondence with Real-Time Feedback and Noisy Human Supervision
proposals.motivation: Learning dense visual correspondences is fundamental for scene understanding, but state-of-the-art methods rely on curated, static datasets (e.g., MegaDepth, ScanNet) that are expensive to create and lack the scale and diversity of real-world visual phenomena. This creates a bottleneck for generalization. Simultaneously, real-time optical flow and SIFT flow algorithms provide immediate estimates but often lack semantic robustness. This project aims to bridge this gap by creating a scalable, interactive data collection and learning paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that couples a fast, initial correspondence algorithm with a robust online learning model can efficiently distill noisy, real-time feedback from non-experts into a high-performance, generalizable dense correspondence model, surpassing the quality achievable from static datasets alone.
proposals.method: We will develop an interactive web tool where users are shown image pairs and a real-time SIFT flow visualization. Users provide quick, noisy feedback via clicks (correcting endpoints), drags (adjusting flow vectors), or binary judgments. This stream of (image pair, initial flow, user correction) triplets will train a robust neural model in an online fashion. The core architecture will be a transformer-based flow refinement network, inspired by RAFT's iterative updates but conditioned on aggregated user signal. We will employ noise-aware loss functions (e.g., based on beta distributions) to weight the reliability of crowd annotations.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, scalable framework for collecting dense correspondence data via real-time interaction. 2) A new state-of-the-art model for open-vocabulary dense correspondence, trained via this loop, evaluated on panoptic segmentation and geometric matching tasks. 3) A public dataset of crowd-refined flow fields, capturing challenging real-world correspondences.
proposals.novelty: The closed-loop fusion of real-time flow computation for immediate user interaction and online learning from the resulting noisy signal is a new paradigm for visual correspondence, moving beyond static dataset curation. It leverages the 'human-in-the-loop' not just for annotation, but as an integral, real-time component of the learning algorithm itself.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Scene Understanding with Noisy Non-Expert Annotations via Multimodal Embedding Calibration
proposals.motivation: Scaling high-quality visual datasets for tasks like panoptic segmentation is bottlenecked by the cost and expertise required for precise pixel-level annotation. While crowdsourcing offers scalability, label noise from non-expert workers severely degrades model performance, especially for open-vocabulary recognition where novel categories are involved. This work addresses the critical need for annotation frameworks that are both scalable and robust, enabling the creation of larger, more diverse datasets without sacrificing model accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the noise patterns of non-expert annotators within a multimodal (vision-language) embedding space, we can learn a calibration function that corrects noisy labels and enables training of high-fidelity open-vocabulary segmentation models directly from inexpensive crowd-sourced data.
proposals.method: We will extend embedding modulation techniques from open-vocabulary segmentation to incorporate a noise-aware training pipeline. A small set of expert-verified images will seed a noise model that captures common non-expert errors (e.g., confusion between visually or semantically similar categories). This model will inform a lightweight transformer-based calibration network that operates on the language-aligned visual embeddings, adjusting them during training to be robust to annotation inconsistencies. We will validate the approach on a newly collected dataset of indoor scenes annotated by both experts and non-experts via a platform like Amazon Mechanical Turk.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for learning from noisy non-expert annotations in open-vocabulary visual recognition, 2) a new benchmark dataset with paired expert/non-expert annotations for evaluating label noise robustness, and 3) empirical evidence that calibrated multimodal embeddings can significantly close the performance gap between models trained on expensive versus scalable annotation sources.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior label noise methods that assume closed-set classification, our approach uniquely tackles open-vocabulary segmentation by leveraging the semantic structure of pretrained multimodal embeddings to model and correct noise, directly connecting Torralba's work on visual datasets and object understanding with recent advances in language-vision models and online assistance.

proposals.title: Unbinding Scene Understanding: Probing Visual-Linguistic Models in Abstract and Synthetic Visual Environments
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models are trained on internet-scale datasets of natural images, conflating object recognition with real-world contextual priors. This makes it difficult to isolate and evaluate a model's core capacity for compositional scene understanding from its reliance on learned statistical regularities of the physical world. A systematic study using non-natural visual inputs is needed to diagnose these dependencies and build more robust, generalizable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art vision-language models will show a significant performance drop on scene understanding tasks when presented with abstract or synthetic visual inputs lacking natural spatial context, and that this drop can be mitigated by targeted training with a curriculum of procedurally generated scenes.
proposals.method: We will create a novel benchmark, 'SynthPanoptic,' using game engines and procedural generation to produce synthetic and abstract scenes with panoptic segmentation masks. We will evaluate pretrained models like OFA and Open-vocabulary DETR on this benchmark. To improve robustness, we will employ embedding modulation techniques, inspired by Torralba's prior work, to adapt frozen visual backbones using a contrastive objective on a new multimodal dataset pairing abstract scenes with detailed textual descriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a diagnostic benchmark for disentangling contextual priors from scene understanding, 2) insights into the failure modes of large vision-language models on non-natural imagery, and 3) a method to enhance model robustness using synthetic data, reducing dependency on large-scale real-world image collection.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic data is often used for domain adaptation, its systematic use as a tool to *unbind* and *probe* the components of scene understanding in multimodal models—combining Torralba's expertise in visual semantics, datasets, and embedding modulation—is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Panoptic-DG: A Domain-Generalization Benchmark for Open-Vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Current panoptic segmentation models are typically evaluated on single datasets, leading to over-optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment where visual domains (e.g., indoor, urban, web) vary drastically. This creates a critical gap in understanding model robustness and generalizability. A unified, multi-domain benchmark is essential to drive progress towards reliable, real-world vision systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a domain-invariant evaluation protocol, which explicitly separates in-domain and cross-domain performance, will reveal significant generalization gaps in state-of-the-art models not captured by existing benchmarks, and that models leveraging cross-modal alignment (vision-language) will show superior cross-domain robustness.
proposals.method: We will create a unified benchmark by curating and standardizing the evaluation splits of ADE20K (indoor/scenes), Cityscapes (urban driving), Mapillary Vistas (diverse street-level), and COCO (general objects). The core technical contribution is a two-part domain-invariant scoring system: (1) standard in-domain metrics (PQ, SQ, RQ) per dataset, and (2) a novel cross-domain generalization score computed by evaluating a model trained on a source domain (e.g., COCO) directly on all other target domains without fine-tuning. We will benchmark leading open-vocabulary models (e.g., based on CLIP, VLMs) and traditional supervised models using this protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized benchmark for evaluating cross-domain robustness in panoptic segmentation, offering a more realistic assessment for real-world applications. It will establish new baselines, identify failure modes across domains, and release a public evaluation framework to guide future research in domain generalization and model robustness.
proposals.novelty: While multi-dataset benchmarks exist for classification, a panoptic segmentation benchmark with a formalized cross-domain generalization metric is novel. The explicit decoupling of in-domain and cross-domain performance moves beyond simple aggregate scoring to diagnose specific model weaknesses.

proposals.title: PQ-ADE: A Unified Panoptic Quality Metric and Standardized Protocol for Scene Understanding, Inspired by Ecological Community Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current panoptic segmentation evaluation is fragmented, using separate metrics for 'things' (instance segmentation) and 'stuff' (semantic segmentation). This makes holistic model comparison difficult and fails to capture the true goal of unified scene parsing. A single, principled metric is needed to drive progress, similar to how unified indices like the Simpson Index advanced biodiversity studies in ecology by combining richness and evenness.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified panoptic quality (PQ) metric, derived by mathematically reconciling instance-level precision/recall with region-level coverage accuracy, will provide a more holistic and interpretable measure of model performance. Furthermore, a standardized evaluation protocol on ADE20K will significantly reduce measurement variance and establish a definitive benchmark.
proposals.method: We will formally define PQ by extending the ecological Simpson Index analogy: treating 'things' classes as species (richness) and 'stuff' regions as proportional abundances (evenness). The metric will integrate a modified version of the Average Precision for 'things' with a Jaccard-like index for 'stuff' into a single score. We will implement this metric in a public evaluation toolkit and establish a rigorous protocol for ADE20K, defining official splits, annotation consistency rules (e.g., for boundary pixels and rare classes), and a fixed evaluation pipeline to ensure reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, interpretable PQ metric that unifies panoptic segmentation evaluation. 2) A standardized, community-adopted benchmark protocol for ADE20K, reducing inconsistent reporting. 3) A re-evaluation of existing state-of-the-art models under this new framework, providing new insights into their strengths and weaknesses.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct, formal analogy from ecological community analysis to computer vision evaluation, leading to a mathematically grounded unified metric. While metrics like the existing Panoptic Quality exist, our approach derives from first principles of diversity measurement and is coupled with a strict, ecological-field-method-inspired protocol for benchmark consistency.

proposals.title: Phylo-Seg: Hierarchical Visual Segmentation with Phylogenetic Clustering Priors
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation models treat object categories as a flat list, ignoring the rich, hierarchical part-whole structure inherent in visual scenes (e.g., car->wheel->tire). This limits their ability to enforce consistent segmentation across scales and to leverage structural priors for recognizing novel objects. Drawing from computational biology, where inferring the tree of life from sequence data is a solved hierarchical clustering problem, offers a principled framework to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a phylogenetic-inspired hierarchical clustering loss into a vision transformer or CNN backbone will force the learned visual embeddings to respect annotated object-part hierarchies, leading to more consistent and structurally coherent segmentation, especially for rare or novel object categories.
proposals.method: We will adapt algorithms from phylogenetic tree reconstruction (e.g., neighbor-joining, hierarchical Bayesian models) to operate on visual feature embeddings. A transformer-based segmentation model (e.g., Mask2Former) will be extended with a dedicated loss term that minimizes the divergence between the pairwise distance matrix of segment embeddings and a target matrix derived from ground-truth hierarchical annotations (e.g., from PartImageNet). This loss will be integrated with standard segmentation losses during training, encouraging the feature space to embed hierarchical relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel segmentation model that explicitly reasons about object-part hierarchies, improving segmentation consistency and generalization. It will also contribute a new benchmark for evaluating hierarchical segmentation performance and demonstrate the value of cross-disciplinary analogies from computational biology for computer vision.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical losses exist, directly translating phylogenetic tree-building algorithms into a differentiable loss for visual representation learning is novel. This moves beyond flat multi-label or simple tree-structured models to a probabilistic, distance-based formulation of visual taxonomy.

proposals.title: Hierarchical CRFs with Multi-Scale Graph Cuts for Consistent Object-Part Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation models, while powerful, often produce segmentations that are topologically inconsistent, such as parts floating without their parent objects. This limits their utility for robotics and scene understanding, where logical structure is crucial. A principled inference framework that enforces known semantic hierarchies during segmentation, rather than just at training time, is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating segmentation as energy minimization in a multi-scale Conditional Random Field (CRF) with an explicit object-part hierarchy, and solving it with adapted graph-cut algorithms, will yield more semantically consistent segmentations than flat, post-hoc reasoning models.
proposals.method: We will construct a CRF with random variables at multiple scales (e.g., object, part, superpixel) connected by hierarchical edges encoding 'part-of' relationships. The energy function will combine unary potentials from a vision-language backbone (e.g., CLIP) with pairwise potentials enforcing boundary coherence and hierarchical consistency. We will develop a novel multi-label graph-cut algorithm, extending α-expansion, that propagates label constraints across scales during the move-making process, ensuring cuts respect the semantic tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new structured prediction model that integrates hierarchical semantic knowledge directly into the inference engine for segmentation. It will provide a benchmark for evaluating topological consistency in segmentation and deliver a practical tool for generating more reliable scene parses for embodied AI agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a known semantic hierarchy into the graph structure of the CRF and the design of a graph-cut optimization procedure that explicitly operates across scales, moving beyond using hierarchies only as a source of training labels or for post-processing.

proposals.title: Causal Scene Diagnosis: Disentangling Dataset Biases for Robust Cross-Dataset Scene Parsing
proposals.motivation: Scene understanding models trained on datasets like ADE20K often fail to generalize to new environments due to entangled dataset biases in texture, object context, and label semantics. Current robustness evaluations are largely passive, measuring failure without diagnosing its specific causes. A causal, intervention-based framework is needed to actively identify and mitigate these biases to build models that understand the physical world, not just a specific dataset.
proposals.hypothesis: The generalization failure of scene parsers across datasets can be attributed to a small set of identifiable, causal factors (e.g., texture-context confounding, taxonomic mismatches), which can be isolated through controlled interventions and causal mediation analysis on model representations.
proposals.method: We will create a causal intervention framework by constructing hybrid training regimes from ADE20K and target datasets (e.g., Cityscapes, Mapillary Vistas). Using a panoptic segmentation backbone (e.g., Mask2Former), we will systematically intervene on data factors—applying style transfer for texture, swapping object co-occurrences, and aligning taxonomies via embedding modulation inspired by open-vocabulary methods. We will then apply causal mediation analysis (e.g., using interventional direct/indirect effect estimation) to the model's intermediate multimodal transformer representations to quantify each factor's causal effect on prediction errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A diagnostic methodology to pinpoint the causal drivers of cross-dataset failure, moving beyond correlation. 2) A set of model-agnostic, prescriptive guidelines and targeted data augmentation strategies (e.g., causal debiasing augmentations) to actively improve robustness. 3) New insights into the compositional grounding of vision models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work measures generalization gaps, we propose actively intervening on the data generation process to perform causal disentanglement of biases, transforming a diagnostic task into a prescriptive one. This integrates causal inference with large-scale vision model analysis in a novel way for scene understanding.

proposals.title: Scene Grammar: Syntactic Parsing of Visual Hierarchies using Compositional Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Understanding scenes requires more than object detection; it requires modeling their intrinsic hierarchical part-whole structure. Current scene graphs are often flat or manually defined, lacking the rich, recursive compositionality found in natural language syntax. Bridging this gap could enable more structured, interpretable, and generalizable scene understanding for robotics and AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: That the compositional mechanisms and dependency parsing algorithms from NLP can be effectively adapted to induce a latent 'scene grammar' from visual data, where objects form constituents that combine into larger scene structures.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP) with a neural syntactic parser. Building on work like 'Multimodal Neurons in Pretrained Text-Only Transformers', we will train a Transformer-based constituency parser to operate on region embeddings from an open-vocabulary panoptic segmenter (inspired by Torralba's prior work). The parser will be trained using a contrastive loss, aligning visual constituency trees with syntactically parsed textual descriptions of the scene from datasets like Visual Genome or a newly curated set.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for inducing hierarchical scene structure without predefined graph schemas. 2) A new dataset pairing images with parsed visual and textual trees. 3) Demonstrated improvements on downstream tasks requiring structural reasoning, such as compositional visual question answering or robotic instruction following.
proposals.novelty: While scene graphs are common, applying the formal machinery of syntactic constituency and dependency parsing from NLP to visual structure is largely unexplored. The key novelty is treating object embeddings as words and learning a grammar for their visual composition, rather than predicting fixed relation types.

proposals.title: Meta-SimAL: Meta-Active Learning via Simulation for Robust Visual Annotation Under Domain Shift
proposals.motivation: Active learning (AL) strategies are highly sensitive to domain shift, making strategies learned on one dataset (e.g., indoor scenes) perform poorly on another (e.g., driving scenes). This unreliability hinders the efficient annotation of large-scale, diverse visual datasets, which is critical for training robust models in real-world applications like robotics and autonomous systems. A principled method to adapt AL strategies to new visual domains without extensive trial-and-error is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, simulation-based meta-selection process, informed by a small initial target-domain sample, can reliably predict the most effective combination of active learning criteria (uncertainty, diversity, representativeness) for a given visual domain, leading to more sample-efficient annotation.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-learner that profiles a target domain (e.g., a new robotics environment) using a small seed set and a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to extract distributional features. This profile will parameterize a bank of simulation environments where candidate AL strategies (e.g., CoreSet, BADGE, entropy sampling) are evaluated on a held-out validation set. A meta-predictor (a small transformer) will learn to map domain profiles to optimal strategy weights, which will then guide an adaptive sampler for the real annotation loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for data-efficient annotation transfer across visual domains, reducing the cost of building new datasets. It will yield insights into the relationship between visual data characteristics and effective query strategies. We will release a benchmark for domain-adaptive active learning in vision, building on datasets like ADE20K and OpenImages.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior meta-active learning work focused on within-domain task adaptation, our method explicitly addresses cross-domain visual shift by using simulation-based meta-selection grounded in a domain profile. It integrates vision-language models for domain characterization, aligning with Torralba's recent multimodal research themes.

proposals.title: Optimal Experimental Design for Visual Active Learning: A Geophysical Analogy
proposals.motivation: Active learning for image annotation remains a critical challenge in computer vision, especially for open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation tasks where labeling is extremely costly. Current query strategies (e.g., entropy, margin) are often heuristic and lack a rigorous information-theoretic foundation for sequential decision-making. Meanwhile, geophysical survey design has long solved a structurally similar problem—optimizing expensive sensor placements to infer hidden structures—using principled Optimal Experimental Design (OED).
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing active learning as an OED problem, specifically by maximizing the Fisher Information of a pre-trained vision model's parameters with respect to candidate image annotations, will yield a more efficient and principled query strategy than existing heuristics.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large pre-trained vision model (e.g., a CLIP-based or DINOv2 backbone) as a prior over the visual world. For an unlabeled image pool, we will compute the expected Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) for model parameters based on the predictive distribution (e.g., from a classification or segmentation head). We will implement a sequential D-optimal design strategy, selecting the batch of images that maximizes the determinant of the aggregated FIM. This core algorithm will be integrated into an active learning loop for annotation on datasets like ADE20K or OpenImages.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a rigorous, model-based framework for active learning derived from physical sciences, providing a new theoretical grounding for sample selection. It will deliver a practical, transferable active learning strategy that reduces annotation cost for state-of-the-art vision models, particularly in data-scarce, open-vocabulary settings.
proposals.novelty: The direct translation of geophysical OED criteria (like D-optimality) to visual active learning is novel, moving beyond common uncertainty sampling to a formal criterion of global parameter informativeness. This bridges a mature methodology from physical inversion with modern large-scale visual representation learning.

proposals.title: Transfer Active Learning for Long-Tail Instance Discovery in Visual Scenes
proposals.motivation: Real-world visual datasets exhibit long-tailed distributions, where rare object categories are critically under-represented, severely limiting the performance of open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation models on these tail classes. Current active learning strategies for vision often prioritize general image-level uncertainty, which is inefficient for discovering and precisely annotating rare instances. This research directly addresses the core bottleneck of annotation cost for building balanced, high-performance models capable of understanding the full diversity of visual scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: A transfer active learning framework, which adapts query strategies from other data modalities and employs a dual-focus acquisition function, can strategically optimize annotation effort to discover and delineate rare object instances, thereby efficiently mitigating the long-tail problem in visual recognition.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first adapts region-based uncertainty sampling techniques from NLP (e.g., inspired by token-level uncertainty in sequence models) and time-series analysis to the visual domain, generating pixel-level uncertainty maps for rare class logits. The core is a dual-focus acquisition function: a batch-level selector uses a diversity measure on CLIP-like embeddings to choose images likely to contain novel rare objects, and a within-image region selector identifies pixels with highest entropy across tail-class predictions. This closed-loop system will be integrated with a panoptic segmentation model (e.g., MaskFormer-based) for iterative training and evaluation on long-tail datasets like LVIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel active learning framework that directly targets annotation efficiency for long-tail visual categories. It will advance open-vocabulary segmentation by providing a method to strategically build more balanced datasets. The resulting system offers a practical path toward socially intelligent assistants that can recognize a comprehensive set of objects in complex home environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the transfer and fusion of active query strategies from non-visual domains specifically for rare instance discovery, combined with a dual-level (image and pixel) acquisition policy that explicitly targets the long-tail distribution, moving beyond generic image-level informativeness.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Open-Vocabulary Scene Parsing: From Simple Objects to Complex Real-World Scenes
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary scene parsing models are trained on complex, cluttered datasets from the start, which can lead to suboptimal learning dynamics and difficulty in recognizing fine-grained object details. A curriculum learning approach, inspired by human learning, could provide a more structured and efficient training pathway. This is crucial for building robust visual understanding systems for real-world applications like home assistants, which must parse dynamic, multi-object environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation model using a curriculum of increasing scene complexity—from isolated objects to cluttered multi-instance scenes—will yield superior generalization, faster convergence, and more robust object and stuff category recognition compared to standard training regimes.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Embedding Modulation framework for open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation. The curriculum will be defined by sampling training batches from datasets of increasing complexity: starting with object-centric datasets (e.g., OpenImages), progressing to structured indoor scenes (e.g., ADE20K), and culminating in dense, real-world street scenes (e.g., Mapillary Vistas or BDD100K).
