We will implement a neurally-guided scheduler, inspired by NOPA, to dynamically adjust the curriculum progression based on the model's online performance, rather than using a fixed schedule. The core transformer backbone will be initialized from a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to leverage multimodal priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training paradigm for open-vocabulary scene understanding, demonstrating measurable gains in accuracy and efficiency. It will yield new insights into how visual recognition systems learn compositional structure. The resulting models will be more capable for socially intelligent assistants that need to understand evolving physical environments.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been explored in narrow domains, its systematic application to open-vocabulary, panoptic-level scene parsing—with a neurally-guided scheduler and a progression defined by real-world scene complexity—is novel. This bridges Torralba's core themes of physics, objects, and real images with recent methods in language-guided segmentation and adaptive interaction.

proposals.title: Efficient Open-Vocabulary Scene Parsing via Embedding-Guided Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: Large-scale vision models trained on datasets like ADE20K achieve impressive open-vocabulary scene understanding but are computationally prohibitive for real-time applications like robotics or augmented reality. Knowledge distillation offers a path to efficient deployment, but standard techniques often fail to preserve the nuanced, multi-modal semantic and spatial knowledge required for dense prediction tasks like panoptic segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distilling knowledge from a large, open-vocabulary teacher model into a compact student network, with a focus on preserving the teacher's rich embedding space structure and spatial consistency, will yield a model that maintains high segmentation accuracy while achieving a significant speed-up suitable for real-time deployment.
proposals.method: We will use a state-of-the-art open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation model (e.g., based on CLIP or a similar vision-language backbone) trained on ADE20K as the teacher. The student will be a lightweight encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., a modified DeepLabV3+ with a MobileNet backbone). Beyond standard logit matching, we will design distillation losses that align the student's intermediate feature maps with the teacher's, enforce consistency in the class-embedding similarity distributions, and use the teacher's 'multimodal neuron' activations as spatial attention guides to help the student focus on semantically critical regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a highly efficient, deployable model for open-vocabulary scene parsing, validated on ADE20K and real-world indoor scenes. It will provide a novel distillation framework specifically tailored for preserving the compositional and semantic knowledge of large vision-language models in dense prediction tasks, advancing the field of efficient multimodal perception.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation is well-studied for classification, its application to open-vocabulary, dense prediction is less explored. The novelty lies in explicitly distilling the structural properties of the teacher's multimodal embedding space and leveraging insights from 'multimodal neurons' to guide spatial learning in the student, moving beyond simple output mimicry.

proposals.title: Webly-Supervised Scaling of Scene Understanding: Expanding ADE20K with Cross-Modal Embedding Alignment
proposals.motivation: Large-scale, densely annotated datasets like ADE20K are foundational for scene understanding but are costly and time-consuming to create, limiting their scale and diversity. Web-crawled image-text pairs offer a vast, inexpensive source of supervision, but leveraging them for structured panoptic segmentation remains a significant challenge. This work addresses the core problem of scaling up high-quality scene parsing data with minimal human effort, directly impacting the development of more robust and generalizable vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by aligning the dense, structured visual embeddings of a panoptic segmentation model with the semantic embeddings of web-sourced text descriptions, we can create a scalable, automated pipeline to accurately label new images for scene parsing, thereby effectively expanding datasets like ADE20K.
proposals.method: We will build upon Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation (OVPS) architectures, which map image regions to a shared CLIP-like embedding space. Our pipeline will: 1) Crawl web images using text queries derived from ADE20K's object and stuff categories. 2) Use a pre-trained OVPS model to generate initial pseudo-labels for these images. 3) Implement a novel embedding modulation network, inspired by recent work on multimodal neurons in language models, to refine these labels by enforcing consistency between the visual region embeddings and the embeddings of their associated web text. 4) Employ a probabilistic filtering module, akin to concepts in NOPA, to identify and retain only high-confidence, consistent annotations for dataset expansion.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, significantly larger-scale webly-supervised scene parsing dataset, extending ADE20K. It will also provide a novel methodology for cross-modal alignment specifically tailored for dense prediction tasks, moving beyond image-level classification. The resulting dataset and models will push the state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of embedding alignment and modulation techniques from open-vocabulary segmentation and multimodal language model analysis to the specific problem of webly-supervised *dense* dataset creation, moving beyond noisy image-level labels to generate structured panoptic pseudo-labels.

proposals.title: Dual-Task Visual Annotation: A Human-in-the-Loop System for Open-Vocabulary Segmentation via Embedding Modulation and Uncertainty Feedback
proposals.motivation: Scaling high-quality, dense annotation for open-vocabulary visual understanding is a major bottleneck in computer vision. Current datasets are expensive to create and struggle to keep pace with the long-tail of real-world objects. A system that efficiently leverages model predictions to accelerate human annotation is crucial for developing more robust and generalizable models, particularly for embodied AI and home assistant applications that require understanding diverse, unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-task system, which uses an open-vocabulary segmentation model's predictions and its associated uncertainty to pre-annotate images, can significantly reduce human annotation time while maintaining or improving label quality through iterative human feedback.
proposals.method: We will build upon the Embedding Modulation framework from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation to generate initial segment proposals and class embeddings for novel images. The system will integrate an uncertainty estimation module, calculating entropy over embedding similarities, to flag low-confidence regions for prioritized human review. Inspired by neurally-guided assistance (NOPA), we will design an interactive interface where annotators correct proposals, with these corrections fed back as contrastive learning signals to fine-tune the model's modulation network, creating a closed-loop, improving system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, production-ready annotation framework that reduces the cost of creating dense segmentation datasets, 2) a new dataset collected with this system, demonstrating efficiency gains, and 3) insights into how human feedback can be formulated as a training signal for refining open-vocabulary visual representations.
proposals.novelty: While model-assisted annotation exists, our approach uniquely combines open-vocabulary segmentation (via embedding modulation) with explicit uncertainty-driven human-in-the-loop correction and a dual-task learning objective that treats human feedback as a direct training signal for the model's core visual-language representations.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Parsing via 2D-3D Embedding Transfer from Large-Scale Image Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current 3D scene understanding models are limited by small-scale, closed-vocabulary 3D datasets, hindering their ability to recognize the vast diversity of objects found in real-world environments. This project addresses this by leveraging the rich, open-vocabulary semantic annotations of large-scale 2D image datasets (like ADE20K) to supervise 3D models, bridging a critical data gap. Enabling robots and AI assistants to understand 3D scenes with the same semantic richness as 2D vision systems is essential for complex physical interaction and navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dense 2D semantic features from open-vocabulary image segmentation models can be effectively transferred and fused into 3D neural scene representations, enabling open-vocabulary querying and segmentation of 3D reconstructions without requiring corresponding 3D labels.
proposals.method: We will use a pipeline that first reconstructs 3D scenes (e.g., using NeRF or volumetric TSDF) from posed image sequences. For each training image (aligned with ADE20K-style labels), we will extract per-pixel CLIP or DINOv2 embeddings using a pre-trained open-vocabulary 2D segmentation model. These 2D feature maps will be projected into the 3D volume using known camera poses to create a spatially aligned 3D feature field. A 3D transformer or small MLP will be trained to denoise and consolidate these multi-view 2D features into a coherent 3D semantic scene representation, enabling query-based 3D segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a method for open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding that bypasses the need for expensive 3D annotations. It will contribute a new benchmark for 3D semantic parsing by linking existing 2D datasets to 3D reconstructions. The outcome is a step towards scalable 3D perception for embodied AI, directly building on Torralba's themes of visual objects and large-scale datasets.
proposals.novelty: While multi-view fusion is established, the novelty lies in distilling open-vocabulary 2D semantic knowledge—specifically from large-scale, richly annotated 2D datasets—into a unified 3D scene representation, creating a scalable path to 3D scene parsing that leverages the semantic breadth of 2D vision.

proposals.title: Multi-Label Scene Understanding via Compositional Embedding Modulation and Cross-Modal Supervision
proposals.motivation: Real-world scenes are compositional, containing multiple categorical elements (e.g., a 'kitchen' with a 'dining table' and 'office chair'). Pre-2026 scene classification models, often trained on single-label datasets like Places, fail to capture this complexity, limiting their utility for embodied AI and assistive robotics. This gap hinders models from reasoning about the nuanced, multi-faceted nature of physical environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained with explicit multi-label supervision and cross-modal alignment between visual scene features and open-vocabulary object embeddings can significantly outperform single-label baselines in compositional scene understanding, without requiring exhaustive per-scene annotation.
proposals.method: We will extend the Embedding Modulation technique from open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation to the scene level. A vision transformer backbone will extract dense features, which will be modulated by a set of candidate scene and object category embeddings derived from a pre-trained text encoder (e.g., CLIP). We will train the model using a combination of: 1) weak supervision from existing single-label datasets (Places365), 2) stronger, automatically derived multi-label signals from panoptic segmentation datasets (ADE20K, COCO), and 3) a contrastive loss to align the modulated scene representation with compositional text descriptions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust benchmark and model for multi-label scene categorization, moving beyond the artificial constraint of single-label classification. It will deliver a scene representation that explicitly encodes the presence and interplay of multiple object categories, directly benefiting downstream tasks like visual question answering for assistive agents and improved environment modeling for robotics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting embedding modulation—a segmentation tool—for holistic scene recognition and leveraging cross-modal supervision from object segmentation datasets to create rich, compositional scene labels without costly manual annotation, bridging Torralba's expertise in scene physics with recent multimodal methods.

proposals.title: SceneWalk: Probabilistic Scene Understanding via Continuous Semantic Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Current scene recognition models rely on discrete, mutually-exclusive category labels (e.g., 'kitchen', 'office'), which poorly reflect the continuous and overlapping nature of real-world visual scenes. This discrete assumption limits model robustness for ambiguous or hybrid environments (e.g., a kitchen-diner) and hinders the development of AI assistants that must navigate the nuanced semantics of real homes and workplaces. A representation that captures the fluidity of scene semantics is a critical step toward more human-like visual understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling scene categories as probability densities within a continuous, multimodal embedding space will yield a more accurate and expressive representation of real-world scenes than discrete classification, leading to improved performance on fine-grained and open-vocabulary scene understanding tasks.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, webly-supervised image-text datasets (e.g., LAION) to train a contrastive vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) as a base semantic encoder. Instead of training a classifier on its embeddings, we will model the distribution of scene concepts using a probabilistic mixture model (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models or Normalizing Flows) in the joint embedding space. For inference, a scene will be represented as a probability vector over all learned mixture components, enabling soft, overlapping assignments. We will evaluate on datasets like ADE20K and a newly curated benchmark of ambiguous indoor scenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel probabilistic framework for scene recognition that replaces hard classification with soft, continuous assignments, 2) new metrics and benchmarks for evaluating semantic ambiguity in scene understanding, and 3) improved foundational representations for downstream tasks in robotics and assistive AI where scene semantics are fluid.
proposals.novelty: While continuous embeddings exist, explicitly modeling scene categories as overlapping probability densities within a pre-trained multimodal space, and evaluating its impact on fine-grained and assistive reasoning tasks, is a novel direction beyond standard open-vocabulary classification.

proposals.title: Beyond Pixels: A Probabilistic Fusion Framework for Audio-Visual-Textual Scene Sufficiency
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art scene understanding models, including open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation, rely predominantly on visual features. However, human scene understanding is inherently multimodal, integrating sound, spatial context, and semantic knowledge. In robotics and assistive agents, this reliance on vision alone leads to failures in occluded, low-light, or ambiguous scenarios where non-visual cues are critical for robust performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a probabilistic, late-fusion architecture integrating synchronized audio, text (from object labels or scene captions), and coarse spatial layout (e.g., from depth or LiDAR) with visual features will yield a more sufficient scene representation, significantly improving robustness and accuracy in understanding tasks under challenging perceptual conditions.
proposals.method: We will extend a pretrained vision transformer (e.g., DINOv2) backbone with parallel encoders for audio spectrograms (using a CNN or audio spectrogram transformer) and textual scene descriptors (using a frozen CLIP text encoder). A probabilistic graphical model, inspired by the principles in NOPA, will perform neurally-guided online fusion of these multimodal embeddings. The fusion model will be trained on a curated subset of multimodal datasets like SoundSpaces or EPIC-KITCHENS, with a novel 'feature sufficiency' loss that penalizes over-reliance on any single modality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel benchmark and metric for evaluating multimodal scene sufficiency beyond standard vision-only metrics, 2) a publicly released probabilistic fusion model that demonstrates significant gains in robustness for downstream tasks like 3D object detection and assistive agent planning in visually degraded settings, and 3) an analysis of cross-modal attention, extending the study of 'multimodal neurons' to explicitly audio-visual-textual domains.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal fusion exists, our approach uniquely frames the problem as one of 'sufficiency,' explicitly testing when and which non-visual signals are necessary. The integration of a neurally-guided probabilistic model for online fusion, building on Torralba's prior work in NOPA and multimodal analysis, to dynamically weight sensor reliability is a novel architectural direction.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Scene Parsing with Compositional Embedding Modulation for Open-Vocabulary Sub-Scene Detection
proposals.motivation: Current scene understanding models excel at segmenting objects and classifying global scenes but struggle to identify and localize semantically meaningful sub-scenes, such as 'a person cooking at a stove' within a larger kitchen scene. This finer-grained understanding is crucial for embodied AI and home assistants to reason about activities, causal relationships, and provide context-aware assistance. Existing panoptic segmentation methods lack the compositional reasoning needed to group objects into functional units.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modulating the embeddings of detected objects and regions using compositional language prompts and learned scene grammar, a vision-language model can be guided to segment and recognize open-vocabulary sub-scenes within complex environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the Embedding Modulation framework from Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation to a hierarchical, multi-scale architecture. A transformer-based scene parser will first generate panoptic segments. Then, a neurally-guided probabilistic model, inspired by NOPA, will propose candidate sub-scene groupings based on object proximity and semantic compatibility. Finally, we will leverage the discovery of multimodal neurons in language models to generate and score descriptive prompts for these groupings, modulating visual embeddings for final sub-scene classification and refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new benchmark and model for open-vocabulary hierarchical scene parsing, explicitly addressing sub-scene detection. It will advance the field beyond object-centric perception towards functional, activity-centric scene understanding. The resulting method will enable more granular and context-sensitive reasoning for interactive AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of embedding modulation for compositional concepts with an online, probabilistic grouping mechanism, creating a unified framework that bridges discrete object detection and the continuous, open-ended interpretation of sub-scene semantics.

proposals.title: Scene Understanding Through Embodied Audio-Visual Priors: Closing the Human-Machine Gap on SUN
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in visual recognition, a significant performance gap persists between humans and machines on holistic scene understanding benchmarks like the SUN database. This gap stems from machines' lack of intuitive, real-world physical and functional priors that humans use to infer object properties, relationships, and scene semantics from limited visual data. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing AI agents that can interact robustly in real-world environments, such as the socially intelligent home assistants explored in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating implicit physical and functional knowledge, learned from multimodal audio-visual signals and structured through language, will allow computational models to approach human-level accuracy on SUN by improving reasoning about object affordances, material properties, and scene context.
proposals.method: We will develop a model that leverages a pre-trained vision-language backbone (e.g., CLIP) and augments it with two key modules. First, an Embedding Modulation network, inspired by open-vocabulary segmentation work, will condition visual features on physics- and function-oriented text prompts (e.g., "is rigid," "can contain liquid") derived from large language models. Second, we will introduce a neural audio-visual alignment module, informed by the discovery of multimodal neurons in language models, to distill embodied priors about material sound and physical interaction from large-scale video-with-audio datasets (e.g., AudioSet). This combined signal will refine object and region embeddings for a panoptic segmentation and scene classification head.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for the SUN benchmark, quantitatively closing the human-machine gap. It will contribute a novel architecture for injecting real-world physical and functional priors into vision systems and release a new set of audio-visual-physical attribute annotations for a subset of SUN categories to facilitate future research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used language or vision alone, our approach uniquely grounds functional and physical reasoning in concrete, embodied audio-visual signals, moving beyond textual correlations. The explicit use of non-visual sensory modalities to inform visual scene understanding for this benchmark is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Scaling Visual Concepts: A Data-Centric Study on the Impact of Category-Specific Image Expansion for Open-World Recognition
proposals.motivation: Large-scale datasets like ADE20K and COCO have driven progress in scene understanding, but many of their 899+ object and stuff categories remain data-poor, limiting model robustness and generalization. This is a critical bottleneck for open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation models, which must recognize a long tail of visual concepts in real-world environments. A systematic, category-level expansion addresses a core data scarcity problem in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically increasing the number of images per category, with an emphasis on long-tail and fine-grained concepts, will yield disproportionate improvements in model performance for open-vocabulary recognition and segmentation, beyond the gains from simply adding more total images uniformly.
proposals.method: We will develop a semi-automated, neurally-guided data collection pipeline. Building on embedding modulation techniques from open-vocabulary segmentation, we will use pretrained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) to mine large-scale web sources for category-relevant images, prioritizing visual diversity. For ambiguous or fine-grained categories, we will employ a human-in-the-loop system inspired by NOPA, using probabilistic assistance to guide annotators efficiently. The expanded dataset will be used to benchmark and retrain state-of-the-art models like MaskFormer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a significant, publicly released expansion of a major scene understanding benchmark (e.g., ADE20K), providing a resource to study data scaling laws per concept. We will deliver a framework for efficient, targeted dataset growth and quantify the performance impact on downstream tasks like panoptic segmentation and embodied AI perception, offering data-centric insights for building more socially intelligent assistants.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior dataset expansions that add new categories or more images globally, we focus on per-category scaling and its differential impact, guided by insights from multimodal neurons in language models to understand and target the semantic needs of under-represented visual concepts.

proposals.title: ActiveAL: Active Learning for Open-Vocabulary Visual Recognition with Multimodal Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Scaling visual recognition systems to the long tail of real-world objects requires massive, expensively annotated datasets. While open-vocabulary models trained on image-text pairs offer a path forward, their performance on fine-grained or rare categories is limited by a lack of targeted supervision. Efficiently identifying which images, from a vast unlabeled pool, would most improve model competency is a critical unsolved problem for building robust, real-world assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a multimodal acquisition function that quantifies uncertainty across visual, textual, and auditory modalities, an active learning system can prioritize unlabeled images whose annotation will most efficiently improve open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and object recognition performance.
proposals.method: We will develop an active learning framework built upon a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) and an open-vocabulary segmentation model (inspired by Embedding Modulation). The core is a novel acquisition function that combines: 1) visual feature density estimation to find outliers, 2) textual entropy from a masked language model over object descriptions, and 3) consistency between visual predictions and automatically generated soundscape embeddings (leveraging pre-2026 audio-visual datasets). Batches selected by this function will be annotated panoptically, then used to fine-tune the model in an iterative loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, multimodal active learning strategy for scaling visual recognition datasets. It will yield a publicly released benchmark for active learning in open-vocabulary segmentation and demonstrate significant annotation efficiency gains on datasets like ADE20K and Open Images.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior visual active learning, our method uniquely integrates uncertainty from complementary multimodal signals—text and synthetic sound—within an open-vocabulary setting, directly addressing the 'unknown unknown' problem of novel objects.

proposals.title: Scene Constellation Nets: Probabilistic Scene Layout Modeling with Neural Graphical Models
proposals.motivation: Modern scene understanding models, while powerful, often lack an explicit, compositional representation of scene layout and object relationships, which is crucial for robust reasoning and generation. Pre-2026, graphical models like constellation and part-based models provided such structured representations for objects but were not scaled to complex, open-vocabulary scenes. This project aims to bridge this gap, creating models that understand scenes not just as collections of detections but as structured spatial configurations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the probabilistic structure of graphical models, specifically constellation models, into a neural framework can yield a generative model of scene layouts that is more data-efficient, interpretable, and better at capturing long-tail spatial relationships than purely discriminative or implicit neural approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural probabilistic graphical model where detected object embeddings (from an open-vocabulary detector like OV-Seg) serve as observed nodes. A latent variable will represent the scene type, governing the parameters of a Gaussian Mixture Model that defines the expected spatial distribution (position, scale) and co-occurrence of object categories. Inference and learning will be performed via amortized variational inference, using a transformer encoder as the recognition network to approximate the posterior over the latent scene variable and layout parameters, conditioned on the set of detected object embeddings and their spatial coordinates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, generative neural graphical model for explicit scene layout representation, 2) a method for few-shot scene layout prediction and anomaly detection by evaluating scene likelihood, and 3) new metrics for evaluating the structural fidelity of generated or predicted scenes beyond standard detection scores.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the structured, probabilistic semantics of classic graphical models with the flexibility and open-vocabulary capacity of modern neural networks to model entire scene layouts, moving beyond modeling single objects or fixed label sets.

proposals.title: SceneFace: Metric Learning for Fine-Grained Scene Discrimination via Contrastive Embedding Modulation
proposals.motivation: Current open-vocabulary and panoptic segmentation models excel at broad category recognition but struggle with fine-grained distinctions between visually similar scenes (e.g., different types of kitchens, urban streets, or forest trails). This limits their utility in real-world applications like detailed environmental analysis, robotic navigation, and assistive technologies that require nuanced understanding of physical spaces. Pre-2026, face verification has successfully used metric learning to distinguish between highly similar identities, a capability not yet fully leveraged for complex, holistic scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting contrastive metric learning objectives, proven effective for face verification, to a vision-language embedding space will significantly improve a model's ability to learn discriminative features for fine-grained scene categories, surpassing standard classification or triplet losses used in general visual recognition.
proposals.method: We will build upon an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation backbone (e.g., with CLIP-based embeddings). A dedicated embedding modulation network will be trained using a combination of a contrastive loss (like SupCon or ArcFace) and a structured triplet loss, operating on scene-level embeddings. Training will utilize datasets with fine-grained scene annotations (e.g., Places365 fine-grained subsets, ADE20K) and synthetically generated hard negatives. The modulation network will learn to transform base visual embeddings into a metric space where minimal intra-class and maximal inter-class distances are enforced for similar scene types.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework for fine-grained scene recognition that directly optimizes for discriminative embedding distances. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating fine-grained scene discrimination and demonstrate improved performance on downstream tasks like detailed image retrieval and nuanced robotic scene understanding, advancing towards more socially intelligent assistants.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of face verification's rigorous metric learning paradigm to the holistic and compositional domain of scenes, and in the integration of this objective into a modulated, open-vocabulary embedding framework for joint recognition of objects and their fine-grained spatial contexts.

proposals.title: Crowdsourcing Cognitive Strategies: A Large-Scale Analysis of Human Scene Recognition for Grounding Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: While computer vision models achieve high accuracy on scene recognition benchmarks, their internal strategies often diverge from human cognition, leading to brittle and non-intuitive failures. Understanding the compositional, hierarchical, and multimodal cues humans use is crucial for building robust, interpretable AI for real-world applications like home assistants and autonomous systems. Pre-2026, large-scale, fine-grained analysis of human scene understanding strategies remains underexplored compared to aggregate performance metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that human scene recognition is a structured, multi-stage process that can be decomposed into a probabilistic graphical model, where initial object detection guides subsequent relational and contextual reasoning, and that this model can be distilled to improve the robustness and sample efficiency of neural networks.
proposals.method: We will design a novel crowdsourcing pipeline on platforms like Amazon Mechanical Turk, presenting participants with complex scenes from datasets like ADE20K or Hypersim. Using a combination of rapid image tagging, sequential question-answering (e.g., via a customized interface inspired by Visual Question Answering), and free-form verbal descriptions, we will collect dense, time-stamped annotation streams. We will analyze this data to infer a probabilistic graphical model of the recognition process. This human-derived model will then be used to modulate or guide a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., an Open-Vocabulary segmentation model or a CLIP-based architecture) via attention-based embedding modulation or auxiliary loss functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, large-scale dataset of human scene parsing strategies with temporal and reasoning annotations. 2) A computational, probabilistic model explicitly capturing the stages of human scene understanding. 3) A demonstration of how this cognitive model can be integrated into neural networks to improve their grounding, robustness on rare compositions, and alignment with human perception.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond benchmarking human *performance* to algorithmically modeling human *process*, and in directly using this inferred cognitive model as a supervisory signal for neural network training, bridging cognitive science and multimodal machine learning in a new way.

proposals.title: SUN-Mini: A Curated, Multi-Modal Benchmark for Rapid Prototyping of Scene Understanding Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale scene understanding datasets like SUN are foundational for training robust models but are computationally prohibitive for rapid prototyping and iterative research, especially in multimodal settings. This bottleneck slows down the development cycle for novel algorithms in open-vocabulary segmentation, embodied AI, and audio-visual learning. A small, expertly curated benchmark that preserves the statistical complexity and multi-label nature of real-world scenes is urgently needed to accelerate research.
proposals.hypothesis: A strategically sampled and annotated subset of the SUN database can serve as a statistically faithful proxy for the full dataset during the prototyping phase, enabling order-of-magnitude faster iteration without sacrificing the ability to predict final model performance on the full benchmark.
proposals.method: We will create SUN-Mini by applying a clustering-based selection algorithm on the semantic graph of SUN categories to ensure coverage of diverse scene configurations and object co-occurrences. Each selected image will be annotated with panoptic segments, aligning with Torralba's recent work on open-vocabulary segmentation. We will extend annotations to include dense, image-aligned semantic soundscape tags using a pre-trained audio-visual model like PVSS, creating a lightweight multi-modal testbed. The benchmark will be packaged with standardized evaluation protocols for segmentation, classification, and retrieval tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, lightweight benchmark for fast validation of scene understanding models, particularly those integrating vision, language, and sound. It will lower the barrier to entry for research on embodied agents and socially intelligent assistants by providing a common, rapid-testing platform. The curated multi-modal annotations will also enable new research into cross-modal alignment with minimal data overhead.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing small datasets, SUN-Mini is algorithmically designed to be a representative subset of a complex, large-scale scene database, and it uniquely incorporates aligned audio-visual-semantic annotations tailored for prototyping next-generation multi-modal models, a direct extension of Torralba's work on open-vocabulary segmentation and neural multimodal integration.

proposals.title: Web-Scale Scene Understanding: A Billion-Image Dataset with Physics-Aware Automatic Annotation
proposals.motivation: Current scene understanding datasets like ADE20K or COCO are limited to hundreds of thousands of images, constraining models' ability to learn the long-tail distribution of objects, materials, and their real-world physical interactions. A dataset orders of magnitude larger is crucial for training robust models for embodied AI and home assistants that require a dense, physical understanding of environments. This gap is particularly acute for tasks requiring reasoning about object permanence, affordances, and material properties derived from visual appearance.
proposals.hypothesis: By scaling scene image collection to billions of web-sourced examples and applying a multimodal, physics-informed automatic annotation pipeline, we can train models that significantly outperform existing systems in open-vocabulary segmentation and physical reasoning tasks. We hypothesize that scale, coupled with structured physical priors in tagging, will unlock emergent understanding of object functionality and scene dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a large-scale web crawler targeting diverse indoor and outdoor scenes. Each image will be processed through a pipeline combining: 1) A vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) for initial open-vocabulary object and material tagging, 2) A pretrained monocular depth estimator to infer scale and spatial layout, and 3) A novel 'physical consistency' module that uses lightweight physics simulators and commonsense knowledge bases (inspired by 'Multimodal Neurons' work) to validate and refine tags (e.g., ensuring 'glass' is tagged as 'fragile'). Embedding modulation techniques will be used to align noisy web tags with clean visual concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first billion-scale scene understanding dataset annotated with physical property tags (e.g., rigidity, mass, fragility). We will provide benchmarks demonstrating its impact on downstream tasks like neurally-guided robotic assistance and panoptic segmentation in novel environments. The dataset and annotation toolkit will become a foundational resource for research in embodied AI and computational scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While web-crawled datasets exist, none integrate physical reasoning directly into the automatic annotation process to create a 'physically-grounded' scene dataset. The novelty lies in using pretrained multimodal models not just for labeling, but to bootstrap a physics-aware verification system that improves annotation quality at scale, moving beyond static object labels to dynamic property inference.

proposals.title: SceneGen: Compositional Diffusion for Open-Vocabulary Scene Synthesis from Category Labels
proposals.motivation: Current text-to-image models excel at generating images from detailed captions but struggle with the inverse problem: synthesizing a coherent, realistic scene from a single high-level category label (e.g., 'kitchen', 'construction site'). This capability is crucial for data augmentation, simulation, and aiding AI systems in reasoning about the typical composition and physical layout of real-world environments. Grounding this in Torralba's work on scene understanding and object relationships, we aim to bridge the gap between categorical knowledge and visual synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative model conditioned on scene category labels can produce more realistic and compositionally coherent images than standard text-to-image models by explicitly leveraging learned, probabilistic priors over object co-occurrence, scale, and spatial layout inherent to the scene category.
proposals.method: We will develop a latent diffusion model where the conditioning signal is a modulated scene embedding. This embedding will be produced by an 'Object Prior Network' trained on large-scale panoptic segmentation datasets (e.g., ADE20K, Open Images), which learns the distribution of object identities, sizes, and relative positions for a given scene category. The modulation will be inspired by embedding modulation techniques from open-vocabulary segmentation, fusing this structured prior with a text encoder's output for the label. We will employ classifier-free guidance, but with guidance strength also conditioned on the confidence of the object prior.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new model for controllable scene generation from minimal input, a novel dataset of probabilistic scene-object priors derived from real-world annotations, and quantitative metrics for evaluating the physical plausibility and object composition fidelity of generated scenes. It extends open-vocabulary recognition work into the generative domain.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic text-to-image generation, our method explicitly models and injects the structural constraints of a scene category as a learned prior. It uniquely combines Torralba's expertise in scene physics and object datasets with recent advances in embedding modulation and diffusion models to solve the under-constrained inverse problem of scene synthesis from a label.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Scene Typology: Fine-Grained Visual Distinction of Architectural Sub-Categories
proposals.motivation: Current scene recognition models excel at broad categories (e.g., 'kitchen') but fail to capture the nuanced visual and functional distinctions between subtypes (e.g., galley, L-shaped, open-plan kitchens). This granularity is crucial for applications in robotics, augmented reality, and architectural analysis, where understanding the specific layout and affordances of a space is necessary for meaningful interaction. Pre-2026 datasets and models lack the fine-grained labels and representational capacity for this level of detail.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the multimodal alignment of vision-language models and augmenting them with a physics-aware, object-centric reasoning module, we can achieve open-vocabulary, fine-grained distinction of scene subtypes based on the spatial arrangement, functional grouping, and typical object compositions that define them.
proposals.method: We will extend the embedding modulation framework from open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation to condition on textual descriptions of scene typologies (e.g., 'a kitchen with counters along two parallel walls'). A key innovation is a 'Neural Physics' attention layer, inspired by Torralba's work on physical scene understanding, that explicitly models typical object relationships and support surfaces within a category. We will train and evaluate on a newly curated dataset, 'FineGrained-Places', built by annotating subtypes in existing datasets (Places365, ADE20K) and web images using a language-guided clustering pipeline.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel model capable of open-vocabulary, fine-grained scene typology classification. 2) The 'FineGrained-Places' benchmark dataset to spur research in detailed scene understanding. 3) Analysis demonstrating that injecting object-compositional and spatial-relation priors improves subtype discrimination over standard visual or textual embeddings alone.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond labeling *what* objects are present to understanding *how* they are typically arranged to define a functional scene subtype, combining open-vocabulary learning with structured, physics-inspired reasoning. The approach is distinct from standard scene classification or instance segmentation.

proposals.title: Adaptive Cluster Sampling for Correspondence: Saliency-Guided Keypoint Extraction
proposals.motivation: Dense feature extraction for correspondence tasks (e.g., SIFT, deep local features) is computationally expensive and often redundant in uniform image regions. This inefficiency limits real-time applications and scalability for large datasets or video. A method that intelligently allocates computational resources only to informative regions would enable faster and more efficient visual understanding systems.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive, two-stage sampling strategy—using a fast, low-fidelity saliency network to seed regions, followed by intensive local feature computation only around those seeds—can achieve comparable matching performance to dense extraction while significantly reducing computational cost.
proposals.method: We will implement a neural saliency predictor, inspired by Torralba's work on visual context and global scene gist, to produce a coarse saliency map from a low-resolution image. This map will guide an adaptive sampler that selects sparse seed points. Around each seed, a high-resolution patch will be extracted, and a full feature descriptor (e.g., SIFT, or a lightweight CNN like MobileNet) will be computed densely within a local cluster. The system will be trained and evaluated on established correspondence datasets like MegaDepth and ScanNet.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically and ecologically inspired framework for efficient visual correspondence. It will deliver a practical algorithm that reduces feature extraction cost for SLAM, 3D reconstruction, and panoramic stitching. We will also release a benchmark for evaluating adaptive feature sampling methods.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal adaptation of ecological adaptive cluster sampling to computer vision, creating a non-uniform, data-dependent feature extraction pipeline. This differs from traditional uniform sampling or learned sparse detectors by dynamically defining and densely sampling variable-sized clusters based on a rapid saliency cue.

proposals.title: SparseHashCorr: Real-Time Dense Correspondence via Adaptive Keypoint Hashing and Propagation
proposals.motivation: Real-time 3D reconstruction and SLAM require efficient, accurate image correspondence, but a fundamental trade-off persists. Dense methods like SIFT flow are computationally prohibitive, while sparse feature matchers (e.g., SuperPoint, LoFTR) sacrifice completeness. This gap limits the responsiveness and geometric fidelity of interactive vision systems, from home assistants to mobile robotics, which must understand detailed scene structure in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: A sparse set of adaptively selected, highly discriminative keypoints, indexed via locality-sensitive hashing (LSH), can be used not only for fast retrieval but also to guide and validate the reliable propagation of matches to create accurate dense correspondence fields, thereby breaking the speed-accuracy trade-off.
proposals.method: We will first develop an adaptive keypoint selector using a lightweight CNN or a criterion based on descriptor entropy and spatial dispersion to identify maximally informative points. Their descriptors will be indexed in an LSH forest for O(1) candidate retrieval. Crucially, the hash tables will serve as a spatial-graph structure; matched keypoints will seed a propagation algorithm that queries the hash space of neighboring pixels, using geometric consistency (via a weighted RANSAC) validated within the hash buckets to grow matches densely while rejecting outliers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel correspondence framework that achieves dense-matching accuracy with sparse-matching speed, directly enabling new real-time applications. It will also contribute a public benchmark for evaluating the efficiency-accuracy Pareto frontier in correspondence tasks, bridging computer vision and robotics.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the dual-use of the hash data structure for both instantaneous retrieval and as a guide for spatially-coherent match propagation, a synergy not explored in prior hashing-based retrieval (e.g., PatchMatch with hashing) or sparse-to-dense methods.

proposals.title: Sparse-to-Dense Correspondence via Open-Vocabulary Feature Modulation and Probabilistic Guidance
proposals.motivation: Dense SIFT extraction has been a cornerstone for pixel-level correspondence but is computationally expensive and semantically weak, limiting its use in real-time applications and complex scene understanding. Recent advances in open-vocabulary segmentation and large-scale vision-language models offer rich semantic priors, while neurally-guided probabilistic methods provide efficient search strategies. This project aims to leverage these modern tools to bypass the need for exhaustive local feature computation.
proposals.hypothesis: That robust, dense correspondence can be established by intelligently sampling a sparse set of keypoints guided by multimodal semantic and geometric priors, and then propagating these matches efficiently using a modulated feature field derived from a vision-language backbone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a 'Neurally-Guided Sampler' (inspired by NOPA) will select a sparse set of high-saliency keypoints using cues from pretrained multimodal neurons in models like CLIP, avoiding uniform dense grids. Second, an 'Embedding Modulation Network' (extending Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation work) will generate a dense feature field from a backbone like ViT, which is dynamically modulated by the sparse keypoint descriptors and their matches to propagate correspondence. This will be trained on existing datasets (e.g., COCO, ADE20K) with synthetic or geometric correspondence labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new paradigm for correspondence that is significantly more efficient than dense SIFT and more semantically aware. It will contribute a publicly available benchmark for evaluating sparse-to-dense matching and demonstrate applications in tasks like few-shot semantic part transfer and video object tracking without dense per-pixel feature extraction.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the integration of open-vocabulary semantic modulation for feature field generation with an active, neurally-guided sparse sampling policy, moving beyond handcrafted features or uniform sampling to achieve dense correspondence.

proposals.title: Manifold Deviation Mapping for Explicit Novelty Detection and Structured Correspondence in Visual Scenes
proposals.motivation: Open-world visual understanding systems, such as home assistants, must robustly handle novel objects and configurations not present in their training data. Current methods for open-vocabulary segmentation and correspondence often fail gracefully, producing nonsensical matches or overconfident errors when faced with true novelty. A system that explicitly quantifies and explains its uncertainty relative to a known data manifold is crucial for safe, interpretable deployment in real, unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the latent manifold of known visual correspondences, we can transform ambiguous nearest-neighbor searches into a well-posed analysis, where a query's deviation from this manifold provides a structured signal for novelty detection, plausible projection, and diagnostic feature attribution.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) or a normalizing flow on a large-scale dataset of image-object correspondences (e.g., a panoptic segmentation dataset) to learn the manifold of 'known' configurations. For a novel query, we compute its latent representation and its reconstruction error, using the evidence lower bound (ELBO) or likelihood to flag novelty. We then compute the gradient of the deviation with respect to the input features (inspired by multimodal neuron analysis) to identify responsible dimensions, and project the query to the nearest plausible point on the manifold via latent space optimization, outputting both the projection and an uncertainty estimate derived from the model's latent distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for novelty-aware visual correspondence that moves beyond binary detection to offer a structured, interpretable report. It will contribute a method for generating 'safe' plausible hypotheses in the face of novelty and a diagnostic tool for understanding model failures, advancing the development of socially intelligent and reliable assistive agents.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard out-of-distribution detectors that output a score, this method provides a structured decomposition of novelty and a principled projection to plausible in-distribution states. It integrates generative manifold modeling with explicit, gradient-based feature attribution for correspondence tasks, a combination not explored in prior open-vocabulary segmentation or multimodal assistance work.

proposals.title: FragmentMatch: Open-World Correspondence via Multi-Hypothesis Soft Assignment of Visual Primitives
proposals.motivation: Current vision systems for semantic correspondence, retrieval, and open-vocabulary recognition rely on nearest-neighbor matching in a global embedding space, which fails when a query object or scene is a novel combination of known parts or attributes with no single template in the database. This limits the ability of assistive agents and image understanding models to operate in truly open-world environments where novel compositions are frequent. A method that can robustly match queries to novel combinations of database features is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By decomposing visual entities into a set of primitives (e.g., object parts, material patches, spatial relations) and performing a multi-hypothesis soft assignment at this sub-component level, a global correspondence can be probabilistically synthesized that is more robust and flexible than whole-instance matching, enabling recognition of novel compositions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the fragment-based threading paradigm from protein structure prediction. First, we decompose images (or 3D scenes) into a graph of visual primitives using a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., DINO, CLIP) and an object relationship parser. For each query primitive, we compute soft similarity scores to a large database of primitives, generating a distribution of partial match hypotheses. These hypotheses are integrated via a neural graph-matching network, inspired by recent work on multimodal neurons in transformers, which performs a constrained optimization to find a globally consistent assignment, maximizing joint probability without requiring a single best whole-template match.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel correspondence framework that moves beyond global embedding lookup, enabling open-world recognition of novel object/scene compositions. It will provide a new benchmark and dataset for evaluating compositional generalization in visual correspondence tasks. The method will directly impact areas like open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and socially intelligent assistants by improving their ability to understand unseen environments.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard nearest-neighbor assumption by replacing it with a fragment-based, multi-hypothesis alignment scheme, a concept not explored in mainstream computer vision but proven in computational biology. This differs from prior metric learning or attention-based matching by explicitly modeling and synthesizing from a distribution of weak, partial matches.

proposals.title: Compositional Correspondence via Latent Set Matching for Novel Visual Queries
proposals.motivation: Nearest neighbor search in embedding spaces is foundational for tasks like object retrieval and image matching, but fails when a query (e.g., a novel object assemblage or scene) lacks a single, close match in the database. This limits the robustness of vision systems in open-world settings, where novel compositions are common. Addressing this gap is crucial for building assistive agents that can understand and act upon complex, never-before-seen user requests.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel visual query can be matched to a database by decomposing it into constituent, recognizable elements and finding a corresponding set of database entries whose joint representation aligns with the query, even if no single entry is a close match.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based model that takes a query image and a database of image-caption pairs. It will decompose the query into a set of latent object and attribute embeddings using a vision-language encoder (e.g., CLIP). A cross-attention module will then perform iterative, neurally-guided matching between this query set and database embeddings, optimizing for a probabilistic set alignment score rather than a single nearest neighbor. This draws on recent work in multimodal neurons and embedding modulation for open-vocabulary recognition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for visual correspondence that moves beyond one-to-one nearest neighbor retrieval to one-to-set matching. It will yield a benchmark and method for handling novel compositional queries, directly impacting progress in open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation and socially intelligent assistants that must interpret ambiguous or novel commands.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior metric learning or retrieval-augmented methods that assume the query exists in the database distribution, we explicitly model the case where the match is a novel combination of known parts, inverting the core assumption of nearest neighbor search.

proposals.title: CLAC: Conditionally Learned Adaptive Cost for Robust Visual Descriptor Matching
proposals.motivation: Robust local feature matching under severe appearance changes (e.g., day-night, weather) remains a core challenge for 3D reconstruction and visual SLAM. Fixed-norm distances like L2 are brittle to such non-linear perturbations, while hand-crafted robust losses are not adaptive to specific image-pair contexts. This limits the reliability of vision systems in dynamic, real-world environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A matching cost function, whose parameters are dynamically predicted by a neural network conditioned on global image-pair context, can achieve superior robustness and specificity compared to a fixed metric, leading to more accurate and reliable descriptor matching.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight conditioning network that takes a global context vector (from a frozen foundation model like DINOv2 or CLIP) and the local descriptor difference vector as input. This network outputs parameters for a per-dimension weighting function or a compact invertible flow to non-linearly warp the difference space. The system is trained end-to-end on datasets like MegaDepth or ScanNet with a robust contrastive loss, using ground-truth correspondences from SfM. The adaptive cost is integrated into a differentiable matcher for joint optimization.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, context-aware matching cost that generalizes beyond fixed norms. 2) Demonstrated performance gains on challenging matching benchmarks with large appearance variation. 3) An analysis of what visual and geometric cues the conditioning network leverages to modulate matching sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior learned metrics or attention mechanisms, our method explicitly conditions the cost function's *shape* on global image-pair attributes, enabling a single model to adaptively switch between being tolerant to photometric changes and discriminative for viewpoint changes.

proposals.title: Weakly-Supervised Learning of Neural Matching Costs for Domain-Adaptive Geometric Vision
proposals.motivation: Geometric vision tasks like structure-from-motion and visual localization rely on robust local feature matching, but standard matching costs (e.g., L2 distance) are suboptimal for cross-domain or cross-sensor scenarios (e.g., day-night, RGB-depth). Current deep learning alternatives require dense, pixel-level correspondence labels, which are expensive and limit scalability. A method to learn powerful matching priors from readily available weak supervision would significantly enhance the robustness and applicability of 3D vision systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network can be trained to predict a non-linear, domain-adaptive matching cost for local descriptors using only weak supervisory signals, and this learned cost function will generate more geometrically consistent matches than fixed metrics, especially under domain shifts.
proposals.method: We propose a self-supervised loop: 1) Extract initial keypoints and descriptors (SIFT or pretrained CNN). 2) Use a lightweight, permutation-invariant neural network (e.g., a small MLP or transformer) to predict a matchability score for descriptor pairs, forming a putative match set. 3) Fit a geometric model (e.g., fundamental matrix via RANSAC) to these matches. 4) Use the inlier/outlier classification from the fitted model as a supervisory signal to train the neural cost function via a contrastive loss, encouraging scores that lead to high-consensus geometries. The network architecture will draw inspiration from recent work on multimodal neurons and embedding modulation to handle heterogeneous feature types.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for learning matching functions without dense ground truth, lowering the annotation barrier for geometric vision. It will produce new, publicly released neural cost modules that improve matching robustness across domains, validated on datasets like MegaDepth and ScanNet. The method bridges weakly-supervised learning with traditional geometric model fitting.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that learns descriptors with strong supervision or uses fixed metrics, we directly learn the *matching cost function* itself through a differentiable optimization loop closed by geometric consensus, a form of neural guidance for matching akin to concepts in NOPA but applied to low-level vision.

proposals.title: Robust Descriptor Matching via Learned Cost Functions and Multimodal Supervision
proposals.motivation: Local feature matching, a cornerstone of 3D reconstruction and visual SLAM, traditionally relies on simple L1/L2 distances between hand-crafted descriptors like SIFT. In modern computer vision, learned descriptors from deep networks are ubiquitous, yet the matching cost function has not co-evolved. The L1-norm is a suboptimal similarity measure for these high-dimensional, structured embeddings, limiting matching robustness, especially under severe appearance changes. This work addresses this core, underexplored component of the matching pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the fixed L1-norm with a learned, context-aware matching cost function, trained with multimodal supervisory signals, will yield significant improvements in matching robustness and downstream task performance compared to standard distance metrics.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight neural matching module that takes a pair of deep visual descriptors (e.g., from a foundation model like DINOv2 or a tuned network) as input and outputs a robust similarity score. Inspired by Torralba's work on multimodal neurons and embedding modulation, we will train this module using a composite loss. This loss will combine contrastive learning on existing visual datasets (e.g., ImageNet, COCO) with cross-modal supervision from text (using CLIP-style alignment) or audio (leveraging synchronized video-audio data), encouraging the cost function to learn semantically meaningful invariances. The module will be evaluated within a full matching pipeline on tasks like long-term visual localization and open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, trainable matching cost that can be plugged into existing descriptor architectures, 2) a framework for using multimodal data to supervise a core geometric vision task, and 3) demonstrated improvements on challenging, real-world matching benchmarks involving significant viewpoint and appearance variation.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on improving the descriptors themselves, we directly challenge and replace the subsequent, fixed matching operation. The use of non-visual modalities (language, sound) to regularize a geometric matching cost is a novel direction unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: HASM: Hierarchical Attention for Structured Many-to-One Pixel Correspondences in Dynamic Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision models, including those for panoptic segmentation and scene flow, struggle with the inherent many-to-one pixel correspondences in dynamic scenes, such as when multiple source object parts map to a single occluded target pixel. Naive approaches lead to ambiguous, non-physical mappings that degrade performance in robotics and AR/VR applications. A structured, physically-grounded model is needed to resolve these ambiguities while maintaining differentiability for end-to-end learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling pixel correspondences within a spatiotemporal object-part hierarchy and using a learned attention mechanism to resolve occlusions, we can create a deterministic, differentiable mapping that significantly improves the accuracy and physical consistency of scene reconstruction and segmentation in videos.
proposals.method: We will design a Hierarchical Attention Structured Mapping (HASM) network. It builds on a pretrained vision-language backbone (e.g., CLIP) for open-vocabulary object and part segmentation. A multi-layer transformer then computes attention scores between source and target pixels at multiple hierarchical levels (pixel, part, object). A gating network, informed by estimated depth and motion, produces a final spatiotemporally coherent mask that resolves many-to-one contributions deterministically. Training will use a combination of reconstruction loss, a novel hierarchical consistency loss enforcing agreement across levels, and adversarial losses on real image datasets to ensure realism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for handling occlusions and segmentation changes in video understanding tasks, directly improving models for scene flow, video panoptic segmentation, and neural rendering. It will contribute a new benchmark and dataset annotations focusing on many-to-one correspondence challenges.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard attention or optical flow, this method explicitly structures the many-to-one problem via a learnable object-part hierarchy and a deterministic resolution mechanism, moving beyond simple weighted averaging to enforce physical plausibility.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Pixel Binding for Robust Video Segmentation and Tracking
proposals.motivation: Current video segmentation and object tracking models rely on strict one-to-one pixel correspondences across frames. This assumption breaks under complex real-world conditions like severe occlusions, object splitting/merging, and rapid appearance changes, leading to identity switches and segmentation failures. A more flexible correspondence model, inspired by how biological structures are reconstructed from data, is needed for robust long-term scene understanding in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling pixel correspondences across video frames as soft, many-to-one probabilistic bindings—analogous to histological slice stacking—a system can maintain more consistent object identity and segmentation through challenging visual events, outperforming rigid matching schemes.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based architecture that processes a reference frame and a current frame. Instead of a standard attention mechanism that encourages one-to-one matching, we will design a 'binding layer' that allows a query pixel in the current frame to attend to multiple candidate pixels in the reference frame, outputting a distribution over potential correspondences. This distribution will be regularized by higher-level constraints from an object-centric memory module (inspired by recent slot-attention works) that enforces temporal coherence of object properties. The model will be trained end-to-end on video panoptic segmentation datasets like VIPSeg and KITTI-STEP.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel probabilistic framework for pixel-level correspondence that explicitly handles ambiguity. It will provide a new architectural component for video understanding models and deliver improved quantitative results on long-term video segmentation and multi-object tracking benchmarks under occlusion.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit shift from deterministic to probabilistic, many-to-one pixel binding for temporal vision tasks, a concept directly adapted from 3D histological reconstruction, which is unexplored in mainstream computer vision. This contrasts with existing optical flow or attention mechanisms that implicitly enforce uniqueness.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Many-to-One Pixel Embeddings for Robust Video Object Correspondence Under Occlusion
proposals.motivation: Current pixel-level correspondence models, such as those in video object segmentation or tracking, enforce strict one-to-one matching. This fails under real-world dynamics like occlusions, object splitting, or merging, where a pixel's visual signature can map to multiple future states or originate from multiple past states. This limitation reduces the robustness of systems for long-term understanding in assistive robotics and interactive video editing, which are active areas in Torralba's recent work on social home assistants and open-vocabulary segmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling pixel correspondences as a probabilistic, many-to-one mapping, rather than a deterministic one-to-one function, will significantly improve the temporal coherence and robustness of object-level representations in videos with complex occlusions and segmentation changes.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based spatial model (e.g., a DINO or SAM-inspired architecture) by replacing its standard attention-based matching with a learned probabilistic association layer. This layer will output a distribution over potential corresponding regions in adjacent frames for each pixel or superpixel. We will train this model on video datasets like YouTube-VIS or a synthesized dataset with heavy occlusions, using a loss that combines contrastive embedding learning for appearance with a prior encouraging spatial and temporal continuity, inspired by recent work on neural guidance for probabilistic assistance (NOPA).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new formalization and a practical model for many-to-one pixel correspondence, directly addressing a core failure mode in video understanding. It will provide more stable long-term object identities for applications in robotic perception and video editing. The resulting probabilistic embeddings could also serve as a foundational layer for improving open-vocabulary segmentation in dynamic scenes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses optical flow or object tracking with heuristic occlusion handling, we propose to learn a *fundamentally many-to-one* correspondence model end-to-end. This departs from the standard paradigm of enforcing bijectivity and integrates probabilistic reasoning directly into the pixel embedding space, a concept underexplored in pre-2026 vision models.

proposals.title: Learning to Match: Differentiable Optimization for SIFT Flow Parameter Learning
proposals.motivation: SIFT Flow is a seminal algorithm for dense correspondence estimation between images, crucial for scene alignment, object tracking, and 3D reconstruction. However, its performance hinges on manually tuned parameters in its energy function, which balance data terms, smoothness, and occlusion. This heuristic tuning limits robustness across diverse real-world scenes and visual domains, creating a bottleneck for applications requiring high-fidelity matching.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by making the SIFT flow optimization process fully differentiable, its core energy function parameters can be learned directly from data via backpropagation, leading to more accurate and generalizable flow fields than those obtained with hand-crafted settings.
proposals.method: We will implement SIFT Flow as a differentiable optimization layer by unrolling its iterative coarse-to-fine minimization procedure. Using a custom computational graph, we will ensure gradients flow through the SIFT descriptor matching costs, the spatial smoothness term, and the occlusion label assignment. We will then learn the weight parameters of this energy function by minimizing a supervised loss (e.g., an endpoint error) between predicted flows and ground-truth correspondences on a large-scale dataset like MegaDepth or Sintel, using standard stochastic gradient descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully differentiable, learnable version of the classic SIFT Flow algorithm, moving parameter tuning from a heuristic to a data-driven process. It will demonstrate how optimization layers can be applied to foundational computer vision problems, potentially improving matching accuracy and offering a new paradigm for adapting traditional algorithms to specific visual domains.
proposals.novelty: While differentiable optimization has been applied to new neural architectures and physics simulators, applying this principle to learn the internal parameters of a well-established, non-neural correspondence algorithm like SIFT Flow is novel. It bridges a classic, interpretable method with modern learning frameworks.

proposals.title: Learning to Flow: Data-Driven Optimization of SIFT Flow Parameters via Differentiable Matching
proposals.motivation: SIFT flow remains a foundational, physically-inspired algorithm for dense correspondence between real-world images, crucial for object alignment, scene understanding, and video analysis. However, its performance is highly sensitive to hand-tuned parameters (e.g., matching weights, smoothness constraints), which are suboptimal for specific visual domains or tasks. In the pre-2026 era of learned visual features and end-to-end optimization, this reliance on manual tuning limits its adaptability and integration with modern neural pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core parameters governing the SIFT flow energy function can be effectively learned from data using a differentiable approximation of its matching process, leading to more accurate and robust correspondences tailored to specific visual domains (e.g., indoor scenes, articulated objects) than the generic hand-crafted settings.
proposals.method: We will construct a fully differentiable SIFT flow layer. First, we will implement a differentiable version of the SIFT descriptor matching cost volume. This layer will be integrated into a neural network that takes an image pair as input and outputs the optimal flow field. The network's learnable parameters will directly modulate the classical SIFT flow energy terms (data cost, smoothness, small displacement). We will train this model on existing dense correspondence datasets (e.g., MPI-Sintel, KITTI) using a supervised loss on ground-truth flow, leveraging Torralba's expertise in large-scale visual datasets. The optimization will use standard backpropagation through the unrolled iterations of the differentiable SIFT flow solver.
proposals.contribution: This work will bridge classical computer vision techniques and modern learning frameworks, producing a more powerful and adaptable version of a seminal algorithm. It will provide a principled method to specialize correspondence algorithms for specific visual domains without manual parameter search. The resulting 'learned SIFT flow' module can serve as a versatile, interpretable component in larger neural systems for panoptic segmentation, 3D reconstruction, or multimodal alignment.
proposals.novelty: While learned features have replaced SIFT in many contexts, no prior work has directly learned to optimize the *internal parameters* of the SIFT flow algorithm's energy minimization process in an end-to-end manner. This approach preserves the algorithm's interpretable, physics-like structure while making it data-adaptive, contrasting with purely black-box optical flow networks.

proposals.title: Phylo-Video: Hierarchical Video Alignment via Tree Reconciliation
proposals.motivation: Robustly aligning video sequences is crucial for tasks like action recognition, video retrieval, and temporal correspondence learning. Current methods, such as dense optical flow or SIFT flow, operate at a low-level pixel or feature scale, struggling with significant temporal distortions, viewpoint changes, or event insertions/deletions. A high-level, event-aware alignment framework is needed to match the semantic structure of videos.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling video sequences as hierarchical temporal trees of scenes and actions, and aligning them using algorithms adapted from phylogenetic tree reconciliation, will yield a more semantically meaningful and robust correspondence than low-level feature matching, especially for videos with non-linear temporal edits or narrative variations.
proposals.method: We will first parse videos into hierarchical trees using a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) and temporal segmentation to define nodes as coherent actions or scenes, with edges representing temporal succession and containment. We will then adapt the Duplication-Transfer-Loss (DTL) reconciliation algorithm from computational biology. In our adaptation, 'gene tree' and 'species tree' correspond to two video trees, and evolutionary events (duplication, transfer, loss) are mapped to video editing operations (repetition, scene insertion, deletion). A cost model will be learned to optimize the alignment, minimizing a total edit distance between tree structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, interpretable framework for video alignment that operates at the level of semantic events rather than pixels. It will provide a complementary tool to existing computer vision pipelines, enabling better performance in video synchronization and retrieval tasks where narrative structure is key.
