We will release a new benchmark dataset of semantically related but temporally distorted video pairs to evaluate high-level alignment.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the cross-disciplinary adaptation of phylogenetic reconciliation, a well-established bioinformatics technique, to the computer vision problem of video alignment. This provides an explicit probabilistic model for high-level temporal edits, which is distinct from and complementary to prevailing deep learning or dense correspondence approaches.

proposals.title: Semantically-Guided SIFT Flow: Fusing Object and Scene Priors for Robust Dense Correspondence
proposals.motivation: Classical SIFT flow provides dense, low-level correspondences but struggles with large appearance changes, repetitive textures, and occlusions, limiting its application in dynamic real-world scenes. By 2025, vision models offer rich semantic understanding through open-vocabulary segmentation and scene classification. This project aims to bridge this gap, creating a robust matching framework that leverages high-level semantics to guide and disambiguate low-level flow, directly addressing a core limitation in pre-deep-flow correspondence methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conditioning SIFT flow estimation on explicit, pixel-wise semantic labels (object categories) and global scene context will significantly improve matching accuracy in challenging, real-world image pairs by providing strong topological and contextual priors.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural architecture. First, an open-vocabulary segmentation model (e.g., based on CLIP) and a scene classifier will extract pixel-wise object masks and a global scene vector for each input image. Second, we will design a modulation network that takes these semantic maps and the SIFT descriptors as input. This network will learn to weight and gate candidate flow fields from a SIFT flow pyramid, suppressing matches that violate semantic boundaries (e.g., a 'car' descriptor flowing to a 'building' region) and reinforcing flows consistent with the scene category. The training will use a combination of photometric loss and a novel semantic consistency loss on datasets with dense correspondence annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, semantically-aware dense correspondence algorithm that is more robust than classical SIFT flow. It will demonstrate a principled method for fusing low-level visual features with high-level semantic predictions from modern vision-language models. The resulting technique could significantly benefit applications in image editing, 3D reconstruction, and robotic perception where understanding object persistence is key.
proposals.novelty: While neural networks have replaced SIFT flow for many tasks, the explicit, modular integration of open-vocabulary semantic segmentation as a conditioning signal for classical flow estimation is novel. This approach differs from end-to-end optical flow networks by offering interpretable control and leveraging the complementary strengths of handcrafted features and semantic understanding.

proposals.title: Learning to Cut: Differentiable Energy Functions for Adaptive Graph-Cut Inference in Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Traditional graph-cut methods for pixel-level scene understanding (e.g., panoptic segmentation) rely on hand-crafted, fixed energy models. These models struggle to capture the complex, long-range contextual relationships present in real-world scenes, limiting their performance on modern, diverse datasets. A data-driven approach to learning the energy function itself is needed to overcome this rigidity and unlock the full potential of energy-based inference for visual tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network can learn to predict spatially-variant, higher-order clique potentials that, when used within a differentiable relaxation of the alpha-expansion algorithm, will yield a more adaptive and powerful inference model than fixed-energy graph cuts, particularly for tasks with intricate object dependencies.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-encoder neural network: a CNN backbone for pixel-wise unary potentials and a transformer module to model long-range interactions and predict spatially-variant, higher-order clique potentials (e.g., P^n Potts models). These potentials will parameterize a differentiable, continuous relaxation of the graph-cut optimization, building on the work of Tang et al. (2013) for backpropagation through alpha-expansion. The entire system—feature encoder and relaxed solver—will be trained end-to-end using task-specific loss (e.g., segmentation error) on large-scale visual datasets like ADE20K or COCO.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a fully learnable, end-to-end trainable graph-cut framework that integrates modern neural feature learning with robust discrete optimization. It will advance the field of structured prediction by demonstrating how to backpropagate through complex inference algorithms to learn task-optimal energy structures. The resulting model is expected to set a new state-of-the-art for panoptic segmentation in open-vocabulary or challenging real-world settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has made parts of energy minimization differentiable, our proposal uniquely combines the learning of *spatially-variant higher-order cliques* with a differentiable graph-cut solver, creating a fully adaptive energy model learned directly from data, rather than simply tuning parameters of a fixed form.

proposals.title: Annealing Graph Cuts: A Hybrid Ising Model Framework for Robust Multi-Label Energy Minimization
proposals.motivation: Graph cuts are a cornerstone for energy minimization in computer vision tasks like segmentation and stereo, but they can converge to poor local minima for complex, non-convex energies. This is analogous to the challenge of finding ground states in disordered spin systems in statistical physics. Adapting the principled, global search strategies from physics, specifically simulated annealing for the Ising model, offers a promising path to more robust solutions for vision problems where traditional move-making algorithms or continuous relaxations struggle.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a multi-label vision problem as a spin system and integrating a probabilistic annealing schedule within the graph cut expansion move algorithm, we can achieve superior energy minimization, escaping local minima that trap standard graph cut methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid algorithm where each expansion move is treated as a proposed spin flip in a Potts-like model. A Metropolis-Hastings acceptance criterion, governed by a decreasing temperature schedule, will stochastically accept or reject graph cut proposals that increase the global energy. The core will be an augmented alpha-expansion algorithm, where the move acceptance is modulated by a temperature parameter T, initially high to explore the state space and gradually lowered. We will implement this using efficient max-flow computations and test it on established vision benchmarks with challenging, non-submodular energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, physically-grounded optimization tool for the vision community, demonstrably improving results on difficult labeling problems. It will also create a novel bridge between statistical physics methodologies and practical computer vision, potentially inspiring further cross-disciplinary adaptations.
proposals.novelty: While simulated annealing is known, its tight integration *within* the graph cut expansion move framework—treating each max-flow proposal as a Monte Carlo step—is novel. This differs from prior applications of annealing to vision, which typically operate on pixel-level perturbations rather than structured, large-scale moves derived from graph cuts.

proposals.title: Graph-Cut Refinement for Open-Vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Open-vocabulary segmentation models, like those in Torralba's recent work, leverage vision-language embeddings to generalize to novel categories. However, their pixel-level predictions often lack spatial coherence and physical plausibility, especially for complex, real-world scenes with occlusions and fine object boundaries. Integrating classical, physics-inspired energy minimization can enforce spatial consistency that pure neural network outputs may miss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the output of an open-vocabulary segmentation network as a conditional random field (CRF) and optimizing it with efficient graph-cut algorithms will significantly improve the spatial coherence, boundary accuracy, and physical realism of panoptic segmentation masks without sacrificing open-vocabulary recognition capability.
proposals.method: We will build upon a state-of-the-art open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation network (e.g., with embedding modulation) to generate initial class and instance logits. These will form the unary potentials of a densely connected CRF. Pairwise potentials will combine standard bilateral (color, position) terms with novel terms derived from predicted depth (for occlusion reasoning) and object affinity from the network's intermediate features. We will employ a fast, iterative alpha-expansion graph-cut algorithm for energy minimization, ensuring the solution remains computationally tractable for high-resolution images.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, unified framework that marries the semantic richness of large-scale vision-language models with the spatial precision of classical graph-based optimization. We expect to demonstrate measurable gains in segmentation metrics (PQ, mIoU) on challenging datasets like ADE20K and COCO, particularly for boundary precision and small object segmentation. The method will provide a principled way to inject physical and spatial constraints into open-vocabulary systems.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are known for segmentation, their application to refine the nascent task of *open-vocabulary* panoptic segmentation is novel. The key innovation is designing pairwise potentials that leverage multimodal (visual, language, depth) cues from the foundation model itself, creating a tightly coupled, end-to-end trainable refinement module rather than a separate post-processing step.

proposals.title: Online Self-Supervised Adaptation of Visual Features for Robust Cross-Domain Matching
proposals.motivation: Learned visual features from large datasets (e.g., ImageNet) often degrade under domain shift, limiting the robustness of vision systems in novel environments. While handcrafted features are more consistent, they lack semantic power. Enabling deep features to adapt online without labeled data is crucial for deployable systems in robotics and augmented reality, where conditions are unpredictable.
proposals.hypothesis: That a lightweight, attached adaptation module can be continuously refined via geometric self-supervision from unlabeled image streams, aligning a pre-trained feature extractor to a novel domain in real-time and significantly improving matching robustness without catastrophic forgetting of generic knowledge.
proposals.method: We attach a small, convolutional modulation network to a frozen, pre-trained backbone (e.g., ResNet). During deployment, we capture unlabeled image pairs from a moving camera. Using pre-2026 methods like SfM or optical flow, we establish dense geometric correspondences as a self-supervision signal. A contrastive loss (e.g., using a pre-2026 memory bank or a triplet loss) applied to these correspondences fine-tunes only the adapter module online via continual learning techniques like experience replay to mitigate drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical system for online domain-adaptive feature learning, bridging the gap between the high performance of learned features and the cross-domain robustness of handcrafted ones. It will provide a new benchmark for visual feature adaptation in continuous, unlabeled streams and offer insights into the plasticity of pre-trained networks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior offline domain adaptation or test-time adaptation methods, our approach performs continuous, on-the-fly refinement specifically for geometric matching tasks using only the robot's or agent's egocentric video stream, merging principles from lifelong learning and self-supervised 3D vision.

proposals.title: DenseMatchNet: Learning Universal Visual Descriptors from Large-Scale Correspondence
proposals.motivation: Handcrafted local feature descriptors like SIFT have been foundational for geometric computer vision tasks, but their heuristic design limits their robustness to modern challenges like extreme viewpoint changes, non-Lambertian surfaces, and appearance shifts. The success of learned, task-specific representations in other domains, such as molecular fingerprints for drug discovery, suggests a data-driven alternative could significantly advance the field. A learned universal descriptor, trained on massive and diverse visual correspondence data, could provide a new, more powerful primitive for 3D reconstruction, image stitching, and visual localization.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep neural network, trained end-to-end on a large-scale dataset of image patch correspondences with a metric learning objective, can produce dense feature descriptors that significantly outperform traditional engineered features in matching accuracy, robustness, and generalization across diverse visual domains.
proposals.method: We will train a Vision Transformer (ViT) or a fully convolutional network on a curated dataset of image pairs with dense geometric correspondences (e.g., from MegaDepth, ScanNet, or internet-scale stereo data). The model will process an image patch and output a high-dimensional, L2-normalized descriptor. Training will use a contrastive loss, such as a multi-pair variant of the triplet loss, to pull descriptors of matching patches together in embedding space while pushing apart non-matching ones. We will explicitly engineer invariances through data augmentation simulating photometric and mild geometric transformations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art learned local feature descriptor, publicly released as a foundational model. It will provide a comprehensive benchmark against SIFT, ORB, and recent learned features like L2-Net on standard tasks like homography estimation and visual localization. The project will also contribute a large-scale, clean dataset for training general-purpose visual correspondence models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored learned descriptors, they are often limited in scale or tied to specific detectors. Our approach focuses on creating a *dense*, *universal* descriptor model—inspired by the paradigm shift from handcrafted to learned representations in computational chemistry—trained on an unprecedented scale of visual correspondence data, decoupling it from any specific feature detector for maximum flexibility.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Feature Matching: Learning Dense, Discriminative Descriptors for Panoptic-Level Correspondence
proposals.motivation: Handcrafted features like SIFT are brittle under real-world appearance changes and lack semantic grounding, limiting their use in dynamic, open-ended environments. Modern convolutional networks learn powerful, semantically-aware visual features, but these are typically optimized for classification, not for establishing precise, pixel-level correspondences across images. Bridging this gap is crucial for advancing tasks like long-term robotic manipulation, 3D scene understanding, and video object tracking, where matching must be robust to lighting, viewpoint, and occlusion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a convolutional network can be trained to output dense, view-invariant feature descriptors that are more robust and semantically meaningful than SIFT, and that these descriptors can be leveraged within an open-vocabulary framework to establish correspondences for both 'stuff' and 'thing' categories across images.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) or a panoptic segmentation network to initialize semantically rich features. We will design a contrastive learning objective on a large-scale video dataset (e.g., YT-VIS) or a synthetic multi-view dataset, encouraging features from the same physical point across different views to be similar while pushing apart features from different points. The architecture will be a lightweight convolutional head on a frozen or fine-tuned backbone, outputting a dense descriptor field. Matching will be performed via efficient nearest-neighbor search in this learned embedding space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new learned feature matching module that is directly compatible with modern perception systems, enabling more reliable geometric and semantic correspondence. It will provide a quantitative benchmark comparing learned descriptors to SIFT on established matching datasets, and demonstrate qualitative improvements in a downstream application such as neural-guided robotic assistance or open-vocabulary video object segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While learned local features exist, our approach uniquely integrates the open-vocabulary and panoptic understanding from Torralba's recent work, aiming for descriptors that are geometrically precise *and* semantically aware, moving beyond matching low-level interest points to matching object parts and regions across diverse contexts.

proposals.title: Multi-Model Geometric Verification with Adaptive Local Priors for Robust Visual Correspondence
proposals.motivation: Robust geometric verification is fundamental for 3D reconstruction, SLAM, and image matching, but standard RANSAC-based methods assume a single dominant motion. This fails in complex, multi-structure scenes common in robotics and AR, where valid matches on independent objects or planes are incorrectly pruned as outliers. This over-pruning limits the completeness and accuracy of downstream geometric reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling a scene as a collection of multiple geometric models and using local consistency priors to adapt inlier thresholds, we can significantly improve the recall and precision of correspondence filtering in complex environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that first applies a multi-model fitting algorithm (e.g., PEARL or RPA) to cluster putative SIFT or deep feature matches into groups corresponding to different homographies or fundamental matrices. For each cluster, we will compute a spatially and descriptively adaptive inlier threshold based on local match density and feature reliability scores, inspired by learned matching confidence measures. The final verification will enforce consistency within each locally refined model, preserving valid matches that global RANSAC would discard.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a more robust geometric verification module that can handle scenes with multiple independent motions, directly improving tasks like multi-object tracking, panoramic stitching, and 3D reconstruction of cluttered scenes. We will release an open-source implementation and a benchmark dataset of complex multi-structure image pairs to evaluate outlier pruning methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of multi-model fitting with data-driven, local adaptive thresholds for verification, moving beyond the single-model assumption and fixed global thresholds that dominate pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Outlier Pruning for Robust Geometric Verification via Statistical Residual Modeling
proposals.motivation: Robust geometric verification is a fundamental bottleneck in 3D reconstruction, visual localization, and image matching pipelines. Pre-2026 methods, like RANSAC and its variants, rely on iterative random sampling, which can be computationally expensive and sensitive to inlier thresholds. A more principled, model-based approach to outlier rejection after an initial alignment could improve efficiency and robustness, especially for multimodal or cluttered scenes where feature matches are ambiguous.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the residuals of putative feature correspondences, after an initial geometric transformation estimate, follow a predictable statistical distribution, and that modeling this distribution allows for the probabilistic identification and pruning of outliers more effectively than fixed-threshold heuristics.
proposals.method: We will adapt robust statistical signal detection to geometric verification. First, an initial transformation (e.g., homography or fundamental matrix) is estimated using a minimal set of matches. We then model the resulting alignment residuals of all candidate matches using a heavy-tailed distribution (e.g., a Student's t-distribution) to account for potential outliers. Correspondences are classified as inliers or outliers based on a likelihood ratio test against this model, pruning those beyond a defined confidence interval (e.g., 95%). This process can be iterated, refining the model with the newly pruned inlier set.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, probabilistic framework for outlier rejection that provides a clear statistical confidence measure for each match. It will yield a more efficient and interpretable alternative to sampling-based methods, potentially improving the speed and accuracy of geometric verification in large-scale visual datasets and embodied AI systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of statistical outlier rejection from signal processing to the geometric vision domain, moving beyond iterative sampling to a model-based, single-distribution fitting approach for post-alignment verification.

proposals.title: Geometric Verification for Robust Open-Vocabulary Visual Alignment
proposals.motivation: Recent vision-language models enable open-vocabulary alignment of image regions with text queries, but their dense embedding matches are susceptible to semantic and contextual outliers, especially in complex, cluttered scenes. This lack of geometric consistency limits the reliability of these models for real-world robotics and AR applications where precise, physically plausible object localization is required. Pruning these outlier matches is a critical unsolved step for building trustworthy, socially intelligent assistants that interact with a dynamic physical environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a robust geometric verification module, based on principles from classic multi-view geometry, into modern open-vocabulary alignment pipelines will significantly improve the precision and physical plausibility of matched regions by enforcing spatial coherence.
proposals.method: We will extend an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation model (e.g., from Torralba's prior work) by adding a post-alignment geometric verification stage. For each candidate object mask matched to a text query, we will extract keypoints and descriptors from the image region. We will then use RANSAC to fit a fundamental or homography matrix between these features and a canonical model or across multiple views, rejecting matches that are geometric outliers. This module will be designed to be lightweight and differentiable where possible, allowing it to be integrated into end-to-end training pipelines for embedding modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled method for incorporating physical scene geometry into semantically powerful but geometrically naive vision-language models. It will deliver more reliable and precise object matches for robotics and interactive AI, directly impacting the development of socially intelligent home assistants. We will release code and a new benchmark dataset focusing on geometric consistency in open-vocabulary segmentation.
proposals.novelty: While RANSAC is classic in SfM and SLAM, its application as a learned filter within state-of-the-art open-vocabulary neural networks to resolve semantic-geometric ambiguity is novel. This bridges a gap between robust geometric computer vision and modern multimodal learning, which has been largely overlooked in pre-2026 embedding-based alignment research.

proposals.title: Semantic Hash Fields: Learning Robust, Open-Vocabulary Image Retrieval via Metric Hashing and Multimodal Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Efficient large-scale image retrieval is crucial for real-world applications like robotics and visual search, but existing deep hashing methods often fail under open-vocabulary queries or distribution shifts. They rely on fixed, dataset-specific semantic labels, lacking the generalization and robustness needed for dynamic, real environments. This work addresses the brittleness of current hashing by integrating open-vocabulary semantic understanding directly into the metric learning process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by distilling knowledge from a large, pretrained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) into a deep hashing network, we can learn compact binary codes that are both semantically rich and robust to real-world variations. This will enable fast, accurate retrieval using free-form textual or visual queries not seen during hashing training.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional neural network (e.g., ResNet backbone) to output compact binary codes, guided by a triplet loss with online hard negative mining. The key innovation is a joint embedding space where the network's continuous features are aligned, via a distillation loss, with the semantic embeddings from a frozen, pretrained text-only transformer (inspired by 'Multimodal Neurons' findings) or a vision-language model like CLIP. This alignment will be enforced on large-scale, multi-object datasets (e.g., Open Images) to learn a 'semantic hash field' sensitive to object composition and attributes.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel hashing framework that bridges efficient binary retrieval with open-vocabulary semantic understanding. It will yield a robust, real-time retrieval system applicable to socially intelligent assistants and panoptic segmentation refinement. We will also release a new benchmark for evaluating hashing methods under open-vocabulary and adversarial conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly leveraging the emergent semantic structures of large pretrained models (text-only and vision-language) to guide and regularize deep metric hashing, creating a hybrid model that combines the speed of hashing with the flexible semantics of foundation models. This moves beyond simply using these models as fixed feature extractors.

proposals.title: BLAST for Vision: K-mer Hashing for Constant-Time Image Patch Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Large-scale visual search and matching, crucial for tasks like visual localization and instance retrieval, remains bottlenecked by the cost of exhaustive nearest-neighbor search in high-dimensional feature spaces. While approximate methods like FAISS exist, they often trade accuracy for speed and lack the intuitive, sequence-based indexing paradigm of other fields. This work seeks to directly import the proven, ultra-fast seed-and-extend architecture from genomic sequence alignment (e.g., BLAST) to computer vision, offering a radically different and potentially more efficient paradigm for visual search.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating dense image feature sequences as visual analogs to DNA sequences and using locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to implement k-mer hashing, we can construct a hash table index that enables constant-time retrieval of candidate image patch matches, dramatically accelerating search while maintaining high recall.
proposals.method: We will densely extract feature vectors (e.g., from a pretrained CNN like ResNet or a vision transformer) from a large image corpus, treating the spatial sequence of features as our 'visual genome'. Overlapping subsequences of features (k-mers) will be hashed using a learned or data-dependent LSH function (e.g., based on random projections or learned hyperplanes) into compact binary codes. These codes index into a hash table storing pointers to the source image and location. For a query patch, its LSH k-mers are computed and used to retrieve a shortlist of candidate matches from the table, which are then verified and extended using a more precise but expensive similarity measure (e.g., geometric verification).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, bio-inspired indexing algorithm for fast visual search with sub-linear query time complexity. We will release a scalable implementation and benchmark it against established ANN libraries on standard retrieval datasets. The core conceptual contribution is a rigorous translation of genomic alignment principles to the visual domain, potentially opening new avenues for efficient multimodal sequence matching.
proposals.novelty: While LSH is known in vision, the structured application of the k-mer hashing and seed-and-extend workflow from genomics to image patches is novel. This approach explicitly models the spatial continuity of visual features as a sequence, a perspective underexplored compared to treating features as an unordered bag, and leverages the specific efficiency of hash-based exact k-mer lookup.

proposals.title: HashMatch: Efficient Open-Vocabulary Retrieval via LSH-Indexed Multimodal Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Large-scale image retrieval and open-vocabulary segmentation models rely on nearest-neighbor search in high-dimensional embedding spaces, which is computationally expensive and limits real-time applications. As datasets like ADE20K and OpenImages grow, and models like CLIP enable rich multimodal queries, the need for sub-linear time retrieval without sacrificing accuracy becomes critical for interactive systems and embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) can be effectively integrated into a frozen, pretrained multimodal embedding pipeline to achieve order-of-magnitude speedups in retrieval for segmentation and object detection, with minimal loss in recall compared to exact k-NN search.
proposals.method: We will integrate randomized LSH (e.g., SimHash for cosine similarity) into the retrieval backend of an open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation model like MaskCLIP. Image region embeddings from a frozen vision transformer will be pre-processed into LSH buckets. At inference, text or image queries are hashed to the same space, retrieving candidate neighbors from matching buckets for fast similarity computation. We will experiment with multi-probe LSH and learnable hash functions (e.g., based on neural attention from transformers) to improve recall.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable retrieval module for open-vocabulary vision systems, enabling faster interactive segmentation and object search in large datasets. It will provide a systematic analysis of the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for LSH in multimodal embedding spaces, a crucial benchmark for real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While LSH is known, its application is novel within the specific context of real-time, open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation, and we explore the integration of neurally-guided hashing signals from pretrained transformers to improve hash function design.

proposals.title: ADaSK: Adaptive Dense Validation for Sparse Keypoint Flow in Challenging Real-World Scenes
proposals.motivation: Robust optical flow and image alignment are fundamental for robotics and scene understanding, yet sparse keypoint methods (e.g., SIFT, SuperPoint) fail in textureless or repetitive regions, while dense methods are computationally prohibitive for real-time applications. This creates a critical gap for systems like home assistants or autonomous agents that must operate reliably in diverse, cluttered indoor and outdoor environments. An adaptive solution that strategically combines efficiency and robustness is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, parallel assessment network can reliably predict regions of 'keypoint poverty' from a single image, and that selectively densifying correspondence search only in these regions with a fast local matching prior will yield more robust flow than purely sparse methods, without sacrificing overall speed.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture. The first stream performs standard sparse SIFT/SuperPoint keypoint extraction and matching for initial flow. A parallel, efficient CNN (e.g., a MobileNetV3 variant) analyzes the input image pair to output a spatial confidence map highlighting low-texture, homogeneous, or repetitively textured regions. In low-confidence cells, we activate a second-stage, localized dense matching module using fast cost-volume filtering or a lightweight learned patch descriptor (inspired by LIFT or HardNet) to establish a prior. This prior guides and validates the sparse flow, with a final fusion network integrating the two signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, context-aware optical flow framework that dynamically allocates computational resources. It will advance robust visual correspondence for embodied AI and 3D scene reconstruction by providing a practical solution for challenging but common scenes. We will release a new benchmark dataset focusing on 'keypoint-adversarial' real-world scenes (blank walls, forests, facades) to spur further research.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the adaptive, learned switching between sparse and dense correspondence regimes based on a real-time scene assessment, moving beyond static hybrid designs. This integrates scene understanding directly into the low-level correspondence task, a concept explored for segmentation but not for keypoint flow.

proposals.title: StarNet: Sparse Keypoint Alignment for Fast Visual Localization via Geometric Hashing
proposals.motivation: Real-time visual localization and object alignment remain computationally expensive for robotics and AR, especially for large-scale or high-resolution scenes. While dense feature matching and modern neural networks offer high accuracy, they are often too slow for initial bootstrapping or resource-constrained applications. A method for extremely fast, coarse alignment is needed to initialize more precise algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the sparse, robust pattern-matching principles of astronomical star catalog alignment to computer vision, we can achieve order-of-magnitude faster initial pose estimation using only a minimal set of handcrafted keypoints, without sacrificing robustness for known scenes.
proposals.method: We will pre-compute a database of distinctive SIFT keypoints and descriptors for target scenes or objects, akin to a star catalog. At runtime, we extract only the top 5-10 most salient SIFT keypoints from a query image. Inspired by astrometric algorithms, we implement a geometric hashing and voting scheme to match this sparse set against the database, solving for a rough affine or homography transform. This initial estimate will then be evaluated as a prior for refining with a more complete feature matcher.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, ultra-fast alignment module that can bootstrap existing visual localization pipelines, significantly reducing their latency. It will also provide a formal bridge between well-established astrometric techniques and computer vision, demonstrating the utility of classical geometric methods in modern pipelines.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and algorithmic translation of telescope pointing systems—specifically sparse star-pattern matching via geometric hashing—to the problem of general visual alignment, moving beyond typical feature matching heuristics.

proposals.title: SparseFlowSynth: Controllable Image Synthesis via Differentiable Keypoint Flow Inversion
proposals.motivation: Current controllable image synthesis methods, such as those using dense optical flow or text prompts, face a trade-off between precise spatial control and computational efficiency. Dense flow estimation is computationally expensive and can be ambiguous, while text-based control lacks fine-grained spatial precision. A method that uses intuitive, sparse user guidance to synthesize complex image transformations in real-time would bridge this gap, enabling applications in interactive editing, view synthesis, and data augmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: A sparse set of keypoint correspondences, extracted via classical features like SIFT, contains sufficient information to condition a neural network to solve the inverse problem of synthesizing a full, photorealistic target image from a source image, bypassing the need for dense flow estimation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage pipeline. First, we extract a sparse set of SIFT keypoints and compute their displacements between a source and target image pair to form a sparse keypoint flow field. Second, we train a conditional U-Net style network to perform 'differentiable flow inversion'. This network takes the source image and the sparse flow field (represented as a set of 2D displacement vectors anchored to keypoint locations) as input and is trained to directly reconstruct the target image. The loss will combine pixel-wise reconstruction (L1, perceptual loss) with an adversarial loss to ensure visual fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for image manipulation that is both computationally efficient and offers intuitive, sparse control. It will contribute a novel dataset of image pairs with sparse keypoint flow annotations for training and evaluation. The resulting model will enable real-time, high-fidelity image synthesis tasks like object pose manipulation and view interpolation, expanding the toolbox for visual content creation.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct use of a sparse, non-differentiable keypoint flow as a conditioning signal for end-to-end image synthesis, fusing classical computer vision features with modern generative networks. This avoids the intermediate step of densifying the flow field, which is a standard but costly practice in prior view synthesis works.

proposals.title: Sparse Keypoint Flow for Efficient and Robust Visual Scene Alignment
proposals.motivation: Dense optical flow and full-image alignment are computationally expensive, limiting real-time applications in robotics and AR. While recent work leverages large-scale datasets and language for scene understanding, a gap exists for lightweight, geometry-first methods that prioritize speed and robustness in real-world, dynamic environments. A sparse approach using only key visual features could enable faster, more efficient scene parsing and object tracking on resource-constrained platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: A sparse scene alignment model, built on a distilled set of robust keypoints and their learned flow, can achieve performance comparable to dense methods for tasks like rough object localization and scene change detection, while being significantly faster and more interpretable.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will extract and filter SIFT (or learned detector like SuperPoint) keypoints from image pairs, creating a sparse but semantically rich representation. Second, we will train a lightweight graph neural network (GNN) or transformer to predict the flow of these keypoints, incorporating geometric constraints and optionally conditioning on weak text labels from datasets like OpenImages to group keypoints into object-level entities. This sparse flow field will be used for rough alignment and change detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, efficient method for visual scene alignment that bridges classical computer vision robustness with modern learning-based efficiency. It will provide a new baseline for fast, interpretable scene understanding in robotics and interactive systems, and release a benchmark for evaluating sparse alignment performance on dynamic real-world scenes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly designing for sparsity from the ground up for alignment, moving away from dense predictions, and integrating object-level awareness from Torralba's prior work on visual semantics into a keypoint motion framework, which is less explored than end-to-end dense flow learning.

proposals.title: Web-Scale SIFT Flow: Distributed Dense Correspondence for Uncurated Image Collections
proposals.motivation: Dense correspondence algorithms like SIFT flow provide a foundational, physics-inspired understanding of scene structure and object deformation across images, crucial for tasks like 3D reconstruction and video analysis. However, their application has been limited to curated datasets due to prohibitive computational cost. Scaling this analysis to web-scale, uncurated image collections would unlock a massive, real-world corpus for studying visual common sense and object-centric relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: By developing a distributed, fault-tolerant system for SIFT flow computation, we can efficiently generate a planet-scale dense correspondence graph, revealing latent structural regularities and object affordances not capturable by current CNN or transformer-based embeddings alone.
proposals.method: We will design a cloud-native pipeline using Apache Spark or a similar framework to distribute SIFT extraction and flow matching across massive clusters. Key innovations include: (1) a locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) index for rapid candidate image pair retrieval to avoid quadratic comparisons, (2) an optimized, GPU-accelerated version of the SIFT flow algorithm for batched processing, and (3) a novel compression scheme for storing and querying the resulting flow fields. The system will be validated on a corpus of 100+ million images from publicly available web crawls.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, web-scale dataset of dense correspondences, enabling new research in unsupervised scene understanding and cross-modal retrieval. It will also provide a critical, geometry-aware complement to the semantic embeddings from large vision-language models, grounding them in low-level visual physics.
proposals.novelty: While distributed deep learning is common, applying this paradigm to a classical, optimization-based correspondence algorithm at web-scale is unexplored. The project uniquely bridges Torralba's early work on SIFT flow and massive datasets (e.g., 80 Million Tiny Images) with modern distributed systems, moving beyond static embeddings to dynamic, relational visual analysis.

proposals.title: AdvectionNet: Learning Semantic Transport Operators for Image Generation from Flow Fields
proposals.motivation: Current image generation and editing models, while powerful, often lack fine-grained, physically-plausible control over object motion and deformation. Pre-2026, methods for image warping or video prediction struggle with long-range motion and semantic coherence. This work addresses the inverse problem of synthesizing a target image from a source image and a guiding flow field, a task central to controllable generation and video interpolation.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network can be trained as a learned transport operator that generalizes the physics of advection, enabling it to warp and blend image content along arbitrary flow trajectories while preserving semantic and structural integrity better than direct pixel sampling or optical-flow-based warping.
proposals.method: We will design a convolutional network that takes a source image and a dense 2D flow field as input. Inspired by semi-Lagrangian advection schemes, the core module will be a differentiable, content-aware gathering operator that queries and intelligently composites source features from multiple points along predicted backward trajectories. The architecture will integrate modulation mechanisms from embedding-based models (inspired by Torralba's prior work) to condition the transport on semantic features extracted from the source, ensuring objects deform coherently. Training will use synthetic datasets where ground-truth target images and flow fields are known (e.g., from fluid simulations or rendered object motions).
proposals.contribution: This will introduce a new paradigm for flow-guided image synthesis, bridging numerical physics and generative vision. It will provide a robust, learnable primitive for tasks like semantic image editing, future frame prediction, and generating training data for embodied agents. The model will be a step towards more interpretable and controllable image generation systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit formulation of image generation as a learned advection process, moving beyond treating flow as a simple displacement field to a semantic transport operation. This integrates physical simulation principles with neural network design in a way not explored in pre-2026 computer vision.

proposals.title: Flow2Image: Open-Vocabulary Image Synthesis from Dense Motion Fields
proposals.motivation: Understanding and synthesizing visual dynamics is a core challenge in computer vision, critical for robotics, video prediction, and scene understanding. While optical flow estimation is a mature field, the inverse problem—generating a realistic target image from a source image and a flow field—remains under-explored, especially for complex, real-world scenes with occlusions and disocclusions. Solving this would enable powerful applications in video editing, visual planning, and data augmentation for dynamic scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a conditional generative model, trained on large-scale video datasets and guided by open-vocabulary semantic embeddings, can accurately synthesize photorealistic target images from a source image and a dense flow field, even in the presence of significant appearance changes and previously occluded content.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage encoder-decoder architecture. First, a flow-warping module produces an initial, often incomplete, estimate of the target. Second, a refinement network, based on a U-Net with cross-attention layers, inpaints disoccluded regions and corrects artifacts. This network will be conditioned on: 1) the warped image, 2) the flow field itself, and 3) open-vocabulary CLIP embeddings of the source image to provide high-level semantic guidance for content generation. We will train the model on datasets like YouTube-VIS or a large-scale egocentric video dataset, using pairs of frames and pre-computed optical flow (e.g., RAFT).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, general-purpose model for the flow-to-image synthesis task, advancing the state of dynamic scene modeling. It will provide a new tool for generating temporally consistent visual sequences and a novel framework for analyzing the information sufficiency of flow fields. The integration of open-vocabulary conditioning aligns with Torralba's recent multimodal research, bridging low-level motion and high-level semantics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior view synthesis or video prediction works that often predict flow implicitly, our method explicitly takes a flow field as a *direct, primary input* for image generation. The key novelty is the integration of this geometric constraint with open-vocabulary semantic priors from large vision-language models to guide the generation of plausible content in uncertain regions.

proposals.title: Diagnosing SIFT Flow Failures: A Phase Transition Analysis of the Correspondence Energy Landscape
proposals.motivation: SIFT flow is a foundational algorithm for dense correspondence and scene alignment, yet its failures on challenging image pairs are poorly understood. A principled, theoretical diagnosis of its limitations is needed to guide the development of more robust vision systems and understand the fundamental hardness of visual matching. Insights from statistical mechanics on the failure modes of optimization in disordered systems offer a powerful, untapped framework for this analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the failure of SIFT flow alignment is not arbitrary but correlates with a computational phase transition in its energy landscape, characterized by increased ruggedness and replica symmetry breaking, when image deformation, clutter, or noise exceed a critical threshold.
proposals.method: We will formulate the SIFT flow energy minimization as a disordered system, analogous to a spin glass. Using synthetic and real image datasets (e.g., PASCAL, SUN), we will systematically vary deformation parameters and noise to generate alignment difficulty landscapes. We will analyze the energy function's structure using tools from statistical physics, such as computing the overlap distribution between multiple optimization runs and measuring the emergence of many local minima, to detect signatures of a phase transition to a hard, glassy regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first physics-based theoretical diagnosis of SIFT flow's limitations, moving beyond empirical benchmarking. It will yield predictive indicators for when dense correspondence will fail and establish a novel connection between computational vision and the statistical mechanics of optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the formal analysis of phase transitions and replica theory—developed for spin glasses—to a core computer vision optimization problem, creating a rigorous, model-based diagnostic tool instead of an empirical or learning-based one.

proposals.title: Spectral Anchor Networks: Cross-Modal Image Alignment via Spectral-Spatial Fingerprinting
proposals.motivation: Aligning images from radically different sensors (e.g., MRI, SAR, thermal) is critical for multi-modal scene understanding in robotics and medical imaging. Current methods rely on cross-modal feature adaptation or shared backbone training, which struggle with extreme appearance gaps and require large, paired datasets. A more fundamental, physics-inspired approach is needed to find modality-invariant correspondences without direct feature translation.
proposals.hypothesis: A shared latent alignment space for multi-modal images can be constructed by identifying and matching intrinsic 'spectral-spatial fingerprints'—patterns of energy across spatial frequencies that are preserved for the same physical scene or object across different imaging modalities.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage Spectral Anchor Network (SAN). First, a learnable spectral decomposition module, inspired by steerable filter banks and scattering transforms, extracts multi-scale, orientation-selective spectral energy maps from each modality. Second, a lightweight cross-modal transformer correlates these maps to identify consistent spectral-spatial anchor points, generating a sparse set of correspondence fields. These anchors will guide a differentiable thin-plate spline warping module for dense alignment. Training will leverage existing multi-modal datasets (e.g., FLIR-ADAS for visible/thermal, SEN1-2 for SAR/optical) with a contrastive loss that pulls together fingerprint embeddings of aligned scene pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, dataset-efficient paradigm for cross-modal alignment based on spectral signatures rather than learned appearance features. It will provide a robust alignment framework for sensor-fusion applications where paired data is scarce. The method will be validated by improving downstream tasks like panoptic segmentation in multi-modal urban scenes and lesion tracking in multi-sequence medical images.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct analogy to multi-messenger astrophysics, replacing unstable cross-modal feature adaptation with the correlation of intrinsic, modality-agnostic spectral signatures. This shifts the alignment problem from the pixel/feature domain to a more fundamental spectral-spatial domain.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Feature Alignment with Embedding Modulation for Zero-Shot Image Registration
proposals.motivation: Robustly aligning images from different modalities (e.g., RGB, infrared, SAR, medical scans) is a fundamental challenge for scene understanding and robotic perception, but traditional feature descriptors fail under large appearance shifts. Pre-2026 vision-language models offer powerful semantic embeddings, yet their direct application to dense, pixel-level geometric alignment across modalities remains underexplored. This work addresses the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level geometric matching.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a shared, modulation-based embedding space, trained with contrastive and reconstruction losses, can learn modality-invariant local features that enable accurate, zero-shot dense correspondence between semantically similar but visually disparate image pairs.
proposals.method: We will extend the Embedding Modulation framework from open-vocabulary segmentation to dense feature learning. A shared ViT backbone will extract features from image pairs, which are then projected into a common space via lightweight, modality-specific adapter networks. We will train the system using a novel multi-task objective on datasets like MS-COCO and Hypersim, combining a contrastive loss on DINOv2 features for local consistency, a cycle-consistency loss for geometric validity, and a masked autoencoding objective for robustness. Inference will use these modulated features for nearest-neighbor matching and RANSAC-based geometric estimation.
proposals.contribution: This will contribute a novel, training-efficient framework for zero-shot cross-modal image alignment that does not require paired multi-modal data for new modalities. We will release a benchmark for evaluating cross-modal registration and demonstrate applications in augmented reality view synthesis and autonomous driving sensor fusion.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting embedding modulation—previously used for semantic segmentation—to the geometric task of dense feature matching, and in the multi-task training strategy that bridges self-supervised visual features with the geometric constraints necessary for alignment, moving beyond purely semantic similarity.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Scene Priors: Learning Robust Object and Layout Representations from Sketches and Paintings
proposals.motivation: Current computer vision models for scene understanding are heavily reliant on large-scale photographic datasets, which encode biases in lighting, texture, and style. This limits their robustness and ability to generalize to non-photorealistic visual domains like artistic media or simplified synthetic environments. Exploring training on sketches and paintings forces models to focus on the essential structural and semantic priors of a scene, moving beyond superficial photographic cues.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that models trained primarily on non-photographic visual data (e.g., sketches, paintings, synthetic renderings) can learn more robust and disentangled representations of object shape, spatial layout, and semantic context, leading to improved generalization in open-vocabulary scene understanding tasks compared to models trained solely on photographs.
proposals.method: We will leverage a multi-stage training pipeline. First, we will curate and align large-scale datasets of sketches (e.g., QuickDraw) and paintings (e.g., WikiArt) with existing photographic scene datasets (e.g., ADE20K, OpenImages) using textual descriptions for weak supervision. We will employ a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP) as a teacher to align embeddings across modalities. The core architecture will be a transformer-based model, extending Torralba's work on embedding modulation for panoptic segmentation, to fuse features from the non-photographic stream with a lightweight photographic stream in a late-fusion manner, enforcing consistency in the latent object and scene embedding space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for training scene understanding models that are invariant to stylistic domain shifts, 2) new insights into the visual priors necessary for scene comprehension, and 3) a benchmark and model demonstrating superior performance on open-vocabulary segmentation and scene classification for artistic, synthetic, and under-represented visual domains.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is well-studied, the proposal is novel in using non-photographic data as the *primary* training source for learning fundamental scene priors, rather than as a secondary adaptation target, and in explicitly connecting this to open-vocabulary reasoning via modulated embeddings.

proposals.title: SceneSense: Open-Vocabulary Scene Recognition from Non-Visual Sensory Streams
proposals.motivation: Scene understanding is fundamental to AI perception but remains vision-centric. For robust home assistants, autonomous systems, and accessibility technologies, agents must interpret environments when vision is occluded, unreliable, or unavailable (e.g., in darkness or for visually impaired users). Pre-2026 work in audio scene classification and multimodal learning provides a foundation, but lacks a unified, open-vocabulary framework for general scene recognition from non-visual modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: That semantically rich, open-vocabulary scene representations can be learned directly from non-visual sensory data (audio, tactile) by aligning their embeddings with a pre-trained visual-language semantic space, enabling scene classification without visual imagery.
proposals.method: We will leverage the embedding modulation technique from open-vocabulary segmentation to align non-visual features with CLIP's semantic space. Specifically, we will process audio spectrograms (using a ConvNet backbone) and tactile sensor readings (using a Transformer encoder) to produce modality-specific embeddings. A lightweight modulation network will adapt these embeddings to align with CLIP's text embeddings for scene categories, enabling zero-shot and open-vocabulary inference. Training will use datasets like AudioSet and newly collected tactile-scene data, with scene labels from visual datasets (e.g., Places) providing semantic grounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel benchmark for non-visual scene recognition and deliver a unified model capable of open-vocabulary scene classification from audio and touch. It will extend the principles of visual object recognition to other sensory domains, contributing directly to the development of more robust, perceptive, and socially intelligent home assistants and robots.
proposals.novelty: While audio event detection exists, open-vocabulary *scene* recognition from non-visual data is unexplored. The key novelty is transferring the semantic structure of visual scenes to non-visual modalities via neural embedding alignment, creating a 'scene semantics' channel independent of vision.

proposals.title: Places-10M: Scaling and Diversifying Visual Scene Understanding with a Massive, Multi-Label, and Multimodal Dataset
proposals.motivation: The Places Database has been foundational for scene understanding, but its scale and label diversity are outpaced by the demands of modern vision-language models and open-vocabulary tasks. Current models require orders of magnitude more data, and existing scene datasets lack fine-grained, multi-label annotations that reflect the compositional and functional complexity of real-world environments. This gap limits progress in holistic scene interpretation for embodied AI and assistive agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling the Places Database by an order of magnitude and enriching it with diverse, multi-label scene attributes (e.g., materials, affordances, activities, sounds) will significantly improve the robustness, compositional reasoning, and open-vocabulary generalization of scene understanding models.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale web image-text pairs and recent vision-language models (e.g., CLIP, BLIP-2) for automated data collection and candidate label generation. A neurally-guided, human-in-the-loop curation system, inspired by NOPA, will refine and validate labels for scale and accuracy. We will employ embedding modulation techniques from open-vocabulary segmentation to structure the label space, linking visual concepts to a dense, hierarchical taxonomy of scene attributes. The final dataset will include image-level multi-labels and a subset with pixel-level segmentations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver Places-10M, a publicly available benchmark dataset an order of magnitude larger than its predecessor, with a novel multi-label structure. It will enable training of more robust and compositionally-aware scene recognition models and serve as a critical testbed for evaluating open-vocabulary and multimodal reasoning in complex, real-world contexts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of modern vision-language models and interactive human curation to scale a canonical dataset, moving beyond single-category scene labels to a rich, multi-attribute representation that explicitly models the functional and physical properties of environments.

proposals.title: SceneTopics: Discovering Latent Thematic Structure in Visual Scenes via Neural Topic Models
proposals.motivation: Current scene understanding models excel at recognizing objects and their spatial layouts but lack a higher-level, interpretable representation of scene semantics akin to 'themes' or 'topics.' This limits their ability to reason about scene function, cultural context, or abstract affordances. Drawing inspiration from NLP, where topic models like LDA reveal latent thematic structure in documents, we propose to model visual scenes as distributions over latent, reusable thematic components, bridging a gap between low-level perception and high-level semantic reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that visual scenes can be decomposed into a mixture of latent, interpretable 'visual topics'—recurring combinations of objects, materials, and spatial relations—and that modeling this structure will improve performance on tasks requiring holistic scene understanding, such as scene classification, generation, and atypicality detection.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural topic model for images, building on the VQ-VAE architecture to discretize visual features into a 'visual vocabulary.' A transformer-based encoder will process these discrete tokens to produce a scene-level representation, which is then used to infer a mixture over latent topics via a learnable topic matrix. We will train the model on large-scale scene datasets (e.g., ADE20K, Places) using a combined objective of codebook learning, topic distribution sparsity (via a Dirichlet prior), and reconstruction. Inference will yield per-scene topic distributions and per-topic distributions over the visual vocabulary, which can be visualized.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel probabilistic generative model for scenes that provides interpretable, topic-like decompositions, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating the semantic coherence of discovered visual topics, and 3) demonstrated utility of the topic representations as priors for downstream tasks like open-vocabulary scene parsing and contextual reasoning for embodied AI agents.
proposals.novelty: While topic models are well-established in NLP and have been applied to bag-of-words image features, applying modern neural topic modeling directly to the rich, structured visual token stream of contemporary scene representations is novel. This approach moves beyond object co-occurrence to model latent thematic structure emergent from object interactions, materials, and geometry.

proposals.title: VOCAL: Video Object Consistency for Autonomously Learning Scene Representations
proposals.motivation: Current vision models rely heavily on large-scale, manually annotated datasets, which are costly and static. Self-supervised learning from unlabeled video offers a scalable path to learning robust, physically-grounded representations of objects and scenes. This is crucial for building home assistants and robots that must understand dynamic, real-world environments without exhaustive supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enforcing temporal consistency of object and region embeddings across video frames, without explicit labels, will yield visual features that are more semantically structured and better for compositional scene understanding than features from static image SSL.
proposals.method: We will extend a transformer-based architecture (e.g., DINO or MAE) to process video clips. The core objective is a contrastive loss that pulls together embeddings of the same object instance across time, identified via simple optical flow or unsupervised segmentation (e.g., Slot Attention). We will integrate an embedding modulation layer, inspired by Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation work, to allow later language-conditioned querying of the learned object-centric features. Training will use large-scale egocentric video datasets like Ego4D.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a self-supervised, object-aware video pre-training framework that reduces dependency on labeled data for scene parsing. It will produce a model that outputs temporally stable, queryable object embeddings, directly useful for downstream tasks in embodied AI and social home assistants. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating the physical consistency of learned scene representations.
proposals.novelty: While SSL from video exists, our approach uniquely focuses on learning *object-level* consistency by combining temporal cues with recent advances in unsupervised object discovery and embedding modulation, creating a direct bridge to open-vocabulary reasoning without manual annotation.

proposals.title: GistFormer: A Multimodal Transformer for Embedding Cognitive Models of Rapid Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Human scene understanding is remarkably fast, extracting the 'gist' (e.g., 'a kitchen', 'a busy street') within a fraction of a second, a process studied extensively in cognitive psychology. Current computer vision models, while powerful, are not explicitly designed to mimic this rapid, holistic, and often multimodal (visual, auditory) perceptual mechanism. Bridging this gap is crucial for building more efficient, robust, and human-like perception systems for robotics and assistive agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that explicitly integrates computational approximations of cognitive gist mechanisms—such as global feature statistics, spatial envelope properties, and cross-modal priming—will achieve more human-like scene categorization speed and robustness compared to standard end-to-end trained vision models.
proposals.method: We will implement a dual-stream transformer architecture. One stream processes low-resolution image patches to compute global spectral and spatial envelope features, inspired by pre-2026 cognitive models (e.g., Oliva & Torralba, 2001). The second stream processes high-resolution object-centric features from a pretrained open-vocabulary detector (e.g., OV-Panoptic Segmentation). A cross-attention 'gist modulation' layer, informed by the global stream, will guide the object stream's attention, simulating top-down gist priming. We will extend this to audio-visual scenes using synchronized sound embeddings (from CLAP or similar pre-2026 models) as an additional priming signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, cognitively-inspired neural model for scene understanding and release a benchmark dataset for evaluating rapid scene categorization in machines against human behavioral data. It will provide new insights into how inductive biases from human perception can be integrated into modern multimodal architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used deep nets to predict gist, this proposal uniquely integrates explicit, interpretable cognitive model components as architectural priors within a state-of-the-art multimodal transformer, creating a direct bridge between classical psychology and modern embodied AI.

proposals.title: Places-Mini: A Computationally Efficient, Diversity-Preserving Subset for Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Large-scale scene datasets like Places365 are foundational for visual recognition but impose significant computational and environmental costs, hindering rapid prototyping and equitable research access. A principled method to create a small, representative subset would democratize scene understanding research and accelerate experimentation with new multimodal architectures. This aligns with the growing need for efficient, impactful datasets that retain real-world visual and semantic diversity.
proposals.hypothesis: A miniaturized Places subset, curated by maximizing scene category and visual attribute diversity while minimizing dataset size, can achieve performance parity with the full dataset on key scene understanding tasks when used for model pre-training or fine-tuning. The embedding spaces of models trained on this subset will preserve the semantic and structural relationships found in models trained on the full dataset.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) to compute dense, multimodal embeddings for each Places365 image. Using these embeddings, we will apply a graph-based or k-center clustering algorithm, constrained by category labels, to select a maximally diverse and representative core set of images. The selection process will be guided by an objective function that balances intra-class visual variance and inter-class separation. We will validate the subset by benchmarking the performance of standard architectures (e.g., ResNet, Vision Transformers) pre-trained or fine-tuned on Places-Mini against their full-dataset counterparts on downstream tasks like scene classification and open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly released, rigorously curated mini-dataset that reduces the computational barrier to scene understanding research. It will establish a methodology for dataset distillation that prioritizes semantic and visual diversity over mere size. The resulting resource will enable faster iteration on new models, particularly in multimodal and embodied AI contexts where scene priors are crucial.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior dataset distillation work focused on class-wise compression or synthetic image generation, our approach explicitly optimizes for the preservation of holistic scene diversity and multimodal semantic structure, creating a usable subset of real images. This bridges Torralba's expertise in visual datasets and scene physics with recent methods in language-guided representation learning.

proposals.title: Object-to-Scene: Probabilistic Inference of Holistic Scene Attributes from a Single Object
proposals.motivation: Scene understanding is a core challenge in computer vision, but current models typically require a holistic view of the environment. In real-world applications like robotics or assistive technology, an agent's initial view may be occluded or focused on a single item. The ability to probabilistically infer likely scene context (e.g., room type, other objects, layout) from a single detected object would enable more robust reasoning and anticipation with limited perceptual input, bridging a gap between object-centric and scene-centric understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the visual and semantic embedding of a single object contains sufficient information to generate a probabilistic distribution over plausible holistic scene attributes, and that this distribution can be effectively modeled by leveraging large-scale, multi-modal pre-trained models and structured scene-object co-occurrence statistics.
proposals.method: We will build a two-stage model. First, we extract a rich object representation using a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) and an open-vocabulary object detector. Second, this representation is fed into a conditional generative model—specifically, a transformer-based decoder—trained to predict a distribution over scene attributes. These attributes will be defined using structured labels from datasets like ADE20K and Visual Genome, encompassing scene categories, material properties, likely co-occurring objects, and spatial layout priors. The model will be trained using a maximum likelihood objective, regularized by the empirical object-scene co-occurrence statistics mined from these datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel benchmark task and model for probabilistic scene-from-object inference. It will provide a new tool for robotic anticipation and context-aware human-agent interaction, as seen in Torralba's prior work on social home assistants. The resulting model and analysis will also offer insights into the semantic and contextual knowledge embedded within modern vision-language foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied object-scene relationships (e.g., Torralba's early work on context), the novel formulation here is the explicit, probabilistic generation of a full set of scene attributes from a *single* object instance, moving beyond classification to structured prediction by leveraging recent advances in open-vocabulary models and multimodal transformers.

proposals.title: WebScene: Weakly-Supervised Panoptic Understanding from Uncurated Web Imagery
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art scene understanding models are bottlenecked by the scale and manual annotation cost of datasets like COCO and ADE20K. The web contains orders of magnitude more visual data depicting real-world object interactions and scene compositions, but it is unstructured and lacks pixel-level labels. Leveraging this resource is crucial for developing models with robust, real-world visual common sense.
proposals.hypothesis: By distilling knowledge from web-scale, unlabeled image collections using weak supervision from pre-trained multimodal (vision-language) models and large language models, we can train a unified scene understanding model that significantly outperforms models trained on curated datasets alone in open-vocabulary panoptic segmentation.
proposals.method: We will construct a pipeline to harvest billions of web images with associated alt-text or surrounding captions. Weak supervision will be generated by: 1) using a large vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) to propose candidate object regions aligned with text nouns, and 2) employing a large language model (inspired by 'Multimodal Neurons' work) to reason about physical and spatial relationships between these candidates, generating pseudo-labels for 'stuff' and 'thing' categories. A student model, based on an architecture like Mask2Former with an embedding modulation head (extending 'Open-vocabulary Panoptic Segmentation'), will be trained on this noisy data using a robust loss function designed to handle label uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new paradigm for scaling scene understanding datasets without manual annotation, a massively scaled benchmark derived from web data, and a state-of-the-art model demonstrating superior generalization to novel objects and complex real-world scenes. It directly advances the goal of building socially intelligent agents that perceive environments as humans do.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses web data for image classification, our approach uniquely targets dense, panoptic scene parsing by synergistically combining weak signals from vision-language and language-only models to infer not just objects, but their compositional scene structure and material properties ('stuff').

proposals.title: Open-World Scene Anomaly Detection via Multimodal Out-of-Distribution Scoring
proposals.motivation: Current computer vision systems excel at recognizing predefined categories but fail in open-world settings where novel objects or scene configurations appear. This is a critical limitation for real-world applications like autonomous systems and home assistants, which must recognize when a situation is unfamiliar to safely seek assistance. Grounding anomaly detection in the physics and semantics of real-world scenes, rather than low-level pixel statistics, is an unsolved challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained on large-scale, multimodal datasets (image, text, sound) will learn a joint embedding space where the distance from a test sample's embedding to the nearest known scene prototype provides a robust, semantically-grounded measure of scene anomaly.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pretrained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) as a feature extractor and calibrate it using large-scale scene datasets (e.g., ADE20K, Places). The core method involves constructing a probabilistic scene-graph representation from known categories and using a density estimation technique (e.g., Normalizing Flows or a Mahalanobis distance-based scorer) in the multimodal embedding space to compute an anomaly score. We will extend this by modulating embeddings based on object co-occurrence statistics to better capture physical plausibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel benchmark and evaluation protocol for semantically-meaningful scene anomaly detection. It will also provide a new method that moves beyond low-level reconstruction error, offering a model that can explain *why* a scene is anomalous (e.g., 'unexpected object in context') by leveraging multimodal embeddings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing scene anomaly detection as an open-vocabulary, out-of-distribution detection problem in a multimodal embedding space, explicitly incorporating physical and contextual constraints learned from large datasets, rather than relying on generative pixel-space reconstruction.
